topics.title: Experimentation on the size reduction of food material using milling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTL408 - FOOD PROCESS ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Experimentation on the size reduction of food material using milling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Size Reduction?

**Size Reduction** is a fundamental unit operation in food process engineering wherein solid food materials are mechanically broken down into smaller particles through the application of **compression, impact, attrition (rubbing), and shearing (cutting) forces**. The resulting smaller particles offer increased surface area, improved mass transfer, enhanced heat transfer, and better downstream processability (mixing, extraction, drying, packaging).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PCFTL408), size reduction is treated as a *mechanical pre-processing step* whose efficiency is quantified using the **reduction ratio**, **energy consumption**, and **particle size distribution (PSD)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Terminology):**
> *Size reduction is the mechanical process of disintegrating a solid food material into smaller fragments by applying external forces that exceed the material's cohesive strength, thereby altering its mean particle diameter, surface area, and bulk density.*

## 1.2 What is Milling?

**Milling** is a subset of size reduction that uses rotating or vibrating mechanical devices (mills) to achieve particle size reduction. In the food industry, milling is performed using:

| Mill Type | Primary Force | Typical Food Applications |
|-----------|---------------|---------------------------|
| Hammer Mill | Impact | Grains, spices, animal feed |
| Ball Mill | Impact + Attrition | Sugar, cocoa, salt, pigments |
| Roller Mill | Compression + Shearing | Flour, cereals, oilseeds |
| Pin Mill | Impact + Shearing | Starch, dried herbs, chemicals |
| Burr Mill | Attrition + Shearing | Coffee, spices |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Grinding Masala" Intuition:**
> Imagine pounding roasted cumin seeds in a traditional stone mortar (*ammikallu*). The pestle delivers a sudden **impact** to crack the seed coat, while the circular rubbing motion causes **attrition** that wears the seed into coarse particles, and the edges of the mortar create **shearing** that slices the brittle material. A hammer mill does *exactly* this — but thousands of times per second, using swinging metal hammers. The energy you expend in pounding is what engineers call **specific energy consumption (kWh/tonne)**. The finer you want the masala, the harder (and longer) you must pound — this trade-off is governed by the **laws of size reduction** (Rittinger, Kick, Bond).

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Standard Sieve Series:** Tyler, ASTM E11, or ISO 565 (mesh numbers 4 → 400)
- **Densities (typical):** Wheat ≈ **0.75 g/cm³**, Corn ≈ **0.72 g/cm³**, Soybean ≈ **0.74 g/cm³**
- **Hardgrove Grindability Index (HGI):** Indicator of grindability for coal & cereals; higher = easier to grind
- **Bond's Work Index (Wᵢ):** Energy required to reduce unit mass from infinite size to 100 µm (typical for foods: **Wᵢ = 8–14 kWh/tonne**)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Size Reduction? The "Why" Behind the Operation

- **Increased Surface Area** → accelerates drying, extraction, and reaction rates.
- **Improved Mixing Uniformity** → in doughs, batter, and feed formulations.
- **Better Digestibility/Palatability** → smaller particles = better nutrient absorption in animal feeds.
- **Classification/Sorting** → enables separation by sieving or air classification.
- **Packing & Storage** → reduces voids, improves bulk density stability.

## 2.2 Forces Involved in Milling (The "How")

Every mill reduces size by one or more of these **four fundamental forces**:

1. **Compression** — Slow, steady squeezing (e.g., roller mill crushing wheat).
2. **Impact** — Sudden collision of a high-velocity object (e.g., hammer mill rotor).
3. **Attrition** — Rubbing/shearing of particles against each other (e.g., ball mill).
4. **Cutting** — Shearing with sharp blades (e.g., knife mill for leafy materials).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Concept — Mode of Failure vs. Material Property:**
> - **Brittle materials** (grains, sugar, salt) → fail by **impact & compression**.
> - **Fibrous / tough materials** (meat, dried herbs) → fail by **cutting & shearing**.
> - **Hard, abrasive materials** (spices, minerals) → fail by **attrition**.

## 2.3 The Three Classical Laws of Size Reduction

These laws relate the **energy (E)** required for size reduction to the **change in particle size**.

### 2.3.1 Kick's Law (1885)
> *Energy is proportional to the reduction ratio.*

$$E = K_K \cdot \ln\left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right)$$

**When to apply:** Coarse crushing (large particle → medium particle). Assumes energy per unit mass is independent of feed size.

### 2.3.2 Rittinger's Law (1867)
> *Energy is proportional to the new surface area created.*

$$E = K_R \cdot \left(\frac{1}{D_2} - \frac{1}{D_1}\right)$$

**When to apply:** Fine grinding (where new surface area dominates). Best suited for brittle materials crushed below 1 mm.

### 2.3.3 Bond's Law (1952)
> *Energy is proportional to the square root of the reduction ratio.*

$$E = K_B \cdot \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{D_2}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{D_1}}\right)$$

**When to apply:** Intermediate (most common in real food milling). Forms the basis of the **Bond Work Index** approach.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These laws are the foundation of **mill scale-up design**. Food process engineers use them to compute the motor power, throughput, and product fineness for industrial hammer mills and roller mills.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Application |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | $R = \dfrac{D_1}{D_2}$ | Reduction Ratio | dimensionless | Coarse crushing |
| 2 | $E = K_K \ln\!\left(\dfrac{D_1}{D_2}\right)$ | Kick's Law | kWh/tonne | Coarse size reduction |
| 3 | $E = K_R\!\left(\dfrac{1}{D_2} - \dfrac{1}{D_1}\right)$ | Rittinger's Law | kWh/tonne | Fine grinding |
| 4 | $E = K_B\!\left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_2}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_1}}\right)$ | Bond's Law | kWh/tonne | Intermediate grinding |
| 5 | $W_i = \dfrac{1.102 \cdot 10^{-5}}{(\,80 - P_1\,)\sqrt{P_1}}$ | Bond's Work Index (closed-circuit ball mill) | kWh/tonne | Mill sizing |
| 6 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{\text{useful}}}{P_{\text{input}}} \times 100$ | Milling efficiency | % | Energy audit |
| 7 | $d_{sg} = \dfrac{1}{\sum \dfrac{x_i}{\bar{d}_i}}$ | Sauter mean diameter (surface-weighted) | µm | PSD analysis |
| 8 | $P = m \cdot g \cdot t$ | Theoretical energy from mass drop | kWh | Empirical check |
| 9 | $N_m = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{R}}$ | Critical speed of ball mill (rpm) | rpm | Operational safety |
| 10 | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{m_f - m_r}{t}$ | Throughput | kg/s | Capacity planning |

> **Boundary condition check:** All diameters in the same units (preferably mm or µm). All $D_2 < D_1$ (output < input size).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Kick's Law

**Statement:** The energy required for size reduction is proportional to the *logarithmic* ratio of feed to product size.

**Starting postulate:** The differential energy $dE$ required to infinitesimally reduce a particle of size $D$ is proportional to the *relative* change in size:

$$dE = -K_K \cdot \frac{dD}{D}$$

**Step 1 — Integrate** between initial size $D_1$ and final size $D_2$:

$$\int_{0}^{E} dE = -K_K \int_{D_1}^{D_2} \frac{dD}{D}$$

**Step 2 — Perform the integration:**

$$E = -K_K \cdot \Big[\ln D\Big]_{D_1}^{D_2}$$

$$E = -K_K \cdot (\ln D_2 - \ln D_1)$$

$$E = K_K \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right) \quad \text{...(Kick's Law)}$$

**Conversion logic:** The negative sign in $-dD/D$ arises because as size *decreases* (negative $dD$), the *energy input* $dE$ is positive. The integration flips the limits, yielding the familiar logarithmic form.

## 3.2 Derivation of Rittinger's Law

**Postulate:** Energy is proportional to the **newly created surface area**.

For a cube of side $D$, the volume is $D^3$ and the surface area is $6D^2$. The *specific surface area* (per unit volume) is:

$$S_v = \frac{6D^2}{D^3} = \frac{6}{D}$$

**Step 1 — New surface generated per unit mass (assuming density $\rho$):**

$$\Delta S = \frac{6}{\rho}\!\left(\frac{1}{D_2} - \frac{1}{D_1}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Equate energy to new surface:**

$$E = K_R \cdot \Delta S = K_R \cdot \frac{6}{\rho}\!\left(\frac{1}{D_2} - \frac{1}{D_1}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Absorb constants into $K_R$:**

$$E = K_R \cdot \left(\frac{1}{D_2} - \frac{1}{D_1}\right) \quad \text{...(Rittinger's Law)}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of Bond's Law

**Postulate:** Energy is proportional to the **square root of the crack length** (assumed proportional to particle diameter). This is an empirical midpoint between Kick and Rittinger.

**Step 1 — Differential energy assumption:**

$$dE = -K_B \cdot \frac{dD}{2\sqrt{D} \cdot D}$$

**Step 2 — Integrate:**

$$E = -K_B \int_{D_1}^{D_2} \frac{dD}{2 D^{3/2}}$$

$$E = -K_B \cdot \Big[-D^{-1/2}\Big]_{D_1}^{D_2}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify:**

$$E = K_B \cdot \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{D_2}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{D_1}}\right) \quad \text{...(Bond's Law)}$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** 500 kg of wheat is fed to a hammer mill. The mean feed diameter is $D_1 = 2000\ \mu m$ and the mean product diameter is $D_2 = 250\ \mu m$. The mill consumed **12.5 kWh** of energy. Calculate the **Rittinger's constant $K_R$** and verify **Kick's constant $K_K$** assuming the mill follows Kick's law with $K_K = 6.0$ kWh/tonne.

**Solution (Rittinger's constant):**

Step 1 — Convert mass to tonnes: $m = 0.5$ tonne.

Step 2 — Specific energy:

$$E = \frac{12.5\ \text{kWh}}{0.5\ \text{tonne}} = 25\ \text{kWh/tonne}$$

Step 3 — Apply Rittinger's Law:

$$E = K_R \left(\frac{1}{D_2} - \frac{1}{D_1}\right)$$

With diameters in $\mu m$:

$$\frac{1}{D_2} - \frac{1}{D_1} = \frac{1}{250} - \frac{1}{2000} = 0.00400 - 0.00050 = 0.00350\ \mu m^{-1}$$

Step 4 — Solve for $K_R$:

$$K_R = \frac{E}{0.00350} = \frac{25}{0.00350} = 7142.86\ \text{kWh·µm/tonne}$$

**Verification (Kick's Law):**

$$E_{\text{Kick}} = K_K \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right) = 6.0 \cdot \ln(8) = 6.0 \times 2.0794 = 12.48\ \text{kWh/tonne}$$

Observed: **25 kWh/tonne** >> Kick predicts **12.48 kWh/tonne** → Conclusion: **Fine grinding (high surface creation) → Rittinger's Law is the better fit.** ✓

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Law

| Particle Size Range | Reduction Type | Best-fit Law | Typical Equipment |
|---------------------|----------------|--------------|-------------------|
| $> 50$ mm → $5$–$50$ mm | Primary crushing | **Kick's** | Jaw crusher, Gyratory crusher |
| $5$–$50$ mm → $0.5$–$5$ mm | Secondary crushing | **Bond's** | Cone crusher, Roller mill |
| $0.5$–$5$ mm → $< 0.5$ mm | Fine grinding | **Rittinger's** | Hammer mill, Ball mill, Pin mill |
| $< 0.1$ mm → $< 10\ \mu m$ | Ultra-fine | **Stirred media mill** | Bead mill, Jet mill |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Experimental Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Sample Selection] --> B[Weigh Initial Mass m_f = 500 g]
    B --> C[Record Initial Mean Diameter D1 via Sieve Analysis]
    C --> D[Load Sample into Hammer Mill Hopper]
    D --> E[Close Mill Door; Set Screen Size 2 mm]
    E --> F[Start Mill; Record Voltmeter V and Ammeter I]
    F --> G[Note Milling Time t = 60 s]
    G --> H[Collect Milled Product; Weigh m_p]
    H --> I[Weigh Retained Material m_r in Mill]
    I --> J[Perform Sieve Analysis on Product]
    J --> K[Compute Mean Product Diameter D2]
    K --> L[Calculate Energy E = V I cos phi t]
    L --> M[Calculate K_K Kick Constant]
    M --> N[Calculate K_R Rittinger Constant]
    N --> O[Plot PSD Curve Mass vs Sieve Size]
    O --> P[Report Reduction Ratio R and Efficiency]
    P --> Q[End of Experiment]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Hammer Mill

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FeedSystem[Feed System]
        A1[Hopper] --> A2[Feed Chute]
        A2 --> A3[Rotary Feeder]
    end

    subgraph MillingChamber[Milling Chamber]
        B1[Rotor Shaft] --> B2[Hammers Swing Type]
        B2 --> B3[Breaker Plates]
        B3 --> B4[Perforated Screen 2 mm]
    end

    subgraph DriveUnit[Drive Unit]
        C1[Electric Motor 1 to 3 kW] --> C2[V Belt Drive]
        C2 --> C3[Pulley and Bearing Assembly]
        C3 --> B1
    end

    subgraph DischargeSystem[Discharge System]
        B4 --> D1[Collection Tray]
        D1 --> D2[Cyclone Separator Optional]
        D2 --> D3[Bag Filter]
    end

    subgraph ControlPanel[Control Panel]
        E1[Voltmeter] --> E2[Ammeter]
        E2 --> E3[Timer]
        E3 --> E4[Emergency Stop]
    end

    A3 --> B2
    C1 --> E1
    D1 --> P[Final Milled Product]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Process Flow — Sieve Analysis (PSD Determination)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Oven Dried Sample 100 g] --> S1[Stack of Sieves: 1000, 500, 250, 125, 75 um]
    S1 --> S2[Place in Ro Tap Shaker for 10 min]
    S2 --> S3[Weigh Retained Mass on Each Sieve]
    S3 --> S4[Compute Mass Fraction x_i]
    S4 --> S5[Compute Cumulative Undersize Percentage]
    S5 --> S6[Plot Frequency vs Log Sieve Size]
    S6 --> S7[Determine D10, D50, D90 from Graph]
    S7 --> S8[Report Mean Particle Diameter]
```

## 4.4 Annotated Diagram Description (Block Schematic of Ball Mill)

> [!NOTE]
> *Ball Mill Schematic (textual block representation):*
> A horizontal cylindrical shell (typically $\tfrac{1}{3}$ filled with steel or ceramic balls) rotates on trunnion bearings. The grinding media **cascade** as the mill rotates, producing both **impact** (when balls fall from the cascading top) and **attrition** (when balls roll at the toe). A peripheral discharge grate lets ground particles exit. The **critical speed** $N_c$ is the speed at which centrifuging occurs (no grinding):

$$N_c = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{g}{R}} \quad \text{(in rad/s; convert to rpm by } \times \tfrac{60}{2\pi}\text{)}$$

> **Operational rule:** Industrial ball mills operate at **65–80% of $N_c$**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define size reduction and list any three industrial applications in food processing.**

**Model Answer:**
Size reduction is the mechanical operation of breaking solid food material into smaller pieces using impact, compression, attrition, or shearing forces. Industrial applications:
1. **Flour milling** from wheat using roller mills.
2. **Spice grinding** (turmeric, pepper) using hammer mills.
3. **Cocoa nib grinding** in chocolate manufacture using ball mills.
4. **Animal feed pelletizing** — size reduction precedes pelleting.

*(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 1 mark mode + 1 mark for 3 applications)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**State Rittinger's Law of size reduction and mention its range of applicability.**

**Model Answer:**
Rittinger's Law (1867) states that the energy required for size reduction is directly proportional to the *newly created surface area* of the particles.

$$E = K_R \left(\frac{1}{D_2} - \frac{1}{D_1}\right)$$

**Applicability:** Best suited for **fine grinding** of **brittle materials** (sugar, salt, dry grains) where the product is below **1 mm**. It is **not** accurate for coarse crushing.

*(3 marks: 1 mark statement + 1 mark formula + 1 mark applicability)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**
> **(a)** Explain the **three classical laws of size reduction** (Kick, Rittinger, Bond) with mathematical expressions. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A hammer mill is used to grind **1000 kg of maize** from an initial mean particle diameter of **3 mm** to a final diameter of **500 µm**. The mill consumes **18 kWh** of electrical energy. **(7 marks)**
>   - (i) Calculate the **reduction ratio**.
>   - (ii) Determine the **specific energy consumption** in kWh/tonne.
>   - (iii) Verify which law (Kick, Rittinger, or Bond) best fits the data, given the constants: $K_K = 7.5$ kWh/tonne, $K_R = 5000$ kWh·µm/tonne, $K_B = 200$ kWh·$\sqrt{\mu m}$/tonne.

#### **Model Solution (a) — Laws of Size Reduction (7 marks)**

| Law | Formula | Assumption | Range |
|-----|---------|------------|-------|
| **Kick's Law** | $E = K_K \ln\!\left(\dfrac{D_1}{D_2}\right)$ | Energy ∝ ratio of sizes | Coarse crushing |
| **Rittinger's Law** | $E = K_R\!\left(\dfrac{1}{D_2} - \dfrac{1}{D_1}\right)$ | Energy ∝ new surface area | Fine grinding |
| **Bond's Law** | $E = K_B\!\left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_2}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_1}}\right)$ | Energy ∝ crack length | Intermediate |

> The energy required to fracture particles propagates through the bulk solid via cracks; the three laws differ in how they model the energy–size relationship. Kick assumes the work per unit volume of feed is constant; Rittinger assumes the work per unit *new surface* is constant; Bond assumes the work per unit *crack length* is constant — a geometric mean of the other two. **(2 marks for explanation of why each law exists)**

#### **Model Solution (b) — Numerical (7 marks)**

**(i) Reduction Ratio (1 mark):**

$$R = \frac{D_1}{D_2} = \frac{3\ \text{mm}}{0.5\ \text{mm}} = 6.0$$

**(ii) Specific Energy Consumption (1 mark):**

$$E_{\text{spec}} = \frac{18\ \text{kWh}}{1000\ \text{kg}} \times 1000 = 18\ \text{kWh/tonne}$$

**(iii) Verifying each law (5 marks):**

Step 1 — Convert units: $D_1 = 3000\ \mu m$, $D_2 = 500\ \mu m$.

Step 2 — **Kick's Law prediction:**

$$E_K = 7.5 \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{3000}{500}\right) = 7.5 \cdot \ln(6) = 7.5 \times 1.7918 = 13.44\ \text{kWh/tonne}$$

Step 3 — **Rittinger's Law prediction:**

$$\frac{1}{D_2} - \frac{1}{D_1} = \frac{1}{500} - \frac{1}{3000} = 0.002000 - 0.000333 = 0.001667\ \mu m^{-1}$$

$$E_R = 5000 \times 0.001667 = 8.33\ \text{kWh/tonne}$$

Step 4 — **Bond's Law prediction:**

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{D_2}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{D_1}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{500}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{3000}} = 0.04472 - 0.01826 = 0.02646\ \mu m^{-1/2}$$

$$E_B = 200 \times 0.02646 = 5.29\ \text{kWh/tonne}$$

**Comparison & Conclusion:**

| Law | Predicted Energy | Observed | Deviation |
|-----|------------------|----------|-----------|
| Kick | 13.44 kWh/tonne | 18 kWh/tonne | 25.3% |
| Rittinger | 8.33 kWh/tonne | 18 kWh/tonne | 53.7% |
| **Bond** | **5.29 kWh/tonne** | **18 kWh/tonne** | **— (no law fits!)** |

**Conclusion:** The data does not fit any single classical law — this is common in real food systems. The deviation arises from: (i) inefficiencies like friction and heat loss, (ii) the multi-modal nature of maize fracture, and (iii) screen-clogging at fine sizes. Among the three, **Kick's Law gives the closest match** for this intermediate grinding range. **(1 mark for conclusion)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2]**
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **construction and working of a hammer mill**. List the factors affecting its efficiency. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** In a ball mill experiment, **200 g of sugar** is ground for **10 minutes**. The initial surface mean diameter is **1.2 mm** and the final is **75 µm**. The Bond's Work Index of sugar is **$W_i = 11.5$ kWh/tonne**. Estimate the **power required** for grinding **1 tonne/hour** of sugar from 1.2 mm to 75 µm. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a) — Hammer Mill (7 marks)**

**Construction (4 marks):**
- **Hopper & feed chute** — gravity feeds the product into the milling chamber.
- **Rotor** — high-speed horizontal shaft carrying 4–8 rows of **swing hammers** (stainless steel) hinged on pins.
- **Breaker plates** — stationary hardened liners inside the chamber, providing the impact surface.
- **Perforated screen / sieve** — wrapped around the lower half of the chamber; product exits only when its size is < screen aperture (2–5 mm typical).
- **Drive unit** — 1–50 kW electric motor coupled via V-belt to a heavy flywheel.
- **Discharge tray** — collects the milled product.

**Working (2 marks):**
Material entering the chamber is struck by the high-velocity hammers (tip speed 50–80 m/s). Brittle materials shatter by **impact**; tougher materials get **sheared** against the breaker plates and perforated screen. Centrifugal force keeps oversize in the chamber; only particles smaller than the screen hole exit.

**Factors affecting efficiency (1 mark):**
1. **Screen aperture size** — finer screen → lower throughput, higher energy.
2. **Hammer tip speed** — higher speed → finer grind, more heat.
3. **Feed moisture content** — wet feed → clogging, reduced efficiency.
4. **Hammer wear** — dull hammers reduce impact force.
5. **Feed rate** — overload reduces grinding effectiveness.

#### **Model Solution (b) — Ball Mill Power (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Recall Bond's Work Index equation:**

$$W = W_i \cdot \left(\frac{10}{\sqrt{P}} - \frac{10}{\sqrt{F}}\right)$$

where $F$ = feed size (µm) at 80% passing, $P$ = product size (µm) at 80% passing, $W_i$ = Bond Work Index (kWh/tonne).

**Step 2 — Substitute the given values** (assume $F = 1200\ \mu m$, $P = 75\ \mu m$):

$$\frac{10}{\sqrt{75}} = \frac{10}{8.660} = 1.1547$$

$$\frac{10}{\sqrt{1200}} = \frac{10}{34.641} = 0.2887$$

**Step 3 — Compute the bracket:**

$$1.1547 - 0.2887 = 0.8660\ \mu m^{-1/2}$$

**Step 4 — Compute specific energy:**

$$W = 11.5 \times 0.8660 = 9.96\ \text{kWh/tonne}$$

**Step 5 — Compute power for 1 tonne/hour throughput:**

$$P = W \times \dot{m} = 9.96\ \text{kWh/tonne} \times 1\ \text{tonne/hr}$$

$$\boxed{P \approx 9.96\ \text{kW} \approx 10\ \text{kW}}$$

> Allow 10% margin for motor efficiency → recommend **11 kW motor**. **(7 marks: 2 marks formula, 2 marks substitution, 2 marks calculation, 1 mark conclusion)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps (KTU Board Pattern):**
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** Students often plug $D_1$ in mm and $D_2$ in µm in the same equation — all diameters **must** be in the **same units** before applying any law.
> 2. **Forgetting the $\ln$:** In Kick's Law, students mistakenly write $\dfrac{D_1}{D_2}$ instead of $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{D_1}{D_2}\right)$ — a guaranteed zero for that sub-part.
> 3. **Sign errors in Rittinger:** $\frac{1}{D_2}$ must be the *larger* term (since $D_2 < D_1$). Inverting this gives a *negative* energy.
> 4. **Confusing throughput with capacity:** "1 tonne/hr" refers to **mass flow rate** (m_dot), not the total mass ground.
> 5. **No unit for $K_R$ or $K_B$:** The examiner will deduct 0.5 marks if the constant's unit is omitted.
> 6. **Skipping the sieve analysis table:** Always tabulate mass retained, mass fraction, and cumulative % undersize. A raw conclusion without a table is penalized.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 2 Experiment: Size Reduction by Milling**
>
> ☐ **Definition:** Size reduction = mechanical disintegration of solids using compression, impact, attrition, shearing.
>
> ☐ **Milling** = size reduction using rotating/vibrating devices.
>
> ☐ **Four Forces:** Compression, Impact, Attrition, Cutting.
>
> ☐ **Three Laws:**
> - **Kick** → $E = K_K \ln(D_1/D_2)$ → coarse crushing
> - **Rittinger** → $E = K_R (1/D_2 - 1/D_1)$ → fine grinding
> - **Bond** → $E = K_B (1/\sqrt{D_2} - 1/\sqrt{D_1})$ → intermediate
>
> ☐ **Reduction Ratio:** $R = D_1 / D_2$ — large $R$ = more size reduction.
>
> ☐ **Equipment:** Hammer mill (impact), Ball mill (attrition+impact), Roller mill (compression), Pin mill (impact+shear).
>
> ☐ **Critical Speed of Ball Mill:** $N_c = (1/2\pi)\sqrt{g/R}$; operate at 65–80% of $N_c$.
>
> ☐ **Bond's Work Index equation:** $W = W_i (10/\sqrt{P} - 10/\sqrt{F})$; foods typically have $W_i = 8$–$14$ kWh/tonne.
>
> ☐ **PSD Analysis:** Use sieve shaker → compute mass fraction → plot frequency vs $\log$ sieve size → determine $D_{10}, D_{50}, D_{90}$.
>
> ☐ **Sauter mean diameter:** $d_{sg} = 1 / \sum (x_i / \bar{d}_i)$.
>
> ☐ **Efficiency:** $\eta = P_{\text{useful}} / P_{\text{input}} \times 100\%$.
>
> ☐ **Common food $W_i$ values:** Sugar ≈ 11.5, Wheat ≈ 9.5, Maize ≈ 8.0, Cocoa ≈ 13.0 (kWh/tonne, approximate).
>
> ☐ **Exam tip:** Always state the **range of applicability** of each law — 1 free mark in KTU valuations.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of particle size distribution of food material using sieve analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTL408 - FOOD PROCESS ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of particle size distribution of food material using sieve analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Particle Size Distribution (PSD)** is defined as the relative proportion (by mass, volume, or number) of particles of different sizes present in a particulate food material. In food process engineering, PSD is a **critical quality attribute (CQA)** that governs downstream processes such as mixing, dissolution, extraction, heat transfer, and reconstitution behaviour.

**Sieve Analysis** (also called **Sieve Classification** or **Mechanical Sieving**) is a standard, gravimetric laboratory method used to determine the particle size distribution of a granular or powdered food material by passing it through a stack of standard test sieves arranged in descending order of aperture size (largest aperture at top, smallest at bottom). The mass retained on each sieve is measured, and the cumulative weight fraction passing through (or retained on) each sieve is plotted against the aperture size to obtain the **particle size distribution curve**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Sieve analysis is a direct measurement technique in which a known mass of food particulate (e.g., wheat flour, ground spice, sugar, milk powder) is mechanically agitated through a nest of sieves of progressively smaller openings, and the mass retained on each sieve is used to compute the mass frequency and cumulative weight fraction at each aperture size.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a baker making *atta* (whole wheat flour) at home using a traditional hand-chakki. After grinding, the flour is not uniform — it contains a mixture of very fine powder, medium granules, and a few larger bran particles. To get a uniform flour, you pass the mixture through a stack of nested sieves (a fine muslin cloth on top, a coarser brass sieve below). What stays on each sieve tells you how much "coarse" versus "fine" material you have.

**Sieve analysis in a lab is simply the scientific, quantitative version of this everyday kitchen practice.** The only differences are:
- The sieves have **standardized, certified aperture sizes** (in micrometres, µm).
- The agitation is done by a **mechanical sieve shaker** for reproducible time and intensity.
- The retained mass is measured on a **precision analytical balance** (typically ±0.01 g accuracy).

### 1.3 Key Standardized Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Sieve Aperture Units Used in KTU Labs:**
> - **Micrometre (µm)** — 1 µm = $10^{-6}$ m (most common in food labs)
> - **Millimetre (mm)** — 1 mm = $10^{-3}$ m
> - **Mesh number** — Number of openings per linear inch (Tyler series or ASTM E11)

**Standard Sieve Series used globally:**
- **Tyler Standard Sieve Series** (based on 200 mesh = 74 µm)
- **ASTM E-11** (American Standard)
- **ISO 3310-1** (International Standard)
- **BS 410** (British Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constant Value to Remember:**
> The standard ratio between successive sieve apertures in a **geometric series** is:
> $$\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414 \quad \text{(for fine series)}$$
> $$\sqrt[4]{2} = 2^{1/4} \approx 1.189 \quad \text{(for fine sub-series)}$$
> This is the **Geometric Standard** mandated by Tyler & ISO series.

### 1.4 Why is Particle Size Distribution Important in Food Engineering?

| Application | Effect of PSD |
|---|---|
| **Wheat flour** | Influences dough rheology, water absorption, bread volume |
| **Milk powder** | Affects wettability, dispersibility, reconstitution time |
| **Instant coffee** | Smaller particles → faster dissolution |
| **Spice powders** | Controls flavour release kinetics |
| **Sugar** | Crystal size affects dissolution rate in beverages |
| **Salt** | Fine salt dissolves faster; coarse salt is used for toppings |
| **Cocoa powder** | Particle size governs mouthfeel and colour intensity |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Principle of Sieve Analysis

The fundamental assumption of sieve analysis is that a particle is considered to "pass" through a sieve if its **minimum cross-sectional dimension is smaller than the sieve aperture**. Conversely, a particle is "retained" if its minimum cross-section exceeds the aperture.

**The Why behind Sieving:**
1. **Gravity-driven separation:** Particles smaller than the aperture fall through under the action of the vertical agitation and gravity.
2. **Probability of passage:** The probability of a particle passing a given aperture depends on:
   - The number of opportunities to pass (number of impacts during shaking)
   - The orientation of the particle (elongated particles pass differently from spherical ones)
   - The load on the sieve (overloading prevents particles from reaching the mesh)
   - The presence of **fines clogging** the mesh openings (blinding)

### 2.2 Operational Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Sample Preparation:** The food material is dried (if required) to remove surface moisture that may cause agglomeration.
2. **Selection of Sieve Stack:** A set of 5–8 standard sieves is chosen covering the expected size range.
3. **Weighing the Sample:** A known mass (typically **100 g** for inorganic, **50 g** for fine food powders) is weighed accurately on an analytical balance.
4. **Loading the Sieves:** The sample is carefully transferred to the topmost (largest aperture) sieve.
5. **Mechanical Shaking:** The stack is placed in a sieve shaker and agitated for a fixed duration (typically **10–15 minutes** for food powders).
6. **Weighing Retained Mass:** After shaking, the mass retained on each sieve is weighed.
7. **Mass Reconciliation Check:** The total retained mass + mass in pan (undersize) should equal the initial mass within ±2% (acceptable loss).
8. **Calculations:** Compute weight fraction, cumulative weight fraction (oversize and undersize), and mass frequency for each sieve.
9. **Graphical Plot:** Plot **cumulative weight fraction (undersize)** on the Y-axis vs **aperture size** on the X-axis (semi-log plot recommended).
10. **Mean Size Determination:** Use the distribution curve to find $D_{50}$ (median), and optionally $D_{10}$ and $D_{90}$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Weight fraction on sieve $i$ | $w_i = \dfrac{m_i}{m_{\text{total}}}$ | dimensionless | $m_i$ = mass retained on sieve $i$ |
| 2 | Cumulative weight retained (oversize) | $W_{\text{ret},i} = \sum_{j=1}^{i} w_j$ | dimensionless | Sums from top sieve down |
| 3 | Cumulative weight passing (undersize) | $W_{\text{pass},i} = 1 - W_{\text{ret},i}$ | dimensionless | The KEY plotting quantity |
| 4 | Mass frequency (fraction) | $\Delta w_i = W_{\text{ret},i} - W_{\text{ret},i-1}$ | dimensionless | Bar plot for histogram |
| 5 | Arithmetic mean diameter | $D_{\text{mean}} = \sum w_i \cdot d_i$ | µm or mm | Weighted by mass |
| 6 | Geometric mean diameter | $\log D_g = \sum w_i \cdot \log d_i$ | µm | Better for log-normal distributions |
| 7 | Median size ($D_{50}$) | From graph: W = 0.5 | µm | Particle size at 50% passing |
| 8 | Specific surface area (spherical) | $S_w = \dfrac{6}{\rho_p \cdot D_{\text{mean}}}$ | m²/kg | Assumes spherical particles |
| 9 | Total specific surface | $S = \sum \dfrac{6 \cdot w_i}{\rho_p \cdot d_i}$ | m²/kg | Sum over all size classes |
| 10 | Mass balance check | $\sum m_i = m_{\text{initial}} \pm 2\%$ | g | For experimental error verification |

**Where:**
- $m_i$ = mass retained on sieve $i$ (g)
- $m_{\text{total}}$ = total mass of sample (g)
- $d_i$ = geometric mean of sieve $i$ and sieve $i+1$ apertures (µm)
- $w_i$ = weight fraction on sieve $i$
- $\rho_p$ = particle density of food material (kg/m³)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Geometric Mean Aperture $d_i$:** For sieve $i$ with aperture $D_i$ (top) and $D_{i+1}$ (bottom), the **geometric mean** is used as the representative particle size for that fraction:
> $$d_i = \sqrt{D_i \cdot D_{i+1}}$$
> This is preferred over the arithmetic mean because particle size distributions of food powders typically follow a **log-normal distribution**.

### 2.4 The Standard Sieve Series (Tyler Series)

The **Tyler Standard Scale** is the most common reference used in food engineering labs. It is based on a **200-mesh sieve = 74 µm opening** as the reference point. The aperture ratio between successive sieves is $\sqrt{2}$ for the coarse series and $\sqrt[4]{2}$ for the fine sub-series.

| Tyler Mesh | Aperture (µm) | Aperture (mm) | Typical Food Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 10 | 1680 | 1.680 | Crushed wheat, coarse grits |
| 20 | 841 | 0.841 | Cornmeal |
| 40 | 420 | 0.420 | Coarse flour |
| 60 | 250 | 0.250 | Medium flour |
| 80 | 177 | 0.177 | Fine flour |
| 100 | 149 | 0.149 | Refined flour |
| 140 | 105 | 0.105 | Fine sugar |
| 200 | 74 | 0.074 | Milk powder, cocoa |
| 325 | 44 | 0.044 | Pharmaceutical-grade powders |

### 2.5 Sources of Error in Sieve Analysis (Real-world Engineering Insight)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight — Common Sources of Error:**
> 1. **Overloading** the top sieve (recommended: ≤ 50 g per 200 mm diameter sieve for fine powders)
> 2. **Insufficient shaking time** (food powders with cohesive behaviour may need 15–20 min)
> 3. **Moisture** causing particles to stick to sieve wires (caking)
> 4. **Electrostatic charging** in dry food powders (especially milk powder, cocoa)
> 5. **Mesh blinding** — fines lodging in apertures and effectively reducing the opening size
> 6. **Particle attrition** — fragile particles (e.g., agglomerated milk powder) may break during shaking, shifting the distribution toward fines

**Real-world engineering utility:** PSD data obtained from sieve analysis is a direct input for:
- Sizing **cyclone separators** and **bag filters** in dust collection systems.
- Designing **storage silos** (angle of repose depends on PSD).
- Predicting **flowability** of food powders in Hoppers and conveyors.
- Modelling **drying** and **roasting** kinetics (heat transfer depends on particle size).
- Validating **milling efficiency** in size reduction equipment (ball mills, hammer mills, roller mills).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Numerical Worked Example (KTU Lab Standard)

**Given Data (Simulated Lab Result for 100 g of ground food material, e.g., wheat flour):**

A 100 g sample of wheat flour was subjected to sieve analysis using a stack of Tyler standard sieves. The mass retained on each sieve after 15 minutes of mechanical shaking is given below:

| Sieve No. | Tyler Mesh | Aperture (µm) | Mass Retained $m_i$ (g) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 (top) | 30 | 595 | 4.5 |
| 2 | 40 | 420 | 12.8 |
| 3 | 50 | 297 | 18.2 |
| 4 | 70 | 210 | 24.6 |
| 5 | 100 | 149 | 19.3 |
| 6 | 140 | 105 | 11.7 |
| 7 | 200 | 74 | 6.4 |
| 8 (pan) | — | < 74 | 2.5 |

**Total mass recovered** = $4.5 + 12.8 + 18.2 + 24.6 + 19.3 + 11.7 + 6.4 + 2.5 = 100.0$ g ✓ (Perfect mass balance)

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Calculations

#### Step 1: Compute Weight Fraction $w_i$ for each sieve

$$w_i = \frac{m_i}{m_{\text{total}}}$$

**Sieve 1:** $w_1 = \dfrac{4.5}{100} = 0.045$

**Sieve 2:** $w_2 = \dfrac{12.8}{100} = 0.128$

**Sieve 3:** $w_3 = \dfrac{18.2}{100} = 0.182$

**Sieve 4:** $w_4 = \dfrac{24.6}{100} = 0.246$

**Sieve 5:** $w_5 = \dfrac{19.3}{100} = 0.193$

**Sieve 6:** $w_6 = \dfrac{11.7}{100} = 0.117$

**Sieve 7:** $w_7 = \dfrac{6.4}{100} = 0.064$

**Sieve 8 (pan):** $w_8 = \dfrac{2.5}{100} = 0.025$

**Verification:** $\sum w_i = 0.045 + 0.128 + 0.182 + 0.246 + 0.193 + 0.117 + 0.064 + 0.025 = 1.000$ ✓

#### Step 2: Compute Cumulative Weight Retained (Oversize) $W_{\text{ret},i}$

$$W_{\text{ret},i} = \sum_{j=1}^{i} w_j$$

This is a running sum starting from the top sieve:

**Sieve 1:** $W_{\text{ret},1} = 0.045$

**Sieve 2:** $W_{\text{ret},2} = 0.045 + 0.128 = 0.173$

**Sieve 3:** $W_{\text{ret},3} = 0.173 + 0.182 = 0.355$

**Sieve 4:** $W_{\text{ret},4} = 0.355 + 0.246 = 0.601$

**Sieve 5:** $W_{\text{ret},5} = 0.601 + 0.193 = 0.794$

**Sieve 6:** $W_{\text{ret},6} = 0.794 + 0.117 = 0.911$

**Sieve 7:** $W_{\text{ret},7} = 0.911 + 0.064 = 0.975$

**Sieve 8:** $W_{\text{ret},8} = 0.975 + 0.025 = 1.000$ ✓

#### Step 3: Compute Cumulative Weight Passing (Undersize) $W_{\text{pass},i}$

$$W_{\text{pass},i} = 1 - W_{\text{ret},i}$$

**Sieve 1:** $W_{\text{pass},1} = 1 - 0.045 = 0.955$

**Sieve 2:** $W_{\text{pass},2} = 1 - 0.173 = 0.827$

**Sieve 3:** $W_{\text{pass},3} = 1 - 0.355 = 0.645$

**Sieve 4:** $W_{\text{pass},4} = 1 - 0.601 = 0.399$

**Sieve 5:** $W_{\text{pass},5} = 1 - 0.794 = 0.206$

**Sieve 6:** $W_{\text{pass},6} = 1 - 0.911 = 0.089$

**Sieve 7:** $W_{\text{pass},7} = 1 - 0.975 = 0.025$

**Sieve 8:** $W_{\text{pass},8} = 1 - 1.000 = 0.000$ ✓

#### Step 4: Compute Geometric Mean Aperture $d_i$ for Each Sieve

The geometric mean of each sieve's aperture with the next finer sieve:

$$d_i = \sqrt{D_i \cdot D_{i+1}}$$

**Sieve 1 (top – 595 µm and next 420 µm):**

$$d_1 = \sqrt{595 \times 420} = \sqrt{249900} = 499.9 \approx 500 \; \mu m$$

**Sieve 2 (420 µm and 297 µm):**

$$d_2 = \sqrt{420 \times 297} = \sqrt{124740} = 353.2 \;\mu m$$

**Sieve 3 (297 µm and 210 µm):**

$$d_3 = \sqrt{297 \times 210} = \sqrt{62370} = 249.7 \;\mu m$$

**Sieve 4 (210 µm and 149 µm):**

$$d_4 = \sqrt{210 \times 149} = \sqrt{31290} = 176.9 \;\mu m$$

**Sieve 5 (149 µm and 105 µm):**

$$d_5 = \sqrt{149 \times 105} = \sqrt{15645} = 125.1 \;\mu m$$

**Sieve 6 (105 µm and 74 µm):**

$$d_6 = \sqrt{105 \times 74} = \sqrt{7770} = 88.2 \;\mu m$$

**Sieve 7 (74 µm and pan ~37 µm):**

$$d_7 = \sqrt{74 \times 37} = \sqrt{2738} = 52.3 \;\mu m$$

(For the pan fraction, we take the aperture as half of the smallest sieve aperture.)

**Sieve 8 (pan):** $d_8 \approx 37 \; \mu m$ (or 0–37 µm range, midpoint for calculation)

#### Step 5: Compile the Full Analytical Matrix

| Sieve | Mesh | Aperture $D_i$ (µm) | $m_i$ (g) | $w_i$ | $W_{\text{ret}}$ | $W_{\text{pass}}$ | $d_i$ (µm) | $w_i \cdot d_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 30 | 595 | 4.5 | 0.045 | 0.045 | 0.955 | 500 | 22.50 |
| 2 | 40 | 420 | 12.8 | 0.128 | 0.173 | 0.827 | 353 | 45.18 |
| 3 | 50 | 297 | 18.2 | 0.182 | 0.355 | 0.645 | 250 | 45.50 |
| 4 | 70 | 210 | 24.6 | 0.246 | 0.601 | 0.399 | 177 | 43.54 |
| 5 | 100 | 149 | 19.3 | 0.193 | 0.794 | 0.206 | 125 | 24.13 |
| 6 | 140 | 105 | 11.7 | 0.117 | 0.911 | 0.089 | 88.2 | 10.32 |
| 7 | 200 | 74 | 6.4 | 0.064 | 0.975 | 0.025 | 52.3 | 3.35 |
| 8 | pan | <74 | 2.5 | 0.025 | 1.000 | 0.000 | 37 | 0.93 |
| **Total** | — | — | **100.0** | **1.000** | — | — | — | **195.47** |

#### Step 6: Compute Arithmetic Mean Particle Diameter

$$D_{\text{mean}} = \sum w_i \cdot d_i$$

$$D_{\text{mean}} = 195.47 \; \mu m$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification of $\sum w_i$ check:** $\sum w_i \cdot d_i = 195.47$ µm. The mean particle size of **195 µm** is in the typical range for medium-grade wheat flour (D50 ≈ 150–250 µm).

#### Step 7: Determine the Median Size $D_{50}$ from the Graph

$D_{50}$ is the particle size corresponding to $W_{\text{pass}} = 0.5$ (50% passing).

From the table:
- At 297 µm, $W_{\text{pass}} = 0.645$
- At 210 µm, $W_{\text{pass}} = 0.399$

By linear interpolation on the semi-log plot:

$$\log D_{50} = \log(297) + \frac{(0.5 - 0.645)}{(0.399 - 0.645)} \times \left[\log(210) - \log(297)\right]$$

$$\log D_{50} = 2.4728 + \frac{(-0.145)}{(-0.246)} \times (2.3222 - 2.4728)$$

$$\log D_{50} = 2.4728 + 0.5894 \times (-0.1506)$$

$$\log D_{50} = 2.4728 - 0.0887 = 2.3841$$

$$D_{50} = 10^{2.3841} \approx 242 \; \mu m$$

#### Step 8: Compute Specific Surface Area (assuming spherical particles)

**Given:** Particle density of wheat flour, $\rho_p = 1450 \; kg/m^3$ (typical).

$$S_w = \frac{6}{\rho_p \cdot D_{\text{mean}}}$$

$$S_w = \frac{6}{1450 \times 195.47 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$S_w = \frac{6}{0.2834} = 21.17 \; m^2/kg$$

#### Step 9: Verification & Mass Balance Check

**Mass recovered** = 100.0 g
**Initial mass** = 100.0 g
**% Loss** = $\dfrac{100 - 100}{100} \times 100 = 0\%$ ✓ (Within ±2% tolerance)

---

### 3.3 Graphical Representation

The standard KTU lab graph is a **semi-log plot** with:
- **X-axis (log scale):** Particle size (aperture) in µm
- **Y-axis (linear scale):** Cumulative weight fraction passing (undersize)

From the table data points, the resulting **S-shaped (sigmoidal) curve** is characteristic of a typical log-normal particle size distribution for a milled food material.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Components of a Sieve Analysis Setup

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Food Material Sample] --> B[Analytical Balance]
    B --> C[Top Sieve - Largest Aperture]
    C --> D[Sieve 2]
    D --> E[Sieve 3]
    E --> F[Sieve 4]
    F --> G[Sieve 5]
    G --> H[Bottom Sieve - Smallest Aperture]
    H --> I[Collection Pan - Undersize]
    I --> J[Sieve Shaker Mechanical Agitator]
    J --> K[Weighing of Retained Mass per Sieve]
    K --> L[Calculation Table]
    L --> M[Cumulative Weight Fraction vs Aperture Graph]
    M --> N[Mean Particle Size D50]
```

### 4.2 Process Flow: Step-by-Step Sieve Analysis Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Step 1: Collect representative food sample] --> P2[Step 2: Dry if necessary to constant moisture]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Weigh 100 g on analytical balance]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Arrange sieves in descending aperture order]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Place sample on topmost sieve]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6: Cover and clamp onto sieve shaker]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7: Shake for 10-15 minutes at fixed amplitude]
    P7 --> P8[Step 8: Disassemble stack and weigh retained mass per sieve]
    P8 --> P9{Mass balance check within 2 percent}
    P9 -->|Yes| P10[Step 9: Compute weight fraction and cumulative weight fraction]
    P9 -->|No| P11[Repeat experiment with fresh sample]
    P10 --> P12[Step 10: Plot semi-log graph of cumulative weight fraction vs aperture]
    P12 --> P13[Step 11: Read D50 from graph]
    P13 --> P14[Step 12: Calculate mean diameter and specific surface area]
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture: Information Flow in Sieve Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT DOMAIN]
        A1[Food Powder Sample]
        A2[Sieve Stack with Calibrated Apertures]
        A3[Sieve Shaker Settings]
    end

    subgraph PROCESS[PROCESS DOMAIN]
        B1[Mechanical Agitation]
        B2[Gravimetric Separation by Aperture]
        B3[Weighing Operation]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[OUTPUT DOMAIN]
        C1[Mass Retained per Sieve]
        C2[Cumulative Weight Fraction Table]
        C3[Particle Size Distribution Curve]
        C4[Mean Particle Diameter D50]
        C5[Specific Surface Area Sw]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B2
    A3 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> C4
    C1 --> C5
    C2 --> C5
```

### 4.4 Schematic: Vertical Arrangement of Sieve Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Cover Lid] --> S2[Sieve 1: 595 µm - Largest Aperture]
    S2 --> S3[Sieve 2: 420 µm]
    S3 --> S4[Sieve 3: 297 µm]
    S4 --> S5[Sieve 4: 210 µm]
    S5 --> S6[Sieve 5: 149 µm]
    S6 --> S7[Sieve 6: 105 µm]
    S7 --> S8[Sieve 7: 74 µm - Smallest Aperture]
    S8 --> S9[Collection Pan - Undersize Particles]
    S9 --> S10[Base Plate - Mechanical Shaker Mount]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Define particle size distribution. Why is its determination important in food process engineering?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Particle size distribution (PSD) is the relative proportion of particles of various sizes present in a granular or powdered food material. It is typically expressed as the weight (or volume) fraction of particles in specified size intervals, obtained by methods such as sieve analysis, laser diffraction, or image analysis.

**Importance (1 mark):**
PSD is a critical quality attribute that directly influences:
- Flowability of powders in hoppers and conveyors.
- Reconstitution and dissolution behaviour (e.g., instant coffee, milk powder).
- Heat and mass transfer rates during drying, roasting, and frying.
- Mouthfeel, texture, and sensory perception of the final product.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Examiners award the 2-mark definition only if you mention **"relative proportion"** and **"different size classes."** A vague "size of particles" answer will not receive full marks.

---

#### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**State the principle of sieve analysis. What is the role of a sieve shaker in this experiment?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Principle (2 marks):**
The principle of sieve analysis is based on **gravimetric separation** of a particulate food sample through a stack of standard test sieves arranged in descending order of aperture size. Particles whose minimum cross-section is smaller than the aperture pass through to the next finer sieve, while larger particles are retained. The mass retained on each sieve is measured and used to compute the weight fraction and cumulative weight fraction at each aperture size.

**Role of Sieve Shaker (1 mark):**
A sieve shaker provides **standardized, reproducible mechanical agitation** (vertical and horizontal motion) for a fixed duration. This ensures:
- Uniform exposure of all particles to the apertures.
- Prevention of human-induced variability.
- Dislodging of near-mesh particles to avoid clogging (blinding) of the mesh.

---

### Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**(a) Explain with a neat sketch the procedure for determining the particle size distribution of a food material (e.g., wheat flour) using sieve analysis. List the apparatus and chemicals required. (7 marks)**

**(b) The following data were obtained from a sieve analysis of 100 g of ground turmeric powder. Calculate the weight fraction, cumulative weight fraction (passing), and mean particle diameter. Given particle density = 1300 kg/m³, find the specific surface area. (7 marks)**

| Sieve No. | Mesh Size | Aperture (µm) | Mass Retained (g) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 30 | 595 | 5.0 |
| 2 | 40 | 420 | 10.5 |
| 3 | 50 | 297 | 18.0 |
| 4 | 70 | 210 | 22.5 |
| 5 | 100 | 149 | 20.0 |
| 6 | 140 | 105 | 14.0 |
| 7 | 200 | 74 | 7.0 |
| 8 | Pan | < 74 | 3.0 |

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Procedure and Apparatus (7 marks):**

**Apparatus Required (1 mark):**
1. Set of standard test sieves (Tyler or ASTM E-11 series), 200 mm or 100 mm diameter
2. Mechanical sieve shaker (with timer)
3. Analytical balance (accuracy ± 0.01 g)
4. Brush for cleaning sieves
5. Desiccator (for hygroscopic powders)
6. Sample splitter or riffle
7. Oven (for drying)

**Chemicals:** Not applicable (dry food material).

**Procedure (5 marks):**

1. **Sample Preparation (1 mark):** Obtain a representative sample of the food material (e.g., wheat flour) by coning and quartering or using a riffle splitter. If the material is moist, dry it in a hot air oven at 105 °C for 1–2 hours to constant weight and cool in a desiccator.

2. **Weighing (1 mark):** Accurately weigh **100 g** of the prepared sample on the analytical balance. Record the mass as $m_{\text{initial}}$.

3. **Sieve Stack Assembly (1 mark):** Select 7–8 standard test sieves covering the expected size range. Assemble them in **descending order of aperture size** (largest at top, smallest at bottom) on the sieve shaker, with a **collection pan at the bottom** and a **lid at the top**.

4. **Sieving Operation (1 mark):** Transfer the weighed sample quantitatively to the topmost sieve. Clamp the stack securely on the sieve shaker. Set the timer for **10–15 minutes** and start the shaker at the recommended amplitude (typically 1.5 mm vertical stroke at 2850 rpm for rotary shakers).

5. **Weighing Retained Fraction (1 mark):** After shaking, carefully disassemble the stack. Weigh the mass retained on each sieve and on the pan separately. Record as $m_1, m_2, \ldots, m_8$. Verify the **mass balance**: $\sum m_i$ should be within ±2% of $m_{\text{initial}}$.

6. **Calculation and Plotting:** Compute weight fractions and cumulative weight fractions. Plot **cumulative weight fraction (undersize) vs aperture size on a semi-log graph**. Determine the median size $D_{50}$ and the arithmetic mean diameter.

---

**(b) Calculations (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Weight fraction $w_i$ and Cumulative weight passing $W_{\text{pass},i}$ (3 marks)**

| Sieve | Aperture (µm) | $m_i$ (g) | $w_i$ | $W_{\text{ret}}$ | $W_{\text{pass}}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 595 | 5.0 | 0.050 | 0.050 | 0.950 |
| 2 | 420 | 10.5 | 0.105 | 0.155 | 0.845 |
| 3 | 297 | 18.0 | 0.180 | 0.335 | 0.665 |
| 4 | 210 | 22.5 | 0.225 | 0.560 | 0.440 |
| 5 | 149 | 20.0 | 0.200 | 0.760 | 0.240 |
| 6 | 105 | 14.0 | 0.140 | 0.900 | 0.100 |
| 7 | 74 | 7.0 | 0.070 | 0.970 | 0.030 |
| 8 | < 74 | 3.0 | 0.030 | 1.000 | 0.000 |

**Step 2: Geometric mean aperture $d_i$ and $w_i \cdot d_i$ (2 marks)**

| Sieve | $D_i$ (µm) | $D_{i+1}$ (µm) | $d_i = \sqrt{D_i \cdot D_{i+1}}$ (µm) | $w_i \cdot d_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 595 | 420 | 500 | 25.00 |
| 2 | 420 | 297 | 353 | 37.07 |
| 3 | 297 | 210 | 250 | 45.00 |
| 4 | 210 | 149 | 177 | 39.82 |
| 5 | 149 | 105 | 125 | 25.00 |
| 6 | 105 | 74 | 88.2 | 12.35 |
| 7 | 74 | 37 | 52.3 | 3.66 |
| 8 | 37 | 0 | 25 | 0.75 |

**Step 3: Arithmetic mean diameter (1 mark)**

$$D_{\text{mean}} = \sum w_i \cdot d_i = 25.00 + 37.07 + 45.00 + 39.82 + 25.00 + 12.35 + 3.66 + 0.75 = 188.65 \; \mu m$$

**Step 4: Specific surface area (1 mark)**

$$S_w = \frac{6}{\rho_p \cdot D_{\text{mean}}} = \frac{6}{1300 \times 188.65 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$S_w = \frac{6}{0.2452} = 24.47 \; m^2/kg$$

**Final Answer:** Mean particle diameter = **188.65 µm**; Specific surface area = **24.47 m²/kg**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]** [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

**(a) Discuss the various factors that affect the accuracy of sieve analysis results. How would you minimize these errors? (7 marks)**

**(b) Differentiate between the cumulative weight fraction (oversize) and the cumulative weight fraction (undersize) curves. Sketch a typical particle size distribution curve and label the median size $D_{50}$. (7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Factors Affecting Accuracy of Sieve Analysis (7 marks):**

**1. Sample-Related Factors (2 marks):**
- **Moisture content:** Surface moisture causes particles to agglomerate, leading to artificially coarse readings. **Remedy:** Dry the sample to constant weight before testing.
- **Sample heterogeneity:** Non-representative sampling skews the distribution. **Remedy:** Use a **riffle sampler** or **coning and quartering** technique.
- **Electrostatic charging:** Fine dry powders (e.g., cocoa, milk powder) cling to sieve wires. **Remedy:** Use an antistatic agent or add 0.1–0.5% fumed silica as a dispersant.

**2. Sieve-Related Factors (2 marks):**
- **Mesh blinding/clogging:** Fines lodge in apertures, reducing the effective opening. **Remedy:** Use sieves with ultrasonic cleaning, or limit shaking time.
- **Worn or damaged sieves:** Torn wires alter aperture size. **Remedy:** Periodically inspect sieves under a microscope; replace when calibration is lost.
- **Incorrect sieve selection:** Stack range not matching the sample's size range. **Remedy:** Pre-test with a single coarse and single fine sieve to bracket the distribution.

**3. Operational Factors (2 marks):**
- **Insufficient shaking time:** Under-shaking leaves particles on the wrong sieve. **Remedy:** Use a fixed time of 15 minutes; verify by re-shaking for 5 minutes and checking if < 0.2% additional mass passes.
- **Overloading:** Too much sample prevents particles from reaching the mesh. **Remedy:** Limit load to 25–50 g per 200 mm sieve for fine powders.
- **Amplitude/frequency variation:** Inconsistent shaker settings. **Remedy:** Always use a calibrated mechanical shaker with fixed settings.

**4. Particle-Related Factors (1 mark):**
- **Particle shape:** Elongated or flaky particles may pass through the long dimension. **Remedy:** Report PSD as "by sieve diameter" and note the limitation.
- **Attrition:** Brittle particles break during shaking. **Remedy:** Use gentler shaking (lower amplitude) for fragile agglomerates.

---

**(b) Cumulative Oversize vs Undersize Curves (7 marks):**

**Definitions (3 marks):**

| Curve Type | Definition | Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Cumulative Oversize (Retained)** | Total mass fraction retained **on or above** a given sieve | Useful for plant engineers to know how much oversize product is generated |
| **Cumulative Undersize (Passing)** | Total mass fraction passing **through or below** a given sieve | Most common in academic and KTU exams; used to find $D_{50}$ |

**Mathematical Relationship:**

$$W_{\text{pass}}(D) = 1 - W_{\text{ret}}(D)$$

The two curves are **mirror images** of each other about the line $W = 0.5$.

**Characteristics of the S-Curve (2 marks):**
- The cumulative undersize curve is **S-shaped (sigmoidal)** on a semi-log plot.
- The steepness of the curve indicates the **narrowness** of the distribution:
  - **Steep curve** → Narrow (uniform) size distribution
  - **Shallow curve** → Wide (broad) size distribution
- The **inflection point** (slope maximum) corresponds to the **mode** of the frequency distribution.

**Labeling of $D_{50}$ (2 marks):**
- $D_{50}$ is the particle diameter at which the **cumulative weight fraction passing = 0.5** (i.e., 50%).
- It is read by drawing a horizontal line from $W = 0.5$ on the Y-axis to intersect the S-curve, then dropping a vertical line to the X-axis.
- $D_{50}$ is also called the **median diameter** of the distribution.

**Sketch Description (for verbal answer):**
> A typical curve starts near $W = 0$ at the largest aperture (left side of X-axis) and rises sigmoidally to $W = 1$ at the smallest aperture. The point on the curve where $W = 0.5$ is marked as $D_{50}$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the mass balance check:** Examiners allocate 1 mark specifically for verifying that $\sum m_i$ is within ±2% of $m_{\text{initial}}$. Omitting this check costs a mark.
> 2. **Using arithmetic mean of apertures instead of geometric mean:** The geometric mean $d_i = \sqrt{D_i \cdot D_{i+1}}$ is mandated for log-normal food powder distributions. Using arithmetic mean is a common 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Confusing oversize and undersize:** Many students compute cumulative weight retained but plot it incorrectly on the Y-axis without converting to undersize. Always plot the **undersize (cumulative passing)** for standard KTU graphs.
> 4. **Forgetting to specify particle density in specific surface area calculation:** Without $\rho_p$, the specific surface area cannot be calculated. The question may give it in kg/m³ or g/cm³ — **convert units carefully.**
> 5. **Not labelling the X-axis as log scale:** For KTU graphs, the X-axis (aperture) must be on a **log scale** to give the S-shaped curve. Linear scale gives a misleading plot.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **PSD** = relative proportion of particles in different size classes; critical for food powder quality.
- ✅ **Sieve analysis** is a **direct, gravimetric, low-cost** method, valid for particles roughly **45 µm – 100 mm**.
- ✅ Standard sieve series: **Tyler, ASTM E-11, ISO 3310-1** — all based on geometric progression with ratio $\sqrt{2}$ or $2^{1/4}$.
- ✅ **200 mesh = 74 µm** is the international reference.
- ✅ Procedure: dry → weigh 100 g → stack 5–8 sieves → shake 10–15 min → weigh retained mass → verify mass balance (±2%) → compute.
- ✅ **Weight fraction:** $w_i = m_i / m_{\text{total}}$.
- ✅ **Cumulative weight passing (undersize):** $W_{\text{pass},i} = 1 - \sum w_j$ (from top).
- ✅ **Geometric mean aperture for sieve i:** $d_i = \sqrt{D_i \cdot D_{i+1}}$.
- ✅ **Arithmetic mean diameter:** $D_{\text{mean}} = \sum w_i \cdot d_i$.
- ✅ **Geometric mean diameter:** $\log D_g = \sum w_i \cdot \log d_i$ (preferred for log-normal distributions).
- ✅ **Median size $D_{50}$:** particle size at $W_{\text{pass}} = 0.5$ (read from semi-log plot).
- ✅ **Specific surface area (spherical particles):** $S_w = 6 / (\rho_p \cdot D_{\text{mean}})$.
- ✅ **Graph type:** Semi-log plot — **Y-axis linear** (cumulative weight fraction), **X-axis log** (aperture in µm).
- ✅ **Curve shape:** Sigmoidal (S-shaped) for cumulative passing.
- ✅ **Common errors:** mesh blinding, overloading, electrostatic charging, moisture-induced caking, attrition.
- ✅ **Limitations of sieve analysis:** Not suitable for particles < 45 µm (use laser diffraction); assumes spherical particles; cannot detect shape.
- ✅ **Mass balance verification** is mandatory and carries dedicated marks in KTU valuation.
- ✅ **Specific surface area** is **inversely proportional** to particle size — finer powders have much higher surface area, hence higher reactivity, faster dissolution, and higher dust explosion risk.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Development of food product using single screw extrusion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTL408 - FOOD PROCESS ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Development of food product using single screw extrusion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Aim of the Experiment
To develop a food product (typically a snack, pasta, or texturized product) using a **single screw extruder** and to evaluate the effect of key process parameters such as **barrel temperature**, **screw speed**, **feed moisture content**, and **feed composition** on the quality characteristics of the extrudate.

### 1.2 Formal Definition
> [!NOTE]
> **Extrusion Cooking (HTST Extrusion):**
> *Extrusion is a continuous thermo-mechanical process in which food material is forced to flow, under controlled conditions (temperature, pressure, shear), through a die, resulting in products of defined shape, density, and texture.* In **single screw extrusion**, material transport is achieved by a single rotating helical screw inside a heated barrel.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of a meat grinder / Indian Murukku press:**
> When you push dough through a small opening at the back of a hand-press (murukku maker), a helical screw rotates and pushes the dough through a perforated plate (die), shaping it into spirals or sticks. The pressure builds up because the dough has nowhere to go except through the small holes. **An extruder is exactly this — but industrial, heated, and high-pressure.** The starch inside the food actually "cooks" due to the combined action of heat (from the barrel heaters) and friction (from the rotating screw). The sudden drop in pressure at the die causes the water to flash into steam, puffing the product.

### 1.4 Industrial Significance of Single Screw Extrusion
- Used to manufacture **expanded snacks, breakfast cereals, pasta, pet food, and texturized vegetable protein (TVP)**.
- It is classified as an **HTST (High Temperature Short Time)** process.
- Key physical phenomena: **starch gelatinization, protein denaturation, moisture vapor flash-off (puffing), and product shaping**.

---

### 1.5 Standard Process Metrics (to be recorded)
| Parameter | Typical Range |
|---|---|
| Barrel Temperature | **100 – 200 °C** |
| Screw Speed | **100 – 500 rpm** |
| Feed Moisture Content | **10 – 30 % (w.b.)** |
| Die Pressure | **2 – 15 MPa** |
| Specific Mechanical Energy (SME) | **100 – 500 Wh/kg** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step
1. **Feeding:** Pre-conditioned feed (flour/meal with controlled moisture) is fed into the hopper by a volumetric or gravimetric feeder.
2. **Conveying:** The rotating screw conveys the material along the barrel. Friction between the material and barrel wall is critical for forward drag flow.
3. **Compression / Kneading:** As the screw flights become shallower (decreasing *pitch*), the material is compressed, generating **shear and pressure**.
4. **Cooking Zone:** External band heaters raise the temperature; mechanical shear dissipates additional energy (viscous dissipation).
5. **Metering & Shaping:** At the front end, the high-pressure plasticized melt is forced through a **die** of specific geometry.
6. **Puffing & Drying:** As extrudate exits the die, the sudden pressure drop causes **flash evaporation** of moisture → expansion (puffing).
7. **Cutting:** A rotating face cutter slices the extrudate into pieces of defined length.

---

### 2.2 Components of a Single Screw Extruder
- **Hopper** — holds raw feed
- **Feeder** — volumetric (screw) or gravimetric (belt)
- **Barrel** — jacketed with band heaters
- **Screw** — feed zone → compression zone → metering zone
- **Die** — gives final shape
- **Knife / Cutter** — at die face
- **Drive System** — variable-speed motor with gearbox
- **Control Panel** — for temperature zones, rpm, torque

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are tested repeatedly in viva voce and university exams for this experiment.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Throughput (Mass flow rate)** | $$Q_m = \rho \, A \, V$$ | $Q_m$ = kg/s, $\rho$ = density (kg/m³), $A$ = cross-section (m²), $V$ = velocity (m/s) |
| 2 | **Drag flow (Screw conveying)** | $$Q_d = \frac{\pi^2 D^2 N \, \sin\theta \, \cos\theta \, H}{2}$$ | $D$ = screw diameter, $N$ = rpm, $\theta$ = helix angle, $H$ = channel depth |
| 3 | **Pressure flow (back flow)** | $$Q_p = -\frac{\pi D \, H^3 \, \sin^2\theta \, \Delta P}{12 \eta L}$$ | $\Delta P$ = pressure rise, $\eta$ = viscosity, $L$ = length |
| 4 | **Net Throughput** | $$Q_{net} = Q_d - Q_p$$ | Combine drag and pressure components |
| 5 | **Specific Mechanical Energy** | $$SME = \frac{2\pi N \, T}{m_{\text{dry}}}$$ | $T$ = torque (N·m), $m_{\text{dry}}$ = dry feed rate (kg/s). Units: J/kg or Wh/kg |
| 6 | **SME (alternate)** | $$SME = \frac{\tau \, \omega}{Q}$$ | $\tau$ = torque, $\omega$ = angular velocity, $Q$ = mass flow |
| 7 | **Expansion Ratio (ER)** | $$ER = \frac{D_e^2}{D_d^2}$$ | $D_e$ = extrudate diameter, $D_d$ = die diameter |
| 8 | **Specific Length (SL)** | $$SL = \frac{L_e}{D_e}$$ | $L_e$ = length of extrudate |
| 9 | **Moisture (w.b.)** | $$M_{wb} = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100$$ | $W_w$ = wet weight, $W_d$ = dry weight |
| 10 | **Moisture (d.b.)** | $$M_{db} = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100$$ | As above |
| 11 | **Bulk Density** | $$\rho_b = \frac{m}{V_b}$$ | $m$ = mass, $V_b$ = bulk volume (by seed displacement) |
| 12 | **Water Absorption Index (WAI)** | $$WAI = \frac{W_g}{W_s}$$ | $W_g$ = weight of gel, $W_s$ = weight of dry sample |
| 13 | **Water Solubility Index (WSI)** | $$WSI = \frac{W_{dss}}{W_s} \times 100$$ | $W_{dss}$ = weight of dried solids in supernatant |

---

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of These Equations
- **SME** is the single most reported engineering parameter for extruders in published research — it correlates directly with starch gelatinization and protein texturization.
- **Expansion Ratio** is critical for snack manufacturers because consumers perceive higher puff as "better quality."
- **WAI/WSI** indicate the degree of starch modification — directly affecting the **digestibility** and **texture** of breakfast cereals.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Procedure & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Laboratory Procedure (Step-by-Step)

> [!NOTE]
> The following is the **standard KTU practical sequence**. Examiners often ask students to write this in the record.

#### A. Pre-Experiment Preparation
1. **Selection of raw material:** e.g., corn flour, rice flour, sorghum flour, or a blend (maize + soy).
2. **Formulation:** Decide the composition, e.g., 80 % corn flour + 20 % soy flour.
3. **Conditioning:** Adjust moisture content to the desired level (typically 14–18 % w.b.) by adding a known quantity of water and tempering for **1–2 hours**.
4. **Pre-conditioning of extruder:**
   - Switch on the **barrel heaters** and set temperatures in the three zones (feed, compression, metering).
   - Allow the barrel to reach the set temperature (≈ 30 minutes stabilization).
5. **Calibration of feeder:** Set screw feeder rpm to achieve a known feed rate (e.g., 20 kg/h).

#### B. Steady-State Operation
6. Start the **main drive motor** at the lowest screw speed (e.g., 100 rpm).
7. Begin feeding material; observe the **current/torque** reading stabilize (steady state takes ≈ 5–10 min after start-up).
8. Once steady state is reached, **collect the extrudate** continuously for a fixed time (e.g., 60 s).
9. Record: **screw speed (rpm), torque (%), barrel temperatures, die pressure, feed rate, extrudate mass collected, time.**
10. Repeat for at least **3 different screw speeds** (e.g., 150, 200, 250 rpm) and/or **2 different temperatures**, keeping other parameters constant.

#### C. Post-Processing & Evaluation
11. Allow the extrudate to **cool and dry** at room temperature (or in a tray dryer at 50 °C for 1 h).
12. Measure:
    - Diameter ($D_e$) and length ($L_e$) of extrudates using vernier caliper (mean of 10 readings).
    - Bulk density by **seed (rape seed) displacement method**.
    - Moisture content by **oven drying at 105 °C for 3 h**.
13. Sensory evaluation by a small panel for colour, texture, taste (if applicable).
14. Calculate **Expansion Ratio (ER), Specific Length (SL), Bulk Density, SME, Throughput**.

#### D. Cleaning & Shutdown
15. Flush the barrel and screw with raw maize grit to remove residue.
16. Switch off heaters → wait for cooling → stop motor → switch off feeder.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Numerical Example

> [!TIP]
> **This is a KTU University-Exam-style calculation frequently asked for 7 marks.**

**Given:**
- Screw diameter $D = 50 \text{ mm}$
- Channel depth $H = 5 \text{ mm}$
- Screw speed $N = 200 \text{ rpm}$
- Helix angle $\theta = 20°$
- Die pressure $\Delta P = 5 \text{ MPa}$
- Melt viscosity $\eta = 1000 \text{ Pa·s}$
- Screw length $L = 800 \text{ mm}$

**Find:** (a) Drag flow $Q_d$, (b) Pressure flow $Q_p$, (c) Net throughput $Q_{net}$.

**Step 1 — Convert all units to SI:**
$$D = 0.050 \text{ m}, \quad H = 0.005 \text{ m}, \quad L = 0.800 \text{ m}, \quad \Delta P = 5 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 2 — Compute drag flow using the formula:**

$$Q_d = \frac{\pi^2 D^2 \, N \, \sin\theta \, \cos\theta \, H}{2}$$

Convert N to rev/sec:
$$N = \frac{200}{60} = 3.333 \text{ rev/s}$$

Substitute trigonometric values:
$$\sin(20°) = 0.3420, \quad \cos(20°) = 0.9397$$

Now compute each factor:
$$\pi^2 = 9.8696$$

$$D^2 = (0.050)^2 = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$N = 3.333 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

$$\sin\theta \cos\theta = (0.3420)(0.9397) = 0.3214$$

$$H = 0.005 \text{ m}$$

Putting together the numerator:
$$\text{Numerator} = (9.8696)(2.5 \times 10^{-3})(3.333)(0.3214)(0.005)$$

Stepwise multiplication:
$$9.8696 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-3} = 0.02467$$

$$0.02467 \times 3.333 = 0.08223$$

$$0.08223 \times 0.3214 = 0.02643$$

$$0.02643 \times 0.005 = 1.3215 \times 10^{-4}$$

Finally:
$$Q_d = \frac{1.3215 \times 10^{-4}}{2} = 6.607 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{Q_d \approx 6.61 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \;\; (\approx 237.8 \text{ L/h})}$$

**Step 3 — Compute pressure flow:**

$$Q_p = -\frac{\pi D \, H^3 \, \sin^2\theta \, \Delta P}{12 \, \eta \, L}$$

Compute each term:
$$\pi D = \pi (0.050) = 0.1571$$

$$H^3 = (0.005)^3 = 1.25 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^3$$

$$\sin^2\theta = (0.3420)^2 = 0.1170$$

$$\Delta P = 5 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$$

$$12 \eta L = (12)(1000)(0.800) = 9600$$

Numerator:
$$(0.1571)(1.25 \times 10^{-7})(0.1170)(5 \times 10^6)$$

Stepwise:
$$0.1571 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-7} = 1.9638 \times 10^{-8}$$

$$1.9638 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.1170 = 2.2977 \times 10^{-9}$$

$$2.2977 \times 10^{-9} \times 5 \times 10^6 = 1.1488 \times 10^{-2}$$

Finally:
$$Q_p = -\frac{0.011488}{9600} = -1.1967 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{Q_p \approx -1.20 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}}$$

(The negative sign indicates that pressure flow opposes drag flow.)

**Step 4 — Net throughput:**

$$Q_{net} = Q_d - Q_p = 6.607 \times 10^{-5} - (-1.20 \times 10^{-6})$$

$$Q_{net} = 6.607 \times 10^{-5} + 1.20 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{net} \approx 6.727 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \;\; (\approx 242.2 \text{ L/h})}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to mass flow rate** (assuming dough density ≈ 1200 kg/m³):
$$Q_m = \rho \, Q_{net} = 1200 \times 6.727 \times 10^{-5} = 0.0807 \text{ kg/s}$$

$$\boxed{Q_m \approx 290.6 \text{ kg/h}}$$

---

### 3.3 Typical Observation Table (Analytical Matrix)

| Trial No. | Screw Speed (rpm) | Torque (%) | Barrel Temp. (°C) | Feed Rate (kg/h) | Extrudate Mass in 60 s (g) | Die Diameter (mm) | Extrudate Diameter $D_e$ (mm) | Length $L_e$ (mm) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 150 | 35 | 150 | 18.0 | 305 | 4.0 | 7.2 | 65 |
| 2 | 200 | 42 | 150 | 20.0 | 340 | 4.0 | 7.8 | 72 |
| 3 | 250 | 48 | 150 | 22.5 | 380 | 4.0 | 8.4 | 80 |

| Trial No. | ER = $D_e^2/D_d^2$ | SL = $L_e/D_e$ | Bulk Density (kg/m³) | SME (Wh/kg) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 3.24 | 9.03 | 220 | 152 |
| 2 | 3.80 | 9.23 | 175 | 178 |
| 3 | 4.41 | 9.52 | 140 | 196 |

---

### 3.4 Expected Trends (for Discussion in Record)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These trends are the most-likely viva questions.

| Variable ↑ | Expansion Ratio | Bulk Density | SME |
|---|---|---|---|
| Screw Speed | **Increases** (initially, then decreases due to residence time ↓) | **Decreases** | **Increases** (shear ↑) |
| Temperature | **Increases** (more puff) | **Decreases** | **Decreases** (melt ↓) |
| Feed Moisture | **Decreases** (denser) | **Increases** | **Decreases** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of a Single Screw Extruder

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rawFeed[Raw Material Flour Blend] --> conditioner[Conditioner Moisturizer 14 to 18 percent wb]
    conditioner --> hopper[Feed Hopper]
    hopper --> feeder[Volumetric or Gravimetric Feeder]
    feeder --> feedZone[Feed Zone Screw Conveyor]
    feedZone --> compressZone[Compression Zone Shear and Heat]
    compressZone --> meteringZone[Metering Zone High Pressure Melt]
    barrelHeater[Barrel Heaters Zone 1 Zone 2 Zone 3] -.heats.-> feedZone
    barrelHeater -.heats.-> compressZone
    barrelHeater -.heats.-> meteringZone
    motor[Variable Speed Drive Motor and Gearbox] -.drives.-> screwShaft
    screwShaft[Screw Shaft with Helical Flights] --> feedZone
    screwShaft --> compressZone
    screwShaft --> meteringZone
    meteringZone --> die[Die Plate Shapes Product]
    die --> cutter[Rotating Face Cutter]
    cutter --> extrudate[Puffed Extrudate Flashing Steam]
    extrudate --> dryer[Conveyor Dryer or Ambient Cooling]
    dryer --> package[Finished Snack or Cereal Product]
    controlPanel[Control Panel Temp rpm Torque] -.monitors.-> barrelHeater
    controlPanel -.monitors.-> motor
```

---

### 4.2 Schematic of Screw Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph sz1[FEED ZONE Deep Channel Low Pressure]
        A1[Deep flights H max] --> A2[Pitch constant]
    end
    subgraph sz2[COMPRESSION ZONE Tapered Channel]
        B1[Channel depth decreases] --> B2[Kneading blocks optional]
    end
    subgraph sz3[METERING ZONE Shallow Channel High Pressure]
        C1[Shallow flights H min] --> C2[Highest shear and pressure]
    end
    sz1 --> sz2 --> sz3 --> dieE[Die Orifice]
    dieE --> cutterE[Face Cutter]
```

---

### 4.3 Typical Observation Photograph (Schematic of Extrudate Cross-Section)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    dieOpen[Die Orifice mm] -->|exit| melt[Melt Flow High Pressure]
    melt --> flashZone[Flash Evaporation Zone]
    flashZone --> skinForm[Outer Skin Formation]
    flashZone --> innerCell[Inner Cellular Structure Air Cells]
    skinForm --> finalProduct[Final Puffed Extrudate]
    innerCell --> finalProduct
```

---

### 4.4 Process Flow — Lab Setup

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rawMat[Raw Material Weighing] --> grindM[Grinding and Sieving]
    grindM --> blendM[Blending with Salt Sugar etc]
    blendM --> condM[Moisture Conditioning]
    condM --> extruderM[Single Screw Extruder Set Parameters]
    extruderM --> collectM[Extrudate Collection and Cooling]
    collectM --> dryM[Drying Tray Dryer 50 C]
    dryM --> analysisM[Quality Analysis Diameter Length Density SME]
    analysisM --> sensoryM[Sensory Evaluation Panel]
    sensoryM --> resultM[Result and Discussion]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

**[KTU University Exam – Apr 2024]**

**Q1.** Define **extrusion cooking** and name any **three industrially important products** made by single screw extrusion.

**Model Answer:**
*Extrusion cooking is a continuous HTST (High Temperature Short Time) thermo-mechanical process in which a food material is forced through a die under controlled temperature, shear, and pressure, resulting in a shaped, cooked product.* Three industrial products: (i) expanded snacks (e.g., kurkure, cheese balls), (ii) breakfast cereals (corn flakes, oats), (iii) texturized vegetable protein (TVP) / pasta.

---

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2.** Differentiate between **single screw extruder** and **twin screw extruder** in any **three aspects**.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Single Screw | Twin Screw |
|---|---|---|
| Capital cost | **Lower** | **Higher** |
| Mixing ability | **Poor** | **Excellent** |
| Self-wiping | **No** | **Yes** (co-rotating) |
| Typical products | Snacks, cereals | Pet food, confectionery |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Apr 2023]**

**(a)** Describe the **construction and working of a single screw extruder** with a neat diagram. Mention the role of each barrel zone. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **factors affecting the extrudate quality** during single screw extrusion cooking. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer for (a):

A single screw extruder consists of the following parts:

1. **Hopper and Feeder** – Stores and supplies raw feed at a controlled rate (volumetric or gravimetric).
2. **Barrel** – A cylindrical jacket fitted with **electrical band heaters** divided into **3 zones**:
   - **Feed Zone** – Deep flight channel; acts as a conveyor; material is loosely packed.
   - **Compression / Kneading Zone** – Channel depth decreases; mechanical shear and conduction heat raise temperature; **starch granules begin to gelatinize**.
   - **Metering Zone** – Shallow flights; acts as a positive displacement pump; builds up **die pressure** (typically 2–15 MPa).
3. **Screw** – A rotating helical shaft. Its design parameters (diameter D, pitch, helix angle θ, L/D ratio) decide the throughput and SME.
4. **Die Assembly** – Restrictive plate that shapes the melt and creates back-pressure.
5. **Knife / Cutter** – Rotating blade at the die face, cuts the extrudate to desired length.
6. **Drive Motor** – Variable-speed motor + gearbox (typically 5–50 kW).
7. **Control Panel** – Displays & controls rpm, torque, zone temperatures.

**Working:** Feed enters via hopper → conveyed by screw → compressed → heated (electrical + viscous dissipation) → melted starch/protein cooked → forced through die → flash evaporation at die exit causes puffing → cutter slices product.

#### Model Answer for (b):

**Factors affecting extrudate quality:**

| Factor | Effect on ER (Expansion Ratio) | Effect on Bulk Density | Effect on Texture |
|---|---|---|---|
| ↑ Screw Speed | Increases initially, then decreases | Decreases | Crisper |
| ↑ Barrel Temperature | Increases | Decreases | Lighter, more porous |
| ↑ Feed Moisture | Decreases | Increases | Harder, less puffed |
| ↑ Feed Particle Size | Decreases | Increases | Coarser texture |
| ↑ Die L/D Ratio | Decreases | Increases | Denser |

- **Barrel Temperature** governs starch gelatinization degree.
- **Screw Speed** governs residence time and shear (SME).
- **Feed Moisture** acts as a plasticizer — too low causes scorching; too high causes steaming.
- **Feed Composition** (protein, fiber, fat content) significantly alters expansion and texture.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** What is **Specific Mechanical Energy (SME)**? Derive its formula and state its significance in extrusion. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** During a single screw extrusion trial, the following data was recorded: Screw speed = 200 rpm; Torque = 18 N·m; Dry feed rate = 0.02 kg/s. Calculate the **SME** in J/kg and Wh/kg. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer for (a):

**SME Definition:** *Specific Mechanical Energy is the total mechanical energy input per unit mass of dry feed, supplied by the rotating screw into the food material.*

**Derivation:**

Power delivered by screw:
$$P = 2 \pi N \, T$$

Energy per unit mass:
$$SME = \frac{P}{m_{dry}} = \frac{2 \pi N \, T}{m_{dry}}$$

where $N$ is in rev/s. If $N$ is in rpm:
$$SME = \frac{2 \pi N \, T}{60 \times m_{dry}} \quad \text{(in J/kg)}$$

**Significance:**
- Represents the **intensity of mechanical shear** input.
- Correlates with starch modification, protein texturization, and product expansion.
- High SME (> 400 Wh/kg) → over-cooked, dense product; Low SME (< 100 Wh/kg) → under-cooked, glassy product.

#### Model Answer for (b):

**Given:**
$$N = 200 \text{ rpm}, \quad T = 18 \text{ N·m}, \quad m_{dry} = 0.02 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 1 — Convert rpm to rev/s:**
$$N = \frac{200}{60} = 3.333 \text{ rev/s}$$
