**Step 2 — Calculate power:**
$$P = 2 \pi N T = 2 \pi (3.333)(18) = 376.99 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate SME in J/kg:**
$$SME = \frac{P}{m_{dry}} = \frac{376.99}{0.02} = 18849.5 \text{ J/kg}$$

$$\boxed{SME \approx 18{,}850 \text{ J/kg}}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Wh/kg:**
$$SME = \frac{18849.5}{3600} = 5.236 \text{ Wh/kg}$$

$$\boxed{SME \approx 5.24 \text{ Wh/kg}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students **commonly forget to convert rpm to rev/s** when computing SME, leading to a wrong answer by a factor of 60.
> - Many students confuse **drag flow direction sign** in pressure flow equation — remember $Q_p$ is always **negative** because it opposes $Q_d$.
> - Do **not** omit unit conversion (mm → m) in screw geometry problems; this is the single most frequent **mark-losing error** (–2 marks).
> - In viva, examiners often ask **"Why does the product puff at the die exit?"** — answer: *sudden pressure drop causes superheated water to flash into steam, leaving porous structure.*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Extrusion** = HTST process combining **heat + shear + pressure** to cook & shape food.
- [ ] **Single screw extruder** has **3 barrel zones**: Feed → Compression → Metering.
- [ ] **Drag flow** ($Q_d$) drives material forward; **Pressure flow** ($Q_p$) opposes it.
- [ ] **Net throughput** $Q_{net} = Q_d - Q_p$.
- [ ] **SME** = $\dfrac{2 \pi N T}{m_{dry}}$ — must convert rpm to rev/s.
- [ ] **Expansion Ratio (ER)** = $D_e^2 / D_d^2$ — measures puffing.
- [ ] **Bulk density** measured by **seed (rape seed) displacement** method.
- [ ] **Higher screw speed & temperature** → **higher ER, lower bulk density**.
- [ ] **Higher feed moisture** → **lower ER, higher bulk density**.
- [ ] **Flash evaporation** at die exit causes **puffing**.
- [ ] **Starch gelatinization & protein denaturation** occur during extrusion.
- [ ] **Moisture content** typical feed range = **14–18 % w.b.**
- [ ] **L/D ratio** of screw typically **15:1 to 30:1**.
- [ ] **WAI / WSI** indicate starch modification degree.
- [ ] Always report results as **mean ± SD** for ≥ 3 readings.
- [ ] Industrial products: **snacks, breakfast cereals, TVP, pasta, pet food**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of the rate of heat transfer and heat transfer coefficient of heat exchanger
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTL408 - FOOD PROCESS ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of the rate of heat transfer and heat transfer coefficient of heat exchanger

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

A **heat exchanger** is a process engineering device that facilitates the transfer of thermal energy between two or more fluids at different temperatures, without allowing them to mix physically. In the context of **Food Process Engineering**, heat exchangers are ubiquitous — they are used for **pasteurization** (e.g., milk at 72 °C for 15 s), **sterilization** (UHT processing of fruit juices at 140 °C for 4 s), **pre-heating** of edible oils before deodorization, **cooling** of cooked products, and **evaporation** pre-concentration of tomato paste.

The **rate of heat transfer (Q)** is the rate at which thermal energy is exchanged between the hot and cold streams, expressed in **Watts (W)** or **kJ/s**. The **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U)** is the proportionality constant that relates the total heat transferred to the **logarithmic mean temperature difference (LMTD)** and the effective heat transfer area (A), encapsulated in the foundational equation of heat exchanger design:

$$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{lm}$$

where $\Delta T_{lm}$ is the log mean temperature difference driving force.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The experiment typically employs a **Shell-and-Tube Heat Exchanger** or a **Concentric Tube (Double-Pipe) Heat Exchanger** in counter-current flow configuration. Students are expected to determine $Q$ (rate of heat transfer) and $U$ (overall heat transfer coefficient) by measuring the mass flow rates, inlet and outlet temperatures of both the hot and cold water streams.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Two Lanes of Traffic" Model**
> Imagine a two-lane highway where the **left lane** is filled with fast, energetic cars (hot fluid molecules) and the **right lane** has slow, lazy cars (cold fluid molecules). The cars can never change lanes (no mixing of fluids), but they can **exchange their energy through the wall of the highway divider** (the metal tube wall). The fast cars slow down, and the slow cars speed up, until they exit the highway. The "rate" at which energy is exchanged through the divider is our **Q**, and the "ease" with which the divider passes energy is the **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U)**. A thin, highly conductive copper divider has a *high* U; a thick, rusty iron divider has a *low* U.

### 1.3 Key Physical Quantities & Standard Metrics

- **Heat Capacity of Water (C_p):** **4.186 kJ/(kg·K)** — a key constant for both hot and cold water streams.
- **Standard Engineering Units:** $Q$ in **W (J/s)**, $U$ in **W/(m²·K)**, $A$ in **m²**, LMTD in **K** or **°C**.
- **Typical Range of U for Water-Water Systems:** **500 – 3000 W/(m²·K)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The experiment is governed by the **First Law of Thermodynamics** applied to a steady-state, control-volume system with negligible heat losses to the surroundings:

1. **Step 1 — Hot Stream Energy Balance:** The hot fluid enters at $T_{h,in}$, releases thermal energy, and exits at $T_{h,out}$ (where $T_{h,out} < T_{h,in}$).
2. **Step 2 — Cold Stream Energy Balance:** The cold fluid enters at $T_{c,in}$, absorbs thermal energy, and exits at $T_{c,out}$ (where $T_{c,out} > T_{c,in}$).
3. **Step 3 — Equate the Two Sides:** At steady state, the energy lost by the hot stream equals the energy gained by the cold stream (assuming no heat loss).
4. **Step 4 — Identify the Driving Force:** The temperature difference between the streams varies along the length of the exchanger, so we use the **Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)** instead of an arithmetic mean.
5. **Step 5 — Solve for U:** Rearranging the fundamental heat transfer equation $Q = U \cdot A \cdot LMTD$ gives $U = \frac{Q}{A \cdot LMTD}$.

### 2.2 Why Counter-Current Flow?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **counter-current flow**, the hot fluid enters at one end while the cold fluid enters at the opposite end. This configuration provides a **more uniform temperature difference** along the length of the exchanger, yielding a **higher mean driving force** and therefore a **higher rate of heat transfer** for the same area. Counter-current flow also allows the cold fluid outlet temperature to **exceed** the hot fluid outlet temperature — a feat impossible in co-current (parallel) flow.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $Q_h = \dot{m}_h \cdot C_{p,h} \cdot (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out})$ | Heat lost by hot stream | **W** |
| 2 | $Q_c = \dot{m}_c \cdot C_{p,c} \cdot (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$ | Heat gained by cold stream | **W** |
| 3 | $Q_{avg} = \dfrac{Q_h + Q_c}{2}$ | Average heat transfer rate | **W** |
| 4 | $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}$ | Temperature difference at end 1 (counter-current) | **K** |
| 5 | $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in}$ | Temperature difference at end 2 (counter-current) | **K** |
| 6 | $\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | Log Mean Temperature Difference | **K** |
| 7 | $U = \dfrac{Q_{avg}}{A \cdot \Delta T_{lm}}$ | Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient | **W/(m²·K)** |
| 8 | $A = \pi \cdot d \cdot L$ | Heat transfer area (inner tube, concentric) | **m²** |
| 9 | $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot \dot{V}$ | Mass flow rate (from volumetric flow) | **kg/s** |
| 10 | $C_{p,water} = 4.186 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$ | Specific heat of water | **kJ/(kg·K)** |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Food Engineering

- **Plate Heat Exchangers (PHE)** in dairy plants achieve $U \approx 3000$–$6000 \text{ W/(m²·K)}$ due to high turbulence and large surface area.
- **Scraped Surface Heat Exchangers (SSHE)** are used for viscous foods like ice cream mix, chocolate, and margarine.
- **Tubular Heat Exchangers** in juice concentration units operate at lower U values (~500–1500 W/(m²·K)) due to fouling by pectin and sugars.
- The experiment helps students understand **fouling resistance**, an industrial concern in food processing where deposits from proteins, salts, and sugars reduce the effective $U$ over time.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the LMTD for Counter-Current Flow

Consider a differential element of length $dx$ along the exchanger. The temperature of the hot fluid at position $x$ is $T_h(x)$ and the cold fluid is $T_c(x)$.

**Step 1 — Differential heat transfer across the element:**

$$dQ = U \cdot dA \cdot (T_h - T_c) = U \cdot (P \cdot dx) \cdot (T_h - T_c)$$

where $P$ is the perimeter of the tube and $dA = P \cdot dx$.

**Step 2 — Express dQ in terms of the hot fluid temperature change:**

The energy lost by the hot fluid is $dQ = -\dot{m}_h \cdot C_{p,h} \cdot dT_h$. Equating both expressions:

$$-\dot{m}_h \cdot C_{p,h} \cdot dT_h = U \cdot P \cdot (T_h - T_c) \cdot dx$$

Rearranging to separate variables:

$$\dfrac{dT_h}{T_h - T_c} = -\dfrac{U \cdot P}{\dot{m}_h \cdot C_{p,h}} \cdot dx$$

**Step 3 — Similarly for the cold fluid:**

$$dQ = \dot{m}_c \cdot C_{p,c} \cdot dT_c = U \cdot P \cdot (T_h - T_c) \cdot dx$$

$$\dfrac{dT_c}{T_h - T_c} = \dfrac{U \cdot P}{\dot{m}_c \cdot C_{p,c}} \cdot dx$$

**Step 4 — Subtract the two differential equations:**

Let $\theta = T_h - T_c$. Then $d\theta = dT_h - dT_c$.

$$\dfrac{d\theta}{\theta} = -U \cdot P \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{\dot{m}_h C_{p,h}} + \dfrac{1}{\dot{m}_c C_{p,c}}\right) dx$$

Integrating from $x = 0$ (where $\theta = \Delta T_1$) to $x = L$ (where $\theta = \Delta T_2$):

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1}\right) = -U \cdot P \cdot L \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{\dot{m}_h C_{p,h}} + \dfrac{1}{\dot{m}_c C_{p,c}}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Total heat transfer rate:**

$$Q = \dot{m}_h C_{p,h} (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}) = \dot{m}_c C_{p,c} (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$$

**Step 6 — Eliminate intermediate terms to obtain the final LMTD form:**

By algebraic manipulation of the integrated result, we arrive at the celebrated formula:

$$\boxed{\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}}$$

where $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}$ and $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in}$ for counter-current flow.

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Condition Used:** The LMTD formula requires $\Delta T_1 \neq \Delta T_2$. If $\Delta T_1 \approx \Delta T_2$ (within 1%), the arithmetic mean is used as a limiting approximation: $\Delta T_{lm} \approx (\Delta T_1 + \Delta T_2) / 2$.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Lab Question)

> **Given Data (from a standard lab trial):**
> - Hot water mass flow rate: $\dot{m}_h = 0.05 \text{ kg/s}$
> - Cold water mass flow rate: $\dot{m}_c = 0.04 \text{ kg/s}$
> - $T_{h,in} = 70 \text{ °C}$, $T_{h,out} = 55 \text{ °C}$
> - $T_{c,in} = 30 \text{ °C}$, $T_{c,out} = 42 \text{ °C}$
> - Heat transfer area: $A = 0.10 \text{ m}^2$
> - $C_{p,h} = C_{p,c} = 4.186 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$

**Step A — Heat lost by hot water:**

$$Q_h = 0.05 \times 4.186 \times (70 - 55) = 0.05 \times 4.186 \times 15 = 3.1395 \text{ kW} = 3139.5 \text{ W}$$

**Step B — Heat gained by cold water:**

$$Q_c = 0.04 \times 4.186 \times (42 - 30) = 0.04 \times 4.186 \times 12 = 2.0093 \text{ kW} = 2009.3 \text{ W}$$

**Step C — Average heat transfer rate:**

$$Q_{avg} = \dfrac{3139.5 + 2009.3}{2} = 2574.4 \text{ W}$$

**Step D — Compute $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$ for counter-current:**

$$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 70 - 42 = 28 \text{ °C}$$

$$\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 55 - 30 = 25 \text{ °C}$$

**Step E — Calculate LMTD:**

$$\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{28 - 25}{\ln(28/25)} = \dfrac{3}{\ln(1.12)} = \dfrac{3}{0.1133} = 26.47 \text{ °C}$$

**Step F — Calculate the Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U):**

$$U = \dfrac{Q_{avg}}{A \cdot \Delta T_{lm}} = \dfrac{2574.4}{0.10 \times 26.47} = \dfrac{2574.4}{2.647} = 972.5 \text{ W/(m²·K)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Validation Check:** A $U$ value of ~972 W/(m²·K) is realistic for a water-water concentric tube heat exchanger. If your calculated $U$ falls below **100 W/(m²·K)** or above **10,000 W/(m²·K)**, suspect a unit-conversion error (e.g., forgetting to convert kJ to J) or a transposed temperature difference.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Counter-Current Concentric Tube Heat Exchanger

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the functional flow of the experimental setup, including the hot and cold water circuits, thermometers, flow meters, and the data reduction path leading to the calculation of $Q$ and $U$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HW[Hot Water Tank\nPre-heated to 70 C] -->|m_dot_h| P1[Hot Water Pump]
    P1 -->|Discharge| FM1[Rotameter FM1\nmeasures V_dot_h]
    FM1 -->|Hot inlet| HX[Concentric Tube\nHeat Exchanger\ncounter-current flow]
    HX -->|Hot outlet\nT_h_out| ST1[Thermometer T1]
    ST1 -->|Discharge| DC1[Hot Water Drain\nand Recycle]
    CW[Cold Water Tank\nAt 30 C] -->|m_dot_c| P2[Cold Water Pump]
    P2 -->|Discharge| FM2[Rotameter FM2\nmeasures V_dot_c]
    FM2 -->|Cold inlet| HX
    HX -->|Cold outlet\nT_c_out| ST2[Thermometer T2]
    ST2 -->|Discharge| DC2[Cold Water Drain]
    ST1 -->|Reading| DAQ[Data Acquisition\nT_h_in, T_h_out]
    ST2 -->|Reading| DAQ
    FM1 -->|Reading| DAQ
    FM2 -->|Reading| DAQ
    DAQ -->|Inputs| CALC[Calculation Block]
    CALC --> QH[Q_h from hot stream\nm_h Cp_h dT_h]
    CALC --> QC[Q_c from cold stream\nm_c Cp_c dT_c]
    QH --> AVG[Q_avg average]
    QC --> AVG
    AVG --> LMTD[LMTD block\nlog mean dT]
    LMTD --> UCOEF[U equals Q_avg divided by\nA times LMTD]
    UCOEF --> RES[Final Results\nQ in Watts\nU in W per m squared K]
```

### 4.2 Component-Level Schematic Description

> [!NOTE]
> **Layout Description (for viva voce):**
> - **Hot Water Circuit:** Hot water reservoir → centrifugal pump → control valve → rotameter (measures volumetric flow) → enters the **inner tube** at one end of the heat exchanger → exits at the other end (now cooler) → returns to the reservoir.
> - **Cold Water Circuit:** Cold water reservoir → pump → rotameter → enters the **outer (annular) space** at the **opposite end** (counter-current) → exits warmer → returns to reservoir.
> - **Temperature Sensors:** Calibrated mercury-in-glass thermometers or digital RTD probes are placed at all **four** inlet/outlet points ($T_{h,in}, T_{h,out}, T_{c,in}, T_{c,out}$).
> - **Steady State:** Wait ~5 minutes after adjusting flow rates to allow the system to reach thermal steady state (no change in outlet temperatures with time).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define the Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U) of a heat exchanger and state its SI units.*

**Model Answer:**
The Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient ($U$) is defined as the **rate of heat transfer per unit area per unit log mean temperature difference** between the hot and cold fluids. It is a measure of the overall heat transfer capability of the heat exchanger, encompassing the conductive and convective resistances of the wall and the two fluid boundary layers. Its SI unit is **W/(m²·K)**. Mathematically, $U = \dfrac{Q}{A \cdot \Delta T_{lm}}$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sessional]**
*What is the Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)? Why is it preferred over the arithmetic mean temperature difference?*

**Model Answer:**
The LMTD is the **logarithmic mean** of the temperature differences at the two ends of the heat exchanger, given by $\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$. It is preferred over the arithmetic mean because the **temperature difference between the hot and cold streams is not linear** with respect to the exchanger length (it follows an exponential decay), and the LMTD accurately represents the **true driving force** for heat transfer over the entire length of the exchanger.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Counter-Current Calculation**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the working principle of a **counter-current double-pipe heat exchanger**. Why is counter-current flow preferred over co-current flow in most industrial applications? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In an experimental determination of the heat transfer coefficient, the following data were obtained from a concentric tube heat exchanger operating in counter-current flow:

- Hot water inlet temperature: 75 °C
- Hot water outlet temperature: 50 °C
- Cold water inlet temperature: 25 °C
- Cold water outlet temperature: 45 °C
- Mass flow rate of hot water: 0.06 kg/s
- Mass flow rate of cold water: 0.05 kg/s
- Heat transfer area: 0.12 m²
- Take $C_p$ for water = 4.186 kJ/(kg·K)

Calculate: **(i)** the rate of heat transfer $Q$, **(ii)** the LMTD, and **(iii)** the overall heat transfer coefficient $U$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Working Principle (7 marks):**

In a counter-current double-pipe heat exchanger, two concentric tubes are used. The **hot fluid** flows through the **inner tube**, while the **cold fluid** flows through the **annular space** between the inner and outer tubes. The two fluids flow in **opposite directions**, which maintains a relatively **uniform temperature gradient** along the length of the exchanger.

**Sketch Description:** Draw two concentric horizontal tubes. Label the inner tube "Hot fluid in → out" with arrows pointing right, and the outer annular space "Cold fluid out ← in" with arrows pointing left (opposite direction). Mark all four temperature points.

**Why counter-current is preferred:**
1. **Higher mean temperature driving force** → higher heat transfer rate for the same area.
2. The cold fluid exit temperature can **exceed** the hot fluid exit temperature (a thermodynamic impossibility in co-current flow).
3. **More uniform wall temperature** reduces thermal stress and the risk of localized fouling.
4. **Higher thermal efficiency** — closer approach temperatures are achievable.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Rate of heat transfer $Q$:**

$$Q_h = 0.06 \times 4.186 \times (75 - 50) = 0.06 \times 4.186 \times 25 = 6.279 \text{ kW} = 6279 \text{ W}$$

$$Q_c = 0.05 \times 4.186 \times (45 - 25) = 0.05 \times 4.186 \times 20 = 4.186 \text{ kW} = 4186 \text{ W}$$

$$Q_{avg} = \dfrac{6279 + 4186}{2} = 5232.5 \text{ W}$$

**(ii) LMTD:**

$$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 75 - 45 = 30 \text{ °C}$$

$$\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 50 - 25 = 25 \text{ °C}$$

$$\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{30 - 25}{\ln(30/25)} = \dfrac{5}{\ln(1.2)} = \dfrac{5}{0.1823} = 27.42 \text{ °C}$$

**(iii) Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient $U$:**

$$U = \dfrac{Q_{avg}}{A \cdot \Delta T_{lm}} = \dfrac{5232.5}{0.12 \times 27.42} = \dfrac{5232.5}{3.2904} = 1590.2 \text{ W/(m²·K)}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice (Co-Current & Fouling)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Differentiate between **co-current (parallel)** and **counter-current** flow in heat exchangers. Show, using temperature profiles, why counter-current flow yields a higher LMTD. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A shell-and-tube heat exchanger used in a **dairy pasteurization plant** has a heat transfer area of 2.5 m². The hot water (heating medium) enters at 90 °C and leaves at 70 °C. The milk stream enters at 5 °C and leaves at 72 °C. The mass flow rate of milk is 0.5 kg/s with $C_{p,milk} = 3.93 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$. If the overall heat transfer coefficient is 1200 W/(m²·K), determine the LMTD using the counter-current assumption and verify whether the LMTD method is applicable. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Co-current vs Counter-current (7 marks):**

| Feature | Co-current (Parallel) | Counter-current |
|---------|----------------------|-----------------|
| Flow direction | Same direction | Opposite directions |
| Temperature profile | Temperature difference decreases rapidly from inlet | Temperature difference is more uniform |
| Cold fluid exit $T$ | Always less than hot fluid exit $T$ | Can exceed hot fluid exit $T$ |
| LMTD | Lower | Higher |
| Heat transfer efficiency | Lower | Higher |

**Temperature profile reasoning (sketch description):**
- In **co-current flow**, both fluids enter at the same end. The hot fluid cools rapidly and the cold fluid heats rapidly. The curves converge quickly, and the temperature difference becomes very small at the outlet end, leading to a **low average driving force**.
- In **counter-current flow**, the hot fluid entering at one end always meets the coldest incoming cold fluid at the other end, and vice versa. This maintains a **nearly constant temperature difference** along the entire length, yielding a **higher LMTD** for the same inlet/outlet conditions.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Heat transfer rate from milk side (energy balance):**

$$Q = \dot{m}_{milk} \cdot C_{p,milk} \cdot (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}) = 0.5 \times 3.93 \times (72 - 5) = 0.5 \times 3.93 \times 67 = 131.66 \text{ kW} = 131{,}660 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2 — LMTD for counter-current:**

$$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 90 - 72 = 18 \text{ °C}$$

$$\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 70 - 5 = 65 \text{ °C}$$

$$\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{65 - 18}{\ln(65/18)} = \dfrac{47}{\ln(3.611)} = \dfrac{47}{1.2844} = 36.59 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 3 — Check LMTD applicability (use of $P$ and $R$ parameters for shell-and-tube):**

> [!WARNING]
> For shell-and-tube exchangers, the simple LMTD must be corrected by a **Correction Factor (F)**. The LMTD method is directly applicable only when the temperature profiles do not cross. Here, since $T_{c,out} = 72 \text{ °C} > T_{h,out} = 70 \text{ °C}$, there is a **temperature crossover**, which is a **physical impossibility in a single-shell-pass exchanger**. This indicates that either (i) a **multi-pass** configuration is used, or (ii) the data should be re-evaluated. The basic LMTD is therefore an **approximation** for this case.

**Step 4 — Effective U verification (sanity check):**

If the given $U = 1200 \text{ W/(m²·K)}$ is for a clean surface, the actual $U$ in operation (with milk fouling) is lower. The LMTD calculated gives $\Delta T_{lm} = 36.59 \text{ °C}$, and for the given $U$ and $A$:

$$Q_{design} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{lm} = 1200 \times 2.5 \times 36.59 = 109{,}770 \text{ W} \approx 109.8 \text{ kW}$$

This is less than the required $Q = 131.66 \text{ kW}$, confirming the **fouling resistance** in dairy plants significantly reduces the effective U.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks (KTU Valuation Patterns):**
> 1. **Forgetting to average $Q_h$ and $Q_c$:** Always use $Q_{avg} = (Q_h + Q_c) / 2$ because of inevitable heat losses to surroundings. Using only $Q_h$ or $Q_c$ incurs a 2-mark penalty.
> 2. **Mixing up $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$ for counter-current:** A very common error is to compute $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}$ (which is for co-current). The counter-current definition is $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}$.
> 3. **Unit mismatch:** Failing to convert kJ/s to W (multiply by 1000) or L/min to m³/s (multiply by $1.667 \times 10^{-5}$). A 2-mark deduction is typical.
> 4. **Not stating the steady-state assumption:** Examiners award 1 mark for explicitly stating "at steady state, heat loss to surroundings is neglected, hence $Q_h = Q_c$."
> 5. **Reporting $U$ without units:** Always write "W/(m²·K)" — omitting units is a 1-mark deduction.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🔑 **Core Equation:** $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{lm}$ — the *master* equation for all heat exchanger problems.
- 🔑 **LMTD Formula:** $\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ — always verify $\Delta T_1 \neq \Delta T_2$.
- 🔑 **Counter-current definition:** $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}$, $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in}$.
- 🔑 **Average heat transfer:** $Q_{avg} = \dfrac{Q_h + Q_c}{2}$ — accounts for heat loss.
- 🔑 **C_p of water = 4.186 kJ/(kg·K)** — the most-used constant in the experiment.
- 🔑 **Typical U values:** Water-to-water ~500–3000 W/(m²·K); plate exchangers ~3000–6000 W/(m²·K).
- 🔑 **Steady state** must be achieved before recording readings (wait ~5 min after flow adjustment).
- 🔑 **Assumption:** No phase change, negligible heat loss to surroundings, constant $C_p$, no fouling.
- 🔑 **Fouling in food industry:** Proteins, salts, and sugars deposit on surfaces, increasing thermal resistance and **decreasing effective U** over operational time.
- 🔑 **Viva question:** "Why use water as both hot and cold fluid in the lab?" — Answer: Water has a known, high, and constant $C_p$, simplifying calculations and serving as a baseline before testing with actual food fluids.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of rate of filtration for constant pressure filtration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTL408 - FOOD PROCESS ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of rate of filtration for constant pressure filtration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constant Pressure Filtration** is a mode of filtration operation in which the **pressure drop (ΔP)** across the filter medium and the accumulating filter cake is maintained **constant** throughout the entire cycle, while the **volumetric rate of filtrate collection (dV/dt) progressively decreases** as the resistance offered by the growing filter cake increases with time.

In the context of **PCFTL408 — Food Process Engineering Lab**, the experiment is performed on a **leaf filter / plate-and-frame filter press / Büchner funnel assembly** using a food slurry (commonly **calcium carbonate suspension in water, fruit juice, sugar syrup, or starch slurry**) to experimentally determine the **rate of filtration equation constants**, namely the **specific cake resistance (α)** and the **filter medium resistance (Rₘ)**.

The governing physical relationship is **Darcy's Law applied to flow through a compressible / incompressible cake**, integrated under the constraint of constant ΔP.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Coffee Mug Story"

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine you are pouring water through a coffee mug fitted with ground coffee.**

- The **first drop** flows out **very fast** because the resistance is only from the paper filter (Rₘ — *Filter Medium Resistance*).
- As the **coffee bed (cake) builds up**, the flow becomes **progressively slower**, even though the water pressure (ΔP) at the top remains **constant**.
- This is exactly what happens in a food filtration experiment: the **driving force (ΔP) is fixed**, but the **resistance keeps growing** as the cake thickens.
- The **plot of (time ÷ volume) vs volume** is always a **straight line** at constant pressure — this is the **fingerprint** of constant pressure filtration.

> This simple phenomenon underlies the **separation of fruit juices, sugar refining, beer clarification, and edible oil processing** in the food industry.

---

## 1.3 Key Engineering Quantities (Highlighted)

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Unit |
|----------|--------|---------------|
| Pressure drop | **ΔP** | **Pa (or atm, bar)** |
| Viscosity of filtrate | **μ** | **Pa·s (or cP)** |
| Filtrate volume | **V** | **m³ (or L)** |
| Filtration area | **A** | **m²** |
| Cake resistance | **R_c** | **m⁻¹** |
| Medium resistance | **Rₘ** | **m⁻¹** |
| Specific cake resistance | **α** | **m/kg** |
| Solids concentration in feed | **c** | **kg/m³** |
| Filtration rate | **dV/dt** | **m³/s** |

---

## 1.4 Scope of the Experiment (KTU 2024 Module 2)

The student is expected to:
1. Set up a **vacuum / pressure filtration assembly** at a **fixed ΔP**.
2. Record **time (t) vs cumulative filtrate volume (V)** readings.
3. Plot the **filtration curve** (V vs t) and the **modified curve (t/V vs V)**.
4. Determine **α and Rₘ** from the slope and intercept.
5. Comment on **filter cake compressibility** if data at two pressures are taken.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Principle — Darcy's Law for Cake Filtration

The flow of filtrate through the combined resistance of the **filter medium** and the **filter cake** is governed by a modified form of **Darcy's Law**:

$$\frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot (R_c + R_m)}$$

### 2.1.1 Decomposing the Total Resistance

- The **medium resistance (Rₘ)** is a **constant** for a given filter cloth and is independent of the volume of filtrate.
- The **cake resistance (R_c)** is **proportional to the mass of solids deposited**, which in turn is proportional to the **volume of filtrate V** collected.

$$R_c = \alpha \cdot \nu \cdot V = \alpha \cdot \frac{c \cdot V}{A}$$

> Where **ν = c/A** is the *volume of solids deposited per unit filtrate volume per unit area*, and **α** is the **specific cake resistance (m/kg)** — a property of the slurry and cake structure.

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Build-Up

1. **Step 1 — Start with Darcy's Law:** Instantaneous rate depends on the pressure drop and the *total* resistance.
2. **Step 2 — Replace R_c:** Substitute R_c = α·c·V/A (assuming **incompressible cake**).
3. **Step 3 — Isolate the differential:** Separate variables V and t.
4. **Step 4 — Integrate:** Apply limits V = 0 at t = 0 → V = V at t = t.
5. **Step 5 — Arrive at the parabolic law:** The relationship between V and t becomes **quadratic**.
6. **Step 6 — Linearize:** Rearrange into the **t/V vs V linear form** for graphical evaluation.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following six (6) equations are the *complete* mathematical toolkit required for KTU 2024 ESE / Internal assessment on this topic.**

| # | Equation | Meaning of Terms | Valid For |
|---|----------|------------------|-----------|
| 1 | $\dfrac{dV}{dt} = \dfrac{A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot (R_c + R_m)}$ | Darcy's law — instantaneous rate | General filtration |
| 2 | $R_c = \alpha \cdot \dfrac{c}{A} \cdot V$ | Cake resistance (incompressible cake) | Constant pressure |
| 3 | $\dfrac{dV}{dt} = \dfrac{A^2 \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot (\alpha \cdot c \cdot V + A \cdot R_m)}$ | Substituted form (constant ΔP) | Constant pressure |
| 4 | $t = \dfrac{\mu \cdot \alpha \cdot c}{2 \cdot A^2 \cdot \Delta P} \cdot V^2 + \dfrac{\mu \cdot R_m}{A \cdot \Delta P} \cdot V$ | Integrated form (parabolic law) | Constant pressure |
| 5 | $\dfrac{t}{V} = \underbrace{\dfrac{\mu \cdot \alpha \cdot c}{2 \cdot A^2 \cdot \Delta P}}_{\text{slope } m} \cdot V + \underbrace{\dfrac{\mu \cdot R_m}{A \cdot \Delta P}}_{\text{intercept } b}$ | Linearized working equation | Constant pressure |
| 6 | $V^2 + 2 \cdot \dfrac{A \cdot R_m}{\alpha \cdot c} \cdot V = \dfrac{2 \cdot A^2 \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot \alpha \cdot c} \cdot t$ | Alternative quadratic form | Constant pressure |

### 2.3.1 Reverse Formulas (Used for Graphical Back-Calculation)

$$\alpha = \frac{2 \cdot A^2 \cdot \Delta P \cdot m}{\mu \cdot c} \quad\quad\text{(from slope m of } t/V \text{ vs } V\text{)}$$

$$R_m = \frac{A \cdot \Delta P \cdot b}{\mu} \quad\quad\text{(from intercept b of } t/V \text{ vs } V\text{)}$$

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application |
|----------|-------------|
| **Sugar refining** | Clarification of cane juice using rotary vacuum filters |
| **Edible oil** | Removal of spent bleaching earth from refined oil |
| **Beverage industry** | Polishing filtration of fruit juices, beer, wine |
| **Dairy** | Whey protein separation, milk clarification |
| **Starch & gluten** | Dewatering of starch slurries |

> The same **t/V vs V linear plot** is used by **plant engineers daily** to size new filter presses, predict cycle times, and optimize backwash schedules.

---

## 2.5 The Concept of Cake Compressibility (Brief Note)

For **incompressible cakes** (e.g., rigid crystalline precipitates), α is **independent of ΔP**. For **compressible cakes** (most food slurries), α varies with pressure:

$$\alpha = \alpha_0 \cdot (\Delta P)^s$$

Where **s** is the **compressibility index** (0 ≤ s < 1). Determined experimentally by repeating the experiment at **two different ΔP values**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Constant Pressure Filtration Equation

### Step 1 — Starting Point: Darcy's Law

The instantaneous volumetric flow rate of filtrate through the filter medium plus cake combination:

$$\frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot (R_c + R_m)}$$

**Logic:** Darcy's law says the rate is *directly proportional* to the driving force (A·ΔP) and *inversely proportional* to the *viscous resistance* (μ·R_total). We retain dV/dt on the LHS because at constant ΔP, the rate is a *function of V only*.

---

### Step 2 — Replace R_c with the Cake Build-Up Expression

For an incompressible cake with a uniform slurry of solids concentration c (kg of dry solids per m³ of filtrate):

$$R_c = \alpha \cdot \frac{c \cdot V}{A}$$

**Logic:** The mass of solids deposited on the filter is (c·V). Dividing by the filtration area A gives the *mass per unit area* of cake. Multiplying by α converts this to a *resistance per unit area*. As more filtrate is collected, the cake grows linearly with V.

---

### Step 3 — Substitute into Darcy's Equation

$$\frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot \left(\alpha \cdot \dfrac{c \cdot V}{A} + R_m\right)}$$

Simplify by multiplying numerator and denominator by A:

$$\frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{A^2 \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot (\alpha \cdot c \cdot V + A \cdot R_m)}$$

**Logic:** The expression now isolates the V-dependence in the denominator — the variable we will integrate over.

---

### Step 4 — Separate Variables and Integrate

$$(\alpha \cdot c \cdot V + A \cdot R_m) \cdot dV = \frac{A^2 \cdot \Delta P}{\mu} \cdot dt$$

Integrate both sides from V = 0 at t = 0 to V = V at t = t:

$$\int_{0}^{V} (\alpha \cdot c \cdot V + A \cdot R_m) \, dV = \frac{A^2 \cdot \Delta P}{\mu} \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

Performing the integration term-by-term:

$$\frac{\alpha \cdot c \cdot V^2}{2} + A \cdot R_m \cdot V = \frac{A^2 \cdot \Delta P}{\mu} \cdot t$$

**Logic:** The integral of V dV is V²/2, and the integral of a constant is itself. Both sides are now algebraic rather than differential.

---

### Step 5 — Solve for t Explicitly

$$t = \frac{\mu \cdot \alpha \cdot c}{2 \cdot A^2 \cdot \Delta P} \cdot V^2 + \frac{\mu \cdot R_m}{A \cdot \Delta P} \cdot V$$

**Logic:** This is the famous *parabolic law of constant-pressure filtration* — the time required to collect volume V grows quadratically because the cake resistance grows linearly.

---

### Step 6 — Linearize to the (t/V) vs V Form

Divide both sides of the integrated equation by V:

$$\frac{t}{V} = \frac{\mu \cdot \alpha \cdot c}{2 \cdot A^2 \cdot \Delta P} \cdot V + \frac{\mu \cdot R_m}{A \cdot \Delta P}$$

**Logic:** This is a straight line of the form Y = m·X + b, where:
- **Y = t/V** (units: s/m³)
- **X = V** (units: m³)
- **m = slope** = (μ·α·c) / (2·A²·ΔP)
- **b = intercept** = (μ·Rₘ) / (A·ΔP)

The linearization is the **most important practical step**, because it allows graphical determination of α and Rₘ from a single experiment.

---

## 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Example (Typical KTU Lab Data)

### Given Experimental Data (constant ΔP = 0.5 atm, A = 50 cm²)

| Sl. No. | Time t (s) | Volume V (mL) | t/V (s/mL) |
|---------|------------|---------------|------------|
| 1 | 12 | 50 | 0.240 |
| 2 | 30 | 100 | 0.300 |
| 3 | 54 | 150 | 0.360 |
| 4 | 84 | 200 | 0.420 |
| 5 | 120 | 250 | 0.480 |
| 6 | 162 | 300 | 0.540 |

### Step A — Compute t/V for Each Reading

$$(t/V)_1 = \frac{12}{50} = 0.240 \;\text{s/mL}$$

$$(t/V)_2 = \frac{30}{100} = 0.300 \;\text{s/mL}$$

$$(t/V)_3 = \frac{54}{150} = 0.360 \;\text{s/mL}$$

$$(t/V)_4 = \frac{84}{200} = 0.420 \;\text{s/mL}$$

$$(t/V)_5 = \frac{120}{250} = 0.480 \;\text{s/mL}$$

$$(t/V)_6 = \frac{162}{300} = 0.540 \;\text{s/mL}$$

### Step B — Determine Slope m and Intercept b (Least Squares)

Using the linear equation $Y = m \cdot X + b$ with X = V, Y = t/V:

$$m = \frac{n\sum XY - \sum X \sum Y}{n\sum X^2 - (\sum X)^2}$$

$$b = \frac{\sum Y - m \sum X}{n}$$

Where n = 6. Computation yields (rounded):

$$m = 1.20 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{s/mL}^2 \quad\quad b = 0.180 \;\text{s/mL}$$

### Step C — Convert to SI and Back-Calculate α and Rₘ

**Given:** ΔP = 0.5 atm = 0.5 × 101325 = 50662.5 Pa; A = 50 cm² = 50 × 10⁻⁴ = 5 × 10⁻³ m²; μ = 1.0 cP = 1.0 × 10⁻³ Pa·s; c = 10 kg/m³; m = 1.20 × 10⁻³ s/mL² = 1.20 × 10⁻³ × 10⁶ s/m⁶ = 1200 s/m⁶; b = 0.180 s/mL = 0.180 × 10³ s/m³ = 180 s/m³.

**Specific cake resistance α:**

$$\alpha = \frac{2 \cdot A^2 \cdot \Delta P \cdot m}{\mu \cdot c} = \frac{2 \cdot (5 \times 10^{-3})^2 \cdot 50662.5 \cdot 1200}{1.0 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 10}$$

$$\alpha = \frac{2 \cdot 25 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 50662.5 \cdot 1200}{0.01} = \frac{2 \cdot 25 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 6.0795 \times 10^{7}}{0.01}$$

$$\alpha = \frac{2 \cdot 25 \cdot 6.0795 \times 10^{1}}{0.01} = \frac{3039.75}{0.01} = 3.04 \times 10^{5}\;\text{m/kg}$$

**Filter medium resistance Rₘ:**

$$R_m = \frac{A \cdot \Delta P \cdot b}{\mu} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 50662.5 \cdot 180}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$R_m = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 9.11925 \times 10^{6}}{0.001} = 4.56 \times 10^{7}\;\text{m}^{-1}$$

### Step D — Final Results Summary

> **The rate of filtration equation for this experiment is:**

$$\boxed{\;\frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-3})^2 \cdot 50662.5}{1.0 \times 10^{-3} \cdot \left[3.04 \times 10^{5} \cdot 10 \cdot V + 5 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 4.56 \times 10^{7}\right]}\;}$$

$$\frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{1266.56}{3040 \cdot V + 228000}\;\text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

> This is the **working equation** to compute the **instantaneous filtration rate** for any filtrate volume V at the specified ΔP.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Constant Pressure Filtration Apparatus

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    airComp["AIR COMPRESSOR"] -->|"P_in = constant"| presReg["PRESSURE REGULATOR"]
    presReg -->|"Fixed ΔP"| slurryTank["STIRRED SLURRY TANK"]
    slurryTank -->|"Slurry feed line with valve V1"| filterUnit["FILTER UNIT Plate and Frame OR Leaf Filter"]
    filterUnit -->|"Cake builds up on cloth"| cakeLayer["FILTER CAKE Accumulating Solids"]
    filterUnit -->|"Clear filtrate exits"| filtrateMeas["FILTRATE COLLECTION GRADUATED CYLINDER"]
    filtrateMeas -->|"V vs t readings"| dataLog["DATA LOGGING Stopwatch and Balance"]
    dataLog -->|"Calculate t by V"| plotGraph["PLOT t by V vs V Straight Line"]
    plotGraph -->|"Slope m and Intercept b"| calcEngine["CALCULATE alpha and Rm"]
    cakeLayer -->|"Discharged after cycle"| cakeDump["CAKE DISCHARGE"]
```

## 4.2 Process Flow — Experimental Procedure (Step-by-Step)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA(["START EXPERIMENT"]) --> step1["Step 1: Prepare slurry of known solids concentration c kg per m3"]
    step1 --> step2["Step 2: Measure filter area A and record cloth type"]
    step2 --> step3["Step 3: Set pressure regulator to fixed ΔP value"]
    step3 --> step4["Step 4: Open feed valve V1 and start stopwatch simultaneously"]
    step4 --> step5["Step 5: Record cumulative filtrate volume V at fixed time intervals"]
    step5 --> step6["Step 6: Continue until flow becomes negligibly small"]
    step6 --> step7["Step 7: Tabulate t, V and compute t by V"]
    step7 --> step8["Step 8: Plot t by V versus V and obtain slope m and intercept b"]
    step8 --> step9["Step 9: Substitute m, b into reverse formulas to obtain alpha and Rm"]
    step9 --> step10["Step 10: Write rate equation dV by dt as function of V"]
    step10 --> step11["Step 11: Repeat at a second ΔP to find compressibility index s"]
    step11 --> endA(["END AND COMPARE RESULTS"])
```

## 4.3 Logical Interaction Map — Variables in the Rate Equation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    press["DELTA P Constant in this experiment"] --> driver["DRIVING FORCE A TIMES DELTA P"]
    area["FILTER AREA A"] --> driver
    driver --> numerator["NUMERATOR OF RATE EQUATION"]
    visc["FILTRATE VISCOSITY mu"] --> denom["DENOMINATOR OF RATE EQUATION"]
    alpha["SPECIFIC CAKE RESISTANCE alpha"] --> cakeRes["CAKE RESISTANCE alpha c V by A"]
    conc["SOLIDS CONCENTRATION c"] --> cakeRes
    vol["FILTRATE VOLUME V Variable"] --> cakeRes
    cakeRes --> denom
    rmed["MEDIUM RESISTANCE Rm"] --> denom
    numerator --> rateEq["INSTANTANEOUS RATE dV by dt"]
    denom --> rateEq
    rateEq --> result["RATE DECREASES WITH INCREASING V"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the diagram:** The numerator of the rate equation contains only the *fixed operating parameters* (ΔP, A). The denominator contains the *viscous resistance* and the *cake resistance* — both of which grow as V grows. This is why **dV/dt is a continuously decreasing function of V** at constant ΔP.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2]

> **Define the following terms with their SI units:**
> **(a) Specific cake resistance (α)**  **(b) Filter medium resistance (Rₘ)**  **(c) Compressibility index (s)**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **(a) Specific cake resistance (α):** It is defined as the resistance offered by a unit mass of filter cake deposited on the filter medium. It is a characteristic property of the slurry-cake system, independent of the cake thickness. **SI Unit: m/kg.**

- **(b) Filter medium resistance (Rₘ):** It is the resistance offered by the filter cloth (or septum) alone to the flow of filtrate, before any cake has formed. It is a constant for a given filter medium and slurry system. **SI Unit: m⁻¹.**

- **(c) Compressibility index (s):** It is an empirical exponent that quantifies how strongly the specific cake resistance α depends on the pressure drop ΔP, through the relation α = α₀·(ΔP)ˢ. For incompressible cakes, s = 0; for highly compressible cakes, s → 1. **SI Unit: dimensionless.**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2]

> **State the integrated form of the constant pressure filtration equation and explain the physical significance of the slope and intercept of the t/V versus V plot.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The integrated form of the constant pressure filtration equation is:

$$\frac{t}{V} = \frac{\mu \cdot \alpha \cdot c}{2 \cdot A^2 \cdot \Delta P} \cdot V + \frac{\mu \cdot R_m}{A \cdot \Delta P}$$

This is a **straight line** in (t/V) versus V coordinates, where:
- **Slope m = (μ·α·c) / (2·A²·ΔP)** — physically represents the *rate at which filtration slows down* as more cake is deposited; the steeper the slope, the higher the cake resistance or solids concentration.
- **Intercept b = (μ·Rₘ) / (A·ΔP)** — physically represents the *reciprocal of the initial filtration rate* (when V → 0, dV/dt → A·ΔP/(μ·Rₘ)); the larger the intercept, the more the filter cloth itself resists the flow.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **INTERNAL CHOICE INSTRUCTION:** Attempt **any ONE** of the following two question groups. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Sample 2024 Scheme]

> **(a)** Starting from **Darcy's law**, derive the **integrated form of the constant pressure filtration equation** in terms of the specific cake resistance (α) and the filter medium resistance (Rₘ). Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A laboratory leaf filter with filtration area **0.05 m²** is used to filter a **CaCO₃ slurry** at a constant pressure drop of **ΔP = 0.4 atm**. The viscosity of the filtrate is **1.0 cP** and the solids concentration is **20 kg/m³**. The slope and intercept of the t/V vs V plot are found to be **m = 800 s/m⁶** and **b = 200 s/m³** respectively. Calculate the **specific cake resistance (α)** and the **filter medium resistance (Rₘ)**. Also determine the **instantaneous filtration rate when V = 0.8 m³**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

1. State Darcy's law for cake filtration: $\dfrac{dV}{dt} = \dfrac{A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu(R_c + R_m)}$ — **(1 mark)**
2. Express R_c in terms of α, c, V, A: $R_c = \alpha c V / A$ — **(1 mark)**
3. Substitute and rearrange to: $\dfrac{dV}{dt} = \dfrac{A^2 \cdot \Delta P}{\mu(\alpha c V + A R_m)}$ — **(1 mark)**
4. Separate variables and integrate: $\int_0^V (\alpha c V + A R_m) dV = \dfrac{A^2 \Delta P}{\mu} \int_0^t dt$ — **(2 marks)**
5. Solve: $\dfrac{\alpha c V^2}{2} + A R_m V = \dfrac{A^2 \Delta P t}{\mu}$ — **(1 mark)**
6. Linearize to $\dfrac{t}{V} = \dfrac{\mu \alpha c}{2 A^2 \Delta P} V + \dfrac{\mu R_m}{A \Delta P}$ and **list assumptions**: incompressible cake, uniform slurry, constant ΔP, negligible medium clogging. — **(1 mark)**

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given data:**
- A = 0.05 m²
- ΔP = 0.4 atm = 0.4 × 101325 = 40530 Pa
- μ = 1.0 cP = 1.0 × 10⁻³ Pa·s
- c = 20 kg/m³
- m = 800 s/m⁶
- b = 200 s/m³

**Step 1 — Calculate α:**

$$\alpha = \frac{2 \cdot A^2 \cdot \Delta P \cdot m}{\mu \cdot c} = \frac{2 \cdot (0.05)^2 \cdot 40530 \cdot 800}{1.0 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 20}$$

$$\alpha = \frac{2 \cdot 0.0025 \cdot 40530 \cdot 800}{0.02} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.0025 \cdot 3.2424 \times 10^{7}}{0.02}$$

$$\alpha = \frac{1.6212 \times 10^{5}}{0.02} = 8.106 \times 10^{6}\;\text{m/kg}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Rₘ:**

$$R_m = \frac{A \cdot \Delta P \cdot b}{\mu} = \frac{0.05 \cdot 40530 \cdot 200}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$R_m = \frac{0.05 \cdot 8.106 \times 10^{6}}{0.001} = 4.053 \times 10^{8}\;\text{m}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Instantaneous rate at V = 0.8 m³:**

$$\frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{A^2 \cdot \Delta P}{\mu(\alpha c V + A R_m)}$$

$$= \frac{(0.05)^2 \cdot 40530}{1.0 \times 10^{-3} \cdot (8.106 \times 10^{6} \cdot 20 \cdot 0.8 + 0.05 \cdot 4.053 \times 10^{8})}$$

Denominator term 1: $8.106 \times 10^{6} \times 20 \times 0.8 = 1.297 \times 10^{8}$

Denominator term 2: $0.05 \times 4.053 \times 10^{8} = 2.027 \times 10^{7}$

Sum: $1.297 \times 10^{8} + 0.2027 \times 10^{8} = 1.499 \times 10^{8}$

Numerator: $(0.05)^2 \cdot 40530 = 0.0025 \cdot 40530 = 101.325$

$$\frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{101.325}{1.0 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 1.499 \times 10^{8}} = \frac{101.325}{1.499 \times 10^{5}}$$

$$\boxed{\;\frac{dV}{dt} \approx 6.76 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{m}^3/\text{s} = 0.676\;\text{L/s}\;}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Sample 2024 Scheme]

> **(a)** With the help of a **neatly labelled schematic**, describe the **laboratory procedure** for the determination of the rate of filtration at constant pressure using a **Büchner funnel / vacuum leaf filter**. List the **observations and calculations** required. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the effect of the following on the **specific cake resistance (α)** and the **filter medium resistance (Rₘ)** in a food filtration process: **(i) Increase in pressure drop (ΔP)** for a compressible cake; **(ii) Increase in solids concentration (c)** of the feed slurry; **(iii) Pre-coating the filter cloth with a layer of filter aid. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**Apparatus:** Büchner funnel, vacuum flask, vacuum pump, pressure regulator, stopwatch, measuring cylinder, filter paper/cloth, CaCO₃ slurry (or fruit juice/soy milk).

**Procedure:**

1. Assemble the filtration unit — connect the **Büchner funnel** to the **vacuum flask** with a **pressure gauge** in the line, and the vacuum pump to the flask via a regulator. — **(1 mark)**
2. Wet the **filter paper** with a small amount of water to seal it against the funnel base, then measure the **filtration area (A)**. — **(0.5 marks)**
3. Prepare a **slurry of known concentration c** (e.g., 10 kg CaCO₃ per m³ water) and pour it into the **feed tank**. — **(0.5 marks)**
4. Set the regulator to a **fixed ΔP** (e.g., 0.5 atm vacuum) — record the reading of the pressure gauge. — **(0.5 marks)**
5. Open the feed valve and **simultaneously start the stopwatch**. — **(0.5 marks)**
6. Record the **cumulative filtrate volume V** at fixed time intervals (e.g., every 10 s or 30 s) until the flow becomes negligibly small. — **(1 mark)**
7. **Tabulate** t and V; compute t/V for each reading. — **(0.5 marks)**
8. **Plot** t/V vs V; obtain the **slope (m)** and **intercept (b)** of the best-fit straight line. — **(1 mark)**
9. **Calculate** α = 2·A²·ΔP·m/(μ·c) and Rₘ = A·ΔP·b/μ. — **(1 mark)**
10. **Repeat** the experiment at a *second ΔP* to determine the **compressibility index s** from the log–log plot of α vs ΔP. — **(0.5 marks)**

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Increase in ΔP for a compressible cake:** (2.5 marks)
- α **increases** according to α = α₀·(ΔP)ˢ, with **s > 0** (typically 0.3–0.7 for biological/food cakes such as fruit pulp, yeast, or starch).
- Rₘ remains **essentially unchanged** since it is a property of the cloth and not the operating ΔP (assuming no cloth compression).
- The instantaneous rate initially *appears* higher due to larger ΔP, but the rate *decreases faster* because of the rapidly thickening, more compact cake. Net effect: **diminishing returns** at very high ΔP.

**(ii) Increase in solids concentration c:** (2.5 marks)
- α is *not* directly affected (it is a *specific* property of the cake solids).
- However, the *total* cake resistance R_c = α·c·V/A increases in **direct proportion to c** — so the cake builds up faster.
- Rₘ remains **unchanged**.
- The slope m of the t/V vs V line *increases linearly with c*; the intercept b remains the same.
- Practical implication: a higher c slurry filters more slowly and requires **larger filter area** for the same throughput.

**(iii) Pre-coating with filter aid (e.g., diatomaceous earth, perlite):** (2 marks)
- The pre-coat forms a **porous, incompressible layer** that *physically separates* the food solids from the filter cloth.
- This **protects the cloth** from blinding and **increases the effective Rₘ** (paradoxically, the intercept b *increases*) — but more importantly it **prevents Rₘ from increasing over time** as the cloth gets clogged.
- Pre-coat filtration is **standard practice in beverage industry** (juice, beer, wine clarification) to maintain **consistent flow rates** over long production runs.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** — especially cP → Pa·s (multiply by 10⁻³), atm → Pa (multiply by 101325), cm² → m² (multiply by 10⁻⁴). A single unit error makes the *entire numerical answer wrong* — typically **loss of 4–5 marks**.
>
> 2. **Confusing the *constant pressure* equation with the *constant rate* equation** — they look similar but are derived under different constraints. For constant rate, the relationship is *linear* (V vs t); for constant pressure, it is *parabolic* (t vs V²).
>
> 3. **Using V as the *intercept* variable** — students often plot V vs t/V instead of t/V vs V. The slope and intercept assignments in Equation (5) are **not interchangeable**; wrong assignment → wrong α and Rₘ.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to state the assumptions** in the derivation — KTU examiners award **at least 1 mark** specifically for *listing the assumptions* (incompressible cake, constant ΔP, uniform slurry, no medium clogging, etc.).
>
> 5. **Skipping the compressibility index discussion** when the question mentions "two different ΔP values" — this is a *favourite* KTU follow-up and carries **2–3 marks**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Constant pressure filtration** maintains a **fixed ΔP**; rate **decreases with time** as cake grows.
- [x] The **integrated equation** is: $t = \dfrac{\mu \alpha c}{2 A^2 \Delta P} V^2 + \dfrac{\mu R_m}{A \Delta P} V$.
- [x] The **linearized form** $\dfrac{t}{V}$ vs V is a **straight line** — this is the *experimental fingerprint*.
- [x] **Slope m** → yields **specific cake resistance α** via $\alpha = 2 A^2 \Delta P m / (\mu c)$.
- [x] **Intercept b** → yields **medium resistance Rₘ** via $R_m = A \Delta P b / \mu$.
- [x] **Always convert to SI** (Pa, m, kg, s) before plugging values into the formulas.
- [x] For **compressible cakes**, repeat the experiment at **two pressures** and plot **log α vs log ΔP**; the slope gives the **compressibility index s**.
- [x] The **instantaneous rate equation** is: $\dfrac{dV}{dt} = \dfrac{A^2 \Delta P}{\mu(\alpha c V + A R_m)}$.
- [x] **Pre-coating the filter cloth** with diatomaceous earth stabilises Rₘ and is widely used in **food and beverage** industries.
- [x] **Assumptions list is mandatory** in any KTU derivation: incompressible cake, uniform slurry, constant ΔP, no medium clogging, isothermal operation, Newtonian filtrate.
- [x] **Typical order of magnitude** for α in food slurries: 10⁹ to 10¹² m/kg; for Rₘ: 10⁹ to 10¹¹ m⁻¹.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Perform the simple distillation for studying separation of mixture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTL408 - FOOD PROCESS ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Perform the simple distillation for studying separation of mixture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Simple Distillation** is a **batch unit operation** used in food process engineering for the **partial separation of a liquid mixture into its components** based on the difference in their **volatilities (boiling points)**. The process involves heating a liquid mixture to its boiling point, vaporising the more volatile component (the **light key**), condensing the vapour in a condenser, and collecting the condensate (the **distillate**), while the less volatile component (the **heavy key**) remains as the **residue (bottoms)** in the distillation flask.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme lab context (PCFTL408), simple distillation is performed on a **binary liquid mixture** (e.g., ethanol–water, acetone–water, or methanol–water) to **study the relationship between distillate composition and the volume/quantity of distillate collected**. The experiment typically demonstrates that the distillate is **richer in the more volatile component (MVC)** than the original mixture, but **complete separation is NOT achieved** in a single equilibrium stage (this is a critical point examiners test).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — "The Tea Brewing Effect":**
> Imagine boiling a mixture of water and a few drops of lemon essence (volatile flavour) in a kettle. As the mixture boils, water vapour and lemon essence both rise, but the lemon essence (more volatile, lower boiling point) rises *preferentially*. When this vapour hits the cold lid of the kettle, it condenses back to liquid. The condensed droplets taste **strongly of lemon** — the original mixture was only faintly flavoured. The remaining liquid in the kettle is now **diluted / less flavoured**.

This everyday phenomenon is **simple distillation in miniature**:
- The **kettle** = distillation flask
- The **flame** = heat source
- The **lid (cold surface)** = condenser
- The **condensed droplets** = distillate
- The **liquid left behind** = residue (bottoms)

## 1.3 Key Terminology & Standard Metrics

| Term | Definition | Standard Unit |
|------|------------|---------------|
| **Distillate** | Condensed vapour collected as product | mL or g |
| **Residue / Bottoms** | Liquid remaining in flask after distillation | mL or g |
| **MVC (More Volatile Component)** | Component with lower boiling point | — |
| **LVC (Less Volatile Component)** | Component with higher boiling point | — |
| **Reflux Ratio (R)** | Ratio of condensate returned to vapour leaving | dimensionless |
| **Plate / Theoretical Stage** | Zone of vapour–liquid equilibrium contact | dimensionless |
| **Relative Volatility (α)** | Ratio of K-values of two components | dimensionless |
| **Azeotrope** | Mixture that boils at constant composition | mol fraction |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics Used in Lab:**
> - **Boiling point of water:** **100 °C (373.15 K) at 1 atm**
> - **Boiling point of ethanol:** **78.37 °C (351.52 K) at 1 atm**
> - **Universal Gas Constant (R):** **8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹**
> - **Atmospheric pressure at Kerala sea level:** ≈ **101.325 kPa (1 atm)**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Vapour–Liquid Equilibrium (VLE)

The separation achieved in simple distillation is governed by **Raoult's Law** and **Dalton's Law of Partial Presses** acting simultaneously at the liquid–vapour interface.

### 2.1.1 Raoult's Law (Liquid Phase)

For an ideal binary solution, the **partial pressure** of each component in the vapour phase is directly proportional to its **mole fraction in the liquid phase**:

$$ P_i = x_i \cdot P_i^{sat} $$

where:
- $P_i$ = partial pressure of component *i* in vapour (kPa)
- $x_i$ = mole fraction of component *i* in liquid
- $P_i^{sat}$ = vapour (saturation) pressure of pure component *i* at the system temperature (kPa)

### 2.1.2 Dalton's Law (Vapour Phase)

The total pressure is the sum of the partial pressures of all components:

$$ P_{total} = P_A + P_B = x_A P_A^{sat} + x_B P_B^{sat} $$

For a binary system (A = MVC, B = LVC) at **total pressure P**:

$$ P = x_A P_A^{sat} + (1 - x_A) P_B^{sat} $$

### 2.1.3 Vapour Phase Composition (y) — The Key Equation

Combining Raoult's and Dalton's laws, the **mole fraction of the MVC in the vapour phase** is:

$$ y_A = \frac{x_A P_A^{sat}}{x_A P_A^{sat} + (1 - x_A) P_B^{sat}} $$

This is the **fundamental equation that drives the separation** in simple distillation. Because $P_A^{sat} > P_B^{sat}$ (MVC is more volatile), we always get $y_A > x_A$ — meaning the **vapour is always richer in the MVC than the liquid it came from**.

### 2.1.4 Relative Volatility (α)

The **ease of separation** between two components is quantified by **relative volatility**:

$$ \alpha_{AB} = \frac{P_A^{sat}}{P_B^{sat}} = \frac{K_A}{K_B} $$

where $K_i = P_i^{sat} / P$ is the **K-value (equilibrium ratio)** of component *i*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of α:**
> - **α > 1:** Separation is possible (A is more volatile)
> - **α = 1:** Separation is impossible by simple distillation (forms azeotrope)
> - **α >> 1:** Easy separation (e.g., α for benzene–toluene ≈ 2.5 at 1 atm)

### 2.1.5 Rayleigh Equation — The Core of Simple Distillation

Unlike continuous distillation (which uses McCabe–Thiele method with many stages), **simple (differential/batch) distillation** is described by the **Rayleigh Equation**. It relates the **change in liquid composition** to the **amount distilled**:

$$ \ln\left(\frac{L_0}{L}\right) = \int_{x_0}^{x} \frac{dx}{y - x} $$

For **constant relative volatility α** (a standard assumption for dilute/ideal systems), this integrates to:

$$ \ln\left(\frac{L_0}{L}\right) = \frac{1}{\alpha - 1} \left[ \ln\left(\frac{x_0}{x}\right) + \alpha \ln\left(\frac{1 - x}{1 - x_0}\right) \right] $$

or equivalently in terms of moles distilled:

$$ \ln\left(\frac{N_0}{N}\right) = \frac{1}{\alpha - 1} \left[ \ln\left(\frac{x_0}{x}\right) + \alpha \ln\left(\frac{1 - x}{1 - x_0}\right) \right] $$

where:
- $L_0, L$ = initial and final moles of liquid in the flask
- $N_0, N$ = initial and final total moles
- $x_0, x$ = initial and final mole fraction of MVC in liquid
- $y$ = mole fraction of MVC in vapour (in equilibrium with liquid at composition *x*)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is this called a "single-stage" or "differential" distillation?**
> Because there is **only ONE equilibrium contact** between liquid and vapour (in the boiling flask), and the vapour is **continuously removed and condensed** as it forms. The liquid composition changes progressively with time. This is fundamentally different from a **fractionating column** which provides multiple equilibrium stages.

## 2.2 Material Balance in Simple Distillation

Let:
- $F$ = moles of feed (initial charge)
- $D$ = moles of distillate collected
- $W$ = moles of residue remaining
- $x_F$ = mole fraction of MVC in feed
- $x_D$ = overall mole fraction of MVC in distillate
- $x_W$ = mole fraction of MVC in residue

### Overall Mass Balance

$$ F = D + W $$

### Component (MVC) Mass Balance

$$ F \cdot x_F = D \cdot x_D + W \cdot x_W $$

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU exam problems, these two mass balance equations are typically used along with the Rayleigh equation to solve for unknowns.**

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Units / Condition |
|---|----------|-------------|--------------------|
| 1 | $P_i = x_i \cdot P_i^{sat}$ | Raoult's Law | kPa, ideal solution |
| 2 | $P_{total} = \Sigma P_i$ | Dalton's Law | kPa |
| 3 | $y_A = \dfrac{x_A P_A^{sat}}{P_{total}}$ | Vapour phase composition (binary) | mole fraction |
| 4 | $\alpha_{AB} = \dfrac{P_A^{sat}}{P_B^{sat}} = \dfrac{K_A}{K_B}$ | Relative volatility | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\ln(L_0/L) = \dfrac{1}{\alpha - 1}\left[\ln(x_0/x) + \alpha \ln((1-x)/(1-x_0))\right]$ | Rayleigh Equation | constant α |
| 6 | $F = D + W$ | Total mass balance | moles / kg |
| 7 | $F x_F = D x_D + W x_W$ | Component balance | moles of MVC |
| 8 | $x_D = \dfrac{F x_F - W x_W}{D}$ | Distillate composition | mole fraction |
| 9 | $\eta_{MVC} = \dfrac{D \cdot x_D}{F \cdot x_F} \times 100$ | % Recovery of MVC | % |
| 10 | $\eta_{sep} = \dfrac{x_D - x_F}{1 - x_F} \times 100$ | Separation efficiency | % |
| 11 | Antoine: $\log_{10} P^{sat} = A - \dfrac{B}{C + T(^{\circ}C)}$ | Vapour pressure correlation | kPa or mmHg |
| 12 | Clausius–Clapeyron: $\ln(P^{sat}) = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T}\right) + C$ | Vapour pressure vs T | kPa, K |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL KTU Exam Pitfall — Antoine Constants:**
> Antoine constants A, B, C are **substance-specific and unit-specific**. The B and C values are DIFFERENT if T is in °C vs K, and if P is in mmHg vs kPa vs bar. Always use the constants consistent with the units you want. For water (P in mmHg, T in °C): A = 8.07131, B = 1730.63, C = 233.426.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Food Process Engineering

1. **Production of potable spirits** (e.g., country liquor, arrack) — separating ethanol from fermented mash.
2. **Concentration of fruit juices** — removing water to obtain concentrates.
3. **Production of essential oils** (e.g., mint, lemongrass oil) — steam/distillation recovery.
4. **Purification of water** in labs (deionised/distilled water for analytical work).
5. **Recovery of solvents** in food ingredient extraction (e.g., hexane from oil extraction miscella).
6. **Desolventisation** of soybean meal in edible oil refining.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Rayleigh Equation (Detailed Step-by-Step)

### Starting Setup

Consider a batch distillation flask containing $L$ moles of liquid with mole fraction $x$ of the MVC. At an infinitesimal instant, a small amount $dL$ evaporates (becomes vapour of composition $y$ in equilibrium with $x$).

### Total Mass Balance (differential)

The liquid lost by evaporation equals the vapour generated:

$$ dV = -dL \quad \text{(vapour produced = liquid lost)} $$

### Component Mass Balance (MVC)

The MVC lost from the liquid = MVC gained by the vapour:

$$ \text{(Loss of MVC from liquid)} = \text{(MVC carried away in vapour)} $$

$$ -d(Lx) = y \, dV $$

Substituting $dV = -dL$:

$$ -d(Lx) = y(-dL) $$

$$ -d(Lx) = -y \, dL $$

Expanding the left side using the product rule ($d(Lx) = L \, dx + x \, dL$):

$$ -(L \, dx + x \, dL) = -y \, dL $$

$$ L \, dx + x \, dL = y \, dL $$

$$ L \, dx = y \, dL - x \, dL $$

$$ L \, dx = (y - x) \, dL $$

### Separating Variables

$$ \frac{dx}{y - x} = \frac{dL}{L} $$

### Integrating from Initial State ($L_0, x_0$) to Final State ($L, x$)

$$ \int_{x_0}^{x} \frac{dx}{y - x} = \int_{L_0}^{L} \frac{dL}{L} $$

$$ \int_{x_0}^{x} \frac{dx}{y - x} = \ln\left(\frac{L}{L_0}\right) $$

Or, more commonly written as:

$$ \ln\left(\frac{L_0}{L}\right) = \int_{x_0}^{x} \frac{dx}{y - x} $$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **general form of the Rayleigh Equation**. The right-hand side is an integral that requires the relationship between $y$ and $x$ (i.e., the VLE data or equation) to be solvable.

### Solution for Constant Relative Volatility (α = constant)

Using the VLE relation $y = \dfrac{\alpha x}{1 + (\alpha - 1)x}$ (derived from Raoult's + Dalton's), we get:

$$ y - x = \frac{\alpha x - x[1 + (\alpha - 1)x]}{1 + (\alpha - 1)x} = \frac{(\alpha - 1)x(1 - x)}{1 + (\alpha - 1)x} $$

Therefore:

$$ \frac{dx}{y - x} = \frac{1 + (\alpha - 1)x}{(\alpha - 1)x(1 - x)} \, dx $$

Splitting by partial fractions:

$$ \frac{1 + (\alpha - 1)x}{(\alpha - 1)x(1 - x)} = \frac{A}{x} + \frac{B}{1 - x} $$

Solving for A and B gives $A = \dfrac{1}{\alpha - 1}$ and $B = \dfrac{\alpha}{\alpha - 1}$. Therefore:

$$ \frac{dx}{y - x} = \frac{1}{\alpha - 1} \left[ \frac{1}{x} + \frac{\alpha}{1 - x} \right] dx $$

Integrating from $x_0$ to $x$:

$$ \int_{x_0}^{x} \frac{dx}{y - x} = \frac{1}{\alpha - 1} \left[ \ln\left(\frac{x}{x_0}\right) - \alpha \ln\left(\frac{1 - x}{1 - x_0}\right) \right] $$

$$ = \frac{1}{\alpha - 1} \left[ \ln\left(\frac{x}{x_0}\right) + \alpha \ln\left(\frac{1 - x_0}{1 - x}\right) \right] $$

Equating with $\ln(L_0/L)$:

$$ \ln\left(\frac{L_0}{L}\right) = \frac{1}{\alpha - 1} \left[ \ln\left(\frac{x}{x_0}\right) + \alpha \ln\left(\frac{1 - x_0}{1 - x}\right) \right] $$

Rewriting with signs reversed (conventional form):

$$ \boxed{\;\ln\left(\frac{L_0}{L}\right) = \frac{1}{\alpha - 1} \left[ \ln\left(\frac{x_0}{x}\right) + \alpha \ln\left(\frac{1 - x}{1 - x_0}\right) \right]\;} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **final integrated Rayleigh Equation** that KTU examiners expect students to use for binary mixtures with constant α. For non-constant α, graphical or numerical integration of the VLE data is required.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1: Vapour Composition from Liquid Composition

**Problem:** A mixture of benzene (A) and toluene (B) contains $x_A = 0.40$ (mole fraction) in the liquid. At 1 atm total pressure, the saturation pressures are $P_A^{sat} = 178$ kPa and $P_B^{sat} = 74$ kPa. Calculate the vapour phase composition $y_A$.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Apply Raoult's Law to compute total pressure and vapour composition.

$$ y_A = \frac{x_A P_A^{sat}}{x_A P_A^{sat} + (1 - x_A) P_B^{sat}} $$

**Step 2:** Substitute the values.

$$ y_A = \frac{(0.40)(178)}{(0.40)(178) + (0.60)(74)} $$

**Step 3:** Compute numerator.

$$ (0.40)(178) = 71.2 \text{ kPa} $$

**Step 4:** Compute denominator.

$$ (0.60)(74) = 44.4 \text{ kPa} $$

$$ 71.2 + 44.4 = 115.6 \text{ kPa} $$

**Step 5:** Divide.

$$ y_A = \frac{71.2}{115.6} = 0.6159 \approx 0.616 $$

**Result:** $y_A = 0.616$, $y_B = 1 - 0.616 = 0.384$

**Interpretation:** The vapour is **enriched** in benzene from 0.40 (liquid) to 0.616 (vapour), confirming that **benzene is the MVC** and separation is feasible.

**Relative volatility check:**

$$ \alpha_{AB} = \frac{P_A^{sat}}{P_B^{sat}} = \frac{178}{74} = 2.405 $$

Since α = 2.405 > 1, the separation is feasible.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2: Rayleigh Equation — Finding Final Liquid Composition

**Problem:** A charge of **100 mol** of a benzene–toluene mixture with $x_0 = 0.50$ (mole fraction benzene) is subjected to simple distillation. The relative volatility α = 2.47. After some time, **40 mol** of distillate has been collected. Find the **mole fraction of benzene in the remaining liquid** ($x$).

### Given
- $L_0 = 100$ mol
- $L = L_0 - D = 100 - 40 = 60$ mol
- $x_0 = 0.50$
- $\alpha = 2.47$

### Solution

**Step 1:** Write the Rayleigh Equation.

$$ \ln\left(\frac{L_0}{L}\right) = \frac{1}{\alpha - 1} \left[ \ln\left(\frac{x_0}{x}\right) + \alpha \ln\left(\frac{1 - x}{1 - x_0}\right) \right] $$

**Step 2:** Compute the LHS.

$$ \ln(100/60) = \ln(1.6667) = 0.5108 $$

**Step 3:** Compute $\alpha - 1$.

$$ \alpha - 1 = 2.47 - 1 = 1.47 $$

**Step 4:** Substitute.

$$ 0.5108 = \frac{1}{1.47} \left[ \ln\left(\frac{0.50}{x}\right) + 2.47 \ln\left(\frac{1 - x}{0.50}\right) \right] $$

$$ 0.5108 \times 1.47 = \ln\left(\frac{0.50}{x}\right) + 2.47 \ln\left(\frac{1 - x}{0.50}\right) $$

$$ 0.7509 = \ln(0.50) - \ln(x) + 2.47[\ln(1 - x) - \ln(0.50)] $$

$$ 0.7509 = \ln(0.50) - \ln(x) + 2.47 \ln(1 - x) - 2.47 \ln(0.50) $$

$$ 0.7509 = -1.47 \ln(0.50) - \ln(x) + 2.47 \ln(1 - x) $$

Recall $\ln(0.50) = -0.6931$, so $-1.47 \times (-0.6931) = 1.0189$:

$$ 0.7509 = 1.0189 - \ln(x) + 2.47 \ln(1 - x) $$

$$ \ln(x) - 2.47 \ln(1 - x) = 1.0189 - 0.7509 = 0.2680 $$

**Step 5:** Solve by trial-and-error (or Newton's method).

Let $f(x) = \ln(x) - 2.47 \ln(1 - x) - 0.2680 = 0$

Try $x = 0.40$:
- $\ln(0.40) = -0.9163$
- $\ln(0.60) = -0.5108$
- $f(0.40) = -0.9163 - 2.47(-0.5108) - 0.2680 = -0.9163 + 1.2617 - 0.2680 = 0.0774$

Try $x = 0.42$:
- $\ln(0.42) = -0.8675$
- $\ln(0.58) = -0.5447$
- $f(0.42) = -0.8675 - 2.47(-0.5447) - 0.2680 = -0.8675 + 1.3454 - 0.2680 = 0.2099$

Try $x = 0.39$:
- $\ln(0.39) = -0.9416$
- $\ln(0.61) = -0.4943$
- $f(0.39) = -0.9416 - 2.47(-0.4943) - 0.2680 = -0.9416 + 1.2209 - 0.2680 = 0.0113$

Try $x = 0.388$:
- $\ln(0.388) = -0.9469$
- $\ln(0.612) = -0.4906$
- $f(0.388) = -0.9469 + 1.2118 - 0.2680 = -0.0031$ ✓

**Step 6:** Result.

$$ \boxed{x \approx 0.388} $$

**Interpretation:** As distillation proceeds, the **liquid in the flask becomes leaner in the MVC** (benzene drops from 0.50 to 0.388). This confirms that **the MVC preferentially leaves with the vapour**.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 3: Distillate Composition from Material Balance

**Problem:** Continuing from Example 2, calculate the **overall composition of the distillate** ($x_D$) collected.

### Given
- $F = L_0 = 100$ mol, $x_F = 0.50$
- $D = 40$ mol, $W = L = 60$ mol, $x_W = x = 0.388$

### Solution

**Step 1:** Apply component mass balance for benzene (MVC).

$$ F \cdot x_F = D \cdot x_D + W \cdot x_W $$

**Step 2:** Substitute values.

$$ (100)(0.50) = (40) \cdot x_D + (60)(0.388) $$

$$ 50 = 40 \, x_D + 23.28 $$

**Step 3:** Solve for $x_D$.

$$ 40 \, x_D = 50 - 23.28 = 26.72 $$

$$ x_D = \frac{26.72}{40} = 0.668 $$

**Result:** $x_D = 0.668$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key observation:** $x_D = 0.668 > x_F = 0.50 > x_W = 0.388$. This is the **hallmark of distillation separation** — the distillate is always richer in MVC than the original feed, and the residue is always leaner.

**Step 4:** % Recovery of benzene.

$$ \eta_{MVC} = \frac{D \cdot x_D}{F \cdot x_F} \times 100 = \frac{40 \times 0.668}{100 \times 0.50} \times 100 = \frac{26.72}{50} \times 100 = 53.44\% $$

**Step 5:** Separation efficiency (relative to pure MVC).

$$ \eta_{sep} = \frac{x_D - x_F}{1 - x_F} \times 100 = \frac{0.668 - 0.50}{1 - 0.50} \times 100 = \frac{0.168}{0.50} \times 100 = 33.6\% $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Single-stage simple distillation gives limited separation** (~33.6% efficiency here). To achieve higher purity, **multiple stages (fractionating column) or repeated batch distillations** are required.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 4: Numerical VLE Table (For Lab Graph Plotting)

**Problem:** Construct a VLE data table for benzene–toluene at 1 atm (101.325 kPa) using Antoine equations. Use to plot the **x-y equilibrium curve** (McCabe–Thiele style).

### Antoine Constants
- **Benzene (A):** A = 6.90565, B = 1211.033, C = 220.790 (P in mmHg, T in °C)
- **Toluene (B):** A = 6.95464, B = 1344.800, C = 219.482 (P in mmHg, T in °C)

**Note:** For P in kPa, divide the result by 7.5006.

### Computed VLE Data

| T (°C) | $P_A^{sat}$ (kPa) | $P_B^{sat}$ (kPa) | α | x_A (chosen) | y_A (computed) |
|--------|------------------|------------------|------|-------------|----------------|
| 80.1 | 101.3 | 38.9 | 2.60 | 1.000 | 1.000 |
| 85 | 116.9 | 46.0 | 2.54 | 0.780 | 0.900 |
| 90 | 136.2 | 54.2 | 2.51 | 0.580 | 0.780 |
| 95 | 157.7 | 63.3 | 2.49 | 0.400 | 0.620 |
| 100 | 180.6 | 73.3 | 2.46 | 0.260 | 0.450 |
| 105 | 207.0 | 84.5 | 2.45 | 0.130 | 0.260 |
| 110.6 | 235.6 | 101.3 | 2.32 | 0.000 | 0.000 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on the table:** The y values shown are typical for benzene–toluene at 1 atm. For a real KTU lab exam, the student must compute these from Raoult's + Dalton's law using the **Antoine equation** for the given temperature. The x-y plot is **always above the 45° diagonal** for systems with α > 1, and the **distance from the diagonal represents the ease of separation**.

A common viva question: *"Why does the thermometer show a temperature lower than the boiling point of water when distilling a dilute ethanol solution?"* — Answer: Because the vapour composition is **not pure water** but a mixture rich in ethanol (lower boiling point).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Define simple distillation. State two industrial applications of simple distillation in food processing.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):
**Definition (1.5 marks):** Simple distillation is a batch separation process in which a liquid mixture is heated to vaporise the more volatile component, the vapour is condensed, and the condensate (distillate) is collected separately. The process provides **only one equilibrium stage** and therefore yields **partial, not complete, separation**.

**Applications (1.5 marks):**
1. **Production of distilled spirits** (ethanol–water separation from fermented mash).
2. **Concentration of fruit juices** by removing water.
3. *(Optional third)* Recovery of solvents like hexane from oil miscella in edible oil extraction.

> **Marking tip:** Definition carries 1.5 marks; 2 applications × 0.75 = 1.5 marks.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

**State Raoult's Law and Dalton's Law of partial pressures. How are they combined to obtain the vapour phase composition in a binary distillation?**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):
**Raoult's Law (1 mark):** For an ideal solution, the partial pressure of a component is equal to its mole fraction in the liquid multiplied by its pure-component vapour pressure at the system temperature: $P_i = x_i P_i^{sat}$.

**Dalton's Law (0.5 mark):** The total pressure of a gas/vapour mixture equals the sum of the partial pressures of all components: $P_{total} = \sum P_i$.

**Combination to get vapour composition (1.5 marks):**
For a binary mixture of A (MVC) and B (LVC), combining the two laws at total pressure P:
$$ P = x_A P_A^{sat} + (1 - x_A) P_B^{sat} $$
$$ y_A = \frac{x_A P_A^{sat}}{P} = \frac{x_A P_A^{sat}}{x_A P_A^{sat} + (1 - x_A) P_B^{sat}} $$
This is the **equilibrium vapour composition** in contact with liquid of composition $x_A$. Since $P_A^{sat} > P_B^{sat}$, we always have $y_A > x_A$, confirming vapour enrichment in the MVC.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Part B questions for lab courses (PCFTL408) often combine **theory (4 marks)**, **procedure / apparatus (3 marks)**, **calculations / numerical (5 marks)**, and **results / viva (2 marks)**. Students should structure their answers accordingly.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### Q.A (a) [7 Marks] — Theory + Procedure

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat labelled diagram, describe the **experimental procedure** to perform simple distillation of a **benzene–toluene mixture**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer:

**Apparatus Required (2 marks):**
- Round-bottom flask (500 mL), Liebig condenser, distillation head, thermometer (0–150 °C), vacuum adapter, receiving flask (graduated cylinder), heating mantle, rubber tubing, boiling chips, cooling water supply, refractometer / hydrometer for analysis.

**Chemicals (0.5 mark):** Benzene–toluene mixture (e.g., 50:50 v/v), boiling chips.

**Procedure (4 marks):**
1. **Charging the flask:** Take ~200 mL of the benzene–toluene mixture in the round-bottom flask. Add 2–3 boiling chips to ensure smooth boiling and prevent bumping.
2. **Assembly:** Assemble the distillation apparatus in the correct order — heating mantle → flask → distillation head with thermometer bulb at the side-arm level → condenser (with water flow: in from bottom, out from top — **counter-current**) → vacuum adapter → graduated receiving cylinder.
3. **Pre-checks:** Ensure all joints are airtight (use keck clips), cooling water is flowing, and the receiving flask is correctly positioned.
4. **Heating:** Switch on the heating mantle and gradually increase the voltage. Heat slowly to avoid sudden bumping.
5. **Observation:** Note the **first drop of distillate** (this is the **forecut**). Record the **vapour temperature** when steady boiling is established (it should be near the boiling point of benzene, 80.1 °C, if the mixture is rich in benzene).
6. **Collection:** Collect the distillate in **fractions** of 10 mL each, recording the temperature and cumulative volume.
7. **Endpoint:** Stop heating when the temperature starts rising sharply (indicating toluene-rich vapour is now coming over), or when the desired volume of distillate is collected.
8. **Cooling & measurement:** Allow the apparatus to cool, then measure the **residue volume** and **distillate volume**. Determine the composition of each fraction using a **refractometer** (RI method) or **hydrometer** (density method).
9. **Safety:** Perform in a fume hood, wear safety goggles, no open flames (heating mantle only).

**Precautions (0.5 mark):**
- Thermometer bulb position correct.
- Cooling water flow rate adequate (~1–2 L/min).
- Slow heating to prevent superheating / bumping.
- Stop before flask goes dry (risk of fire / explosion).

---

#### Q.A (b) [7 Marks] — Numerical Problem

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(b)** A simple distillation is carried out on a mixture of **methanol (A) and water (B)** containing **0.30 mole fraction methanol**. The system has a **relative volatility α = 3.50** (assumed constant).
