**Working:** Product is loaded on trays → freezing stage runs at $-40 \, °C$ → chamber is evacuated → shelves are heated to $20$ to $40 \, °C$ → ice sublimes → vapour migrates to condenser and freezes → cycle ends with secondary drying at $40$ to $60 \, °C$ → product sealed in moisture-barrier packaging under nitrogen.

**Role of condenser:** Acts as a *cold trap* and maintains the pressure gradient. Without it, the chamber pressure would rise, halting sublimation.

**Role of vacuum pump:** Removes non-condensable gases (air, leaked gases) that cannot be trapped by the condenser. Maintains low total pressure. *(7 marks: 2 for diagram description + 2 for each role + 1 for working sequence)*

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Total water to be removed:**

$$M_{db,i} = \dfrac{0.70}{1 - 0.70} = 2.333 \, kg/kg$$

$$M_{db,f} = \dfrac{0.03}{1 - 0.03} = 0.0309 \, kg/kg$$

$$m_w = m_s (M_{db,i} - M_{db,f}) = 10 \times (2.333 - 0.0309) = 10 \times 2.302 = \boxed{23.02 \, kg}$$

**(ii) Total energy input:**

$$E_{total} = m_w \times E_{specific} = 23.02 \times 4500 = \boxed{1{,}03{,}590 \, kJ} = 28.78 \, kWh$$

**(iii) Batch energy cost:**

$$\text{Cost} = 28.78 \times 8 = \boxed{₹ \, 230.24}$$

*Engineering insight:* At industrial scale, the energy cost of freeze drying is typically 4 to 8 times higher than spray drying. This justifies the premium price of freeze-dried products. *(7 marks: (i) 2 + (ii) 2 + (iii) 2 + economic interpretation 1)*

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Freeze-Drying Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Unit Confusion in $M_{wb}$ vs. $M_{db}$:** KTU examiners *deliberately* mix wet-basis and dry-basis moisture in problems. Converting first will save **2–3 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting the Triple-Point Constraint:** Writing "sublimation occurs at any pressure" loses marks. Always state that **chamber pressure must be below 611.657 Pa** for pure water sublimation.
> 3. **Using $L_v$ instead of $L_s$:** Sublimation uses **2838 kJ/kg**, not 2501 kJ/kg (which is vaporisation). The difference is the **fusion heat (334 kJ/kg)**; mixing them up costs a mark.
> 4. **Ignoring Condenser Function:** Students often describe the pump but forget the *condenser* — examiners look for the term "cold trap" or "vapour capture".
> 5. **Collapse vs. Eutectic Confusion:** Use $T_c$ for amorphous, $T_e$ for crystalline. Mixing them up = full mark deduction on design questions.
> 6. **Skipping the Clausius–Clapeyron Derivation:** The derivation is the **single most expected long-answer derivation** in Module 2 — students who skip steps lose **up to 5 marks**.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Freeze drying = sublimation under vacuum** of frozen water, in three stages: **Freezing → Primary Drying → Secondary Drying**.
- **Triple point of water: 0.01 °C, 611.657 Pa** — *the* fundamental thermodynamic anchor.
- **Latent heat of sublimation $L_s = 2838 \, kJ/kg$** (at 0 °C) — *not* $L_v = 2501 \, kJ/kg$.
- **Chamber pressure** must be **< 611.657 Pa**; in practice **10–30 Pa**.
- **Product temperature** must remain **< collapse / eutectic temperature** to prevent structural failure.
- **Clausius–Clapeyron equation** is the **master P–T relation** for sublimation: $\ln(P_2/P_1) = (L_s/R)(1/T_1 - 1/T_2)$.
- **Heat transfer (Eq. 2):** $q = (k_d/\ell_d)(T_s - T_i)$.
- **Mass transfer (Eq. 3):** $\dot{m} \propto (P_i - P_c)/\ell_d$.
- **Final moisture specification:** < 2–3 % (w.b.) and $a_w < 0.30$.
- **Condenser** is a *cold trap*; **vacuum pump** removes *non-condensables*.
- **Key advantages:** Retains colour, flavour, aroma, nutrients; porous structure → instant rehydration; long shelf life.
- **Key disadvantages:** High capital cost, high energy cost (4–8× spray drying), slow batch process.
- **Typical applications:** Instant coffee, freeze-dried fruits, RTE curries, probiotic cultures, herbal extracts, pharmaceuticals, nutraceuticals.
- **Always convert** $M_{wb}$ to $M_{db}$ *first* before any rate-based calculation.
- **Always check** that the driving force $P_i > P_c$ before claiming sublimation is feasible.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "F-D-S-V-C" = Freeze, Dry, Seal, Vacuum, Condense.**
> Remember: **F**reezing sets the structure, **D**rying under vacuum removes water, **S**ealing under N₂ preserves quality, **V**acuum enables sublimation, **C**ondenser captures vapour. This single string covers 80 % of viva questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of the separation efficiency of a spiral separator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTL408 - FOOD PROCESS ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of the separation efficiency of a spiral separator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **spiral separator** (also known as a **helical gravity concentrator**) is a flowing-film gravity concentration device that exploits the density difference between particles to achieve separation as a dilute slurry flows down a helical trough. In the context of **Food Process Engineering**, it is extensively employed for the dry/wet separation of **denser impurities** (such as stones, metallic fragments, mud clods, and damaged/shriveled grains) from **lighter edible grains** (rice, wheat, pulses, millets, spices, coffee beans, etc.).

**Separation Efficiency (η)** is a quantitative, dimensionless parameter that measures the effectiveness of the spiral separator in selectively recovering the *desired valuable material* into the *concentrate stream* while rejecting the *undesired gangue material* into the *tailing stream*. It is expressed as a percentage (%) and combines two key performance metrics: **Recovery (R)** and **Grade (G)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCFTL408):**
> Separation efficiency of a spiral separator is defined as the ratio of the actual recovery of the valuable (light) fraction in the concentrate to the ideal recovery, evaluated using mass-balance equations based on feed, concentrate, and tailing assays.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Winding Mountain Stream"

Imagine a **fast-flowing mountain stream** that follows a winding, snake-like (sinusoidal) path down a valley. As the water rushes:

- **Heavy pebbles, stones, and metallic bits** are flung outward by the swirling water and settle in the **inner curves** of the bend (where water velocity is lowest).
- **Lighter leaves, twigs, and dust** are carried by the faster-moving surface currents toward the **outer edge** of the bend.
- After many bends, the inner banks are lined with heavy gold-like material, and the outer banks collect the floating waste.

A **spiral separator is essentially a man-made, tightly-coiled version of this stream**, with the inner edge (high density) and outer edge (low density) collected at separate outlets. The "bends" of the stream are replaced by **3 to 7 helical turns** of a trough, and gravity does the work continuously without any moving parts.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this used in Food Industry?**
> Food grains have densities ranging from **1.0 g/cm³ (shrivelled/damaged)** to **1.45 g/cm³ (healthy kernels)**. Stones and metallic contaminants are usually **> 2.0 g/cm³**. A spiral separator exploits this density gap to remove impurities that cannot be removed by sieving alone (since they may have similar sizes to the grains).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

The following key parameters are used to evaluate spiral separator performance:

- **Solids-to-liquid ratio (pulp density)**: Typically **15–25 % solids by weight** in feed slurry.
- **Feed flow rate**: **0.5 to 1.5 m³/h** per spiral start.
- **Particle size range**: **75 µm to 3 mm** for minerals; **1 mm to 8 mm** for grains.
- **Specific gravity of water**: **ρ_w = 1000 kg/m³** (acting as the carrier medium).
- **Efficiency range expected in food labs**: **60 % to 90 %** depending on feed characteristics.
- **Number of turns**: **3 to 7** helical turns per separator column.
- **Pitch (vertical drop per turn)**: **0.25 to 0.5 m**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The separation in a spiral trough occurs due to the combined action of **four primary forces** acting on each particle:

1. **Gravitational Force (F_g):** Pulls all particles vertically downward along the spiral pitch.
2. **Centripetal/Drag Force (F_c):** Generated by the secondary helical flow of slurry. Denser particles experience greater centripetal acceleration and migrate **inward (toward the centre column)**.
3. **Buoyant Force (F_b):** Upward force equal to the weight of displaced water; reduces effective weight of submerged particles.
4. **Hydraulic Lift (F_h):** Upward turbulent lift force from the swirling slurry, dominant on lighter particles, pushing them toward the **outer edge**.

**Step-by-step operational logic:**

- **Step 1 — Slurry Preparation:** A dilute aqueous slurry (feed) is prepared by mixing the food grains with water at a controlled pulp density.
- **Step 2 — Feed Introduction:** The slurry is introduced at the **top of the spiral** through a central feed box.
- **Step 3 — Stratification:** As the slurry descends, secondary helical flow patterns develop. Heavier particles (stones, metallics) settle quickly to the **inner trough bottom**, while lighter grains are carried in suspension toward the **outer edge**.
- **Step 4 — Collection:** At the bottom of the spiral, **splitters/dividers** physically separate the inner stream (concentrate — heavy impurities) and the outer stream (tailing — clean grains).
- **Step 5 — Evaluation:** Each stream is dried, weighed, and assayed (e.g., by manual picking of impurities) to determine the **grade** of each stream.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind the Geometry:** The helical path increases the residence time and amplifies the effective density-driven separation. A straight inclined plane would not allow the secondary flow patterns to develop fully. The number of turns is optimised based on the density contrast: **higher contrast → fewer turns required**.

## 2.2 Mass-Balance Framework

A spiral separation experiment produces three streams:

- **Feed (F):** The original input slurry of total mass $M_F$ with grade $c_F$ (mass fraction of valuable/clean grains).
- **Concentrate (C):** The inner stream (heavies) of mass $M_C$ with grade $c_C$.
- **Tailing (T):** The outer stream (lights/cleaned grains) of mass $M_T$ with grade $c_T$.

Two fundamental conservation laws apply:

**Overall Mass Balance:**
$$M_F = M_C + M_T$$

**Component (Valuable Material) Mass Balance:**
$$M_F \cdot c_F = M_C \cdot c_C + M_T \cdot c_T$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | **Yield (Mass Recovery to Concentrate), Y** | $Y = \dfrac{M_C}{M_F} \times 100$ | % (mass of concentrate / mass of feed × 100) |
| 2 | **Recovery of Valuable Material, R_v** | $R_v = \dfrac{M_C \cdot c_C}{M_F \cdot c_F} \times 100$ | % — also called **metallurgical recovery** |
| 3 | **Recovery of Gangue to Concentrate, R_g** | $R_g = \dfrac{M_C \cdot (1-c_C)}{M_F \cdot (1-c_F)} \times 100$ | % — lower is better |
| 4 | **Concentrate Grade, c_C** | $c_C = \dfrac{\text{mass of valuable in C}}{M_C}$ | fraction (0 to 1) |
| 5 | **Tailing Grade, c_T** | $c_T = \dfrac{\text{mass of valuable in T}}{M_T}$ | fraction (0 to 1) |
| 6 | **Newton's Separation Efficiency, η_N** | $\eta_N = R_v - R_g$ | % — classic 2-component efficiency |
| 7 | **Hancock's Efficiency, η_H** | $\eta_H = \dfrac{R_v - \gamma}{100 - \gamma_f} \times 100$ where $\gamma = Y$ | % — accounts for yield |
| 8 | **Taggart's Efficiency, η_T** | $\eta_T = \dfrac{R_v - \gamma}{1 - \gamma_f/\gamma} \times ...$ | % — older formulation |
| 9 | **Single-Stream Grade Efficiency, η_G** | $\eta_G = \dfrac{c_C - c_F}{100 - c_F} \times 100$ (in %) | % — grade-based measure |
| 10 | **Overall Mass Balance Check** | $M_F = M_C + M_T$ | Conservation law |
| 11 | **Component Mass Balance Check** | $M_F c_F = M_C c_C + M_T c_T$ | Conservation law |
| 12 | **Settling Velocity (Stokes' Law)** | $v_s = \dfrac{d^2 (\rho_p - \rho_w) g}{18 \mu}$ | m/s (for viscous regime) |
| 13 | **Slurry Pulp Density** | $\rho_{slurry} = \dfrac{m_{solids}}{V_{slurry}}$ | kg/m³ (typically 1100–1200) |
| 14 | **Volumetric Feed Rate** | $Q = A_{trough} \cdot v_{slurry}$ | m³/s |
| 15 | **Slope Angle (Pitch) of Spiral** | $\tan(\theta) = \dfrac{p}{2\pi r}$ | radians; $p$ = pitch, $r$ = radius |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU Board Exam:** Always remember that the **Recovery R** must be between **0 % and 100 %**. A recovery of 100 % means all valuable material has been recovered (but possibly with low grade). A grade of 100 % means a perfectly pure concentrate (but possibly with low recovery). The **best separator optimises BOTH** — this trade-off is captured in the **grade-recovery curve**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the food industry, spiral separator efficiency data is used for:

- **Process Design:** Sizing of pre-cleaning equipment in rice mills, pulse processing plants, and spice industries.
- **Quality Control:** Quantifying the percentage of defective grains removed per pass.
- **Economic Decisions:** Deciding whether to add multiple spirals in series (re-cleaning) versus parallel (throughput).
- **Equipment Selection:** Comparing spiral performance against other gravity separators like **destoners, indented cylinders, and shaking tables**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Newton's Separation Efficiency

The classical **Newton's Efficiency** for a two-component separation is derived from the principle of comparing the **actual separation achieved** to the **maximum possible (ideal) separation**.

**Step 1:** Define the recovery of the valuable component in the concentrate:

$$R_v = \frac{M_C \, c_C}{M_F \, c_F} \times 100$$

**Step 2:** Define the recovery of the gangue (undesired) component in the concentrate:

$$R_g = \frac{M_C \, (1 - c_C)}{M_F \, (1 - c_F)} \times 100$$

**Step 3:** In an **ideal separation**, the gangue recovery would be **zero** (i.e., $R_g = 0$) — meaning no impurity ends up in the concentrate. The actual recovery $R_v$ is therefore reduced by the undesired carry-over of gangue.

**Step 4:** The Newton efficiency is the **net effective recovery** after subtracting the gangue contamination:

$$\eta_N = R_v - R_g$$

**Step 5 (Expanded Form):** Substituting the definitions:

$$\eta_N = \left[ \frac{M_C \, c_C}{M_F \, c_F} - \frac{M_C \, (1 - c_C)}{M_F \, (1 - c_F)} \right] \times 100$$

**Step 6 (Mass Simplification):** Let the yield $Y = M_C / M_F$. Then:

$$\eta_N = \left[ \frac{Y \cdot c_C}{c_F} - \frac{Y \cdot (1 - c_C)}{(1 - c_F)} \right] \times 100$$

**Step 7 (Common Denominator):** Bringing to a common form:

$$\eta_N = Y \times \left[ \frac{c_C(1 - c_F) - (1 - c_C)c_F}{c_F(1 - c_F)} \right] \times 100$$

**Step 8 (Numerator Expansion):**

$$c_C(1 - c_F) - (1 - c_C)c_F = c_C - c_C c_F - c_F + c_C c_F = c_C - c_F$$

**Step 9 (Final Compact Form):**

$$\boxed{\eta_N = Y \times \left( \frac{c_C - c_F}{c_F (1 - c_F)} \right) \times 100}$$

This compact form is the most convenient expression for KTU numerical problems and only requires the **yield (Y)**, **concentrate grade (c_C)**, and **feed grade (c_F)** as input.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Exam Style)

**Problem Statement:**
A spiral separator experiment is conducted on a wheat-grain feed contaminated with stone particles. The following data are recorded after a single pass:

- Mass of feed slurry (dry basis): $M_F = 10.0$ kg
- Mass of concentrate (stones + few grains): $M_C = 1.2$ kg
- Mass of tailing (clean grains): $M_T = 8.8$ kg
- Grade of clean wheat in feed: $c_F = 0.95$ (i.e., 95 %)
- Grade of clean wheat in concentrate: $c_C = 0.30$ (mostly stones)
- Grade of clean wheat in tailing: $c_T = 0.998$

**Required:**
(a) Verify the mass balance.
(b) Calculate the recovery of valuable material ($R_v$).
(c) Calculate the recovery of gangue in concentrate ($R_g$).
(d) Calculate Newton's separation efficiency ($\eta_N$).

### Solution (Step-by-Step):

**Part (a): Mass Balance Verification**

Total mass check:

$$M_F \stackrel{?}{=} M_C + M_T$$

$$10.0 \stackrel{?}{=} 1.2 + 8.8 = 10.0 \;\; \checkmark \text{(satisfied)}$$

Component mass check:

$$M_F \cdot c_F = 10.0 \times 0.95 = 9.50 \text{ kg of wheat in feed}$$

$$M_C \cdot c_C + M_T \cdot c_T = (1.2 \times 0.30) + (8.8 \times 0.998) = 0.36 + 8.7824 = 9.1424 \text{ kg}$$

Discrepancy: $9.50 - 9.1424 = 0.358$ kg. (This is acceptable experimental error; in KTU exams, minor discrepancies are ignored or attributed to weighing losses.)

**Part (b): Recovery of Valuable Material (R_v)**

$$R_v = \frac{M_C \cdot c_C}{M_F \cdot c_F} \times 100$$

$$R_v = \frac{1.2 \times 0.30}{10.0 \times 0.95} \times 100$$

$$R_v = \frac{0.36}{9.50} \times 100 = 3.79 \; \%$$

**Interpretation:** Only 3.79 % of the wheat went to the concentrate (which is desirable since we want wheat in the tailing, not in stones).

**Part (c): Recovery of Gangue in Concentrate (R_g)**

$$R_g = \frac{M_C \cdot (1 - c_C)}{M_F \cdot (1 - c_F)} \times 100$$

$$R_g = \frac{1.2 \times (1 - 0.30)}{10.0 \times (1 - 0.95)} \times 100$$

$$R_g = \frac{1.2 \times 0.70}{10.0 \times 0.05} \times 100$$

$$R_g = \frac{0.84}{0.50} \times 100 = 168 \; \%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **A value > 100 % is physically impossible in a single-pass balance.** This indicates that the feed grade $c_F$ was over-estimated or the concentrate grade $c_C$ under-estimated (experimental error). In KTU problems, if $R_g > 100\%$, accept the calculated value and note the inconsistency, OR re-check the given data.

**Part (d): Newton's Separation Efficiency**

Using the compact form:

$$\eta_N = Y \times \left( \frac{c_C - c_F}{c_F (1 - c_F)} \right) \times 100$$

Yield:

$$Y = \frac{M_C}{M_F} \times 100 = \frac{1.2}{10.0} \times 100 = 12 \;\%$$

Substituting:

$$\eta_N = 12 \times \left( \frac{0.30 - 0.95}{0.95 \times (1 - 0.95)} \right) \times 100$$

$$\eta_N = 12 \times \left( \frac{-0.65}{0.95 \times 0.05} \right) \times 100$$

$$\eta_N = 12 \times \left( \frac{-0.65}{0.0475} \right) \times 100$$

$$\eta_N = 12 \times (-13.684) = -164.2 \;\%$$

**Interpretation:** A negative efficiency confirms that this spiral run was very poor — the concentrate (meant for stones) actually contained *less* clean wheat than the feed itself, which can happen when $c_C < c_F$. In a real experiment, the **grade-based efficiency** (Eq. 9 of the cheat sheet) is sometimes preferred for such cases:

$$\eta_G = \frac{c_C - c_F}{100 - c_F} \times 100 \;\% \quad \text{(using percentages in numerator)}$$

$$\eta_G = \frac{30 - 95}{100 - 95} \times 100 = \frac{-65}{5} \times 100 = -1300 \;\%$$

This is also negative. In KTU answer scripts, the conclusion should be:

> **"The negative efficiency indicates that the spiral separator, in this single pass, did not achieve concentration of the heavy fraction. The clean grains were carried outward to the tailing (as desired), but the recovery of the heavy impurities was incomplete — the tailing grade $c_T = 99.8\%$ confirms that the separation favoured the clean-grain product."**

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Efficiency Formula

| Scenario | Recommended Formula | Reason |
|----------|---------------------|--------|
| Two clean products (concentrate + tailing) | **Newton's Efficiency** | Classic, fast, and easy |
| High feed grade (>50 %) | **Hancock's Efficiency** | Corrects for feed grade bias |
| Multi-pass or recirculating systems | **Taggart's Efficiency** | Accounts for circulating load |
| Quick lab assessment, single pass | **Grade Efficiency (η_G)** | Simple, requires no mass balance |
| KTU board exam, full-marks | **Show both Newton + Mass Balance** | Demonstrates complete understanding |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Spiral Separator — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **functional architecture of a spiral separator experiment** as a flow-based block diagram, mapping all material streams, instrumentation, and mass-balance nodes used in the KTU lab procedure.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Feed: Grains + Stones + Water] --> B[Slurry Preparation Tank]
    B --> C[Pulp Density Check 15-25 percent solids]
    C --> D[Feed Pump]
    D --> E[Spiral Separator Column]
    E --> F1[Inner Stream Heavy Impurities]
    E --> F2[Outer Stream Light Grains]
    E --> F3[Middle Stream Middlings]
    F1 --> G1[Drying Oven 105 deg C]
    F2 --> G2[Drying Oven 105 deg C]
    F3 --> G3[Drying Oven 105 deg C]
    G1 --> H1[Weighing Concentrate M_C]
    G2 --> H2[Weighing Tailing M_T]
    G3 --> H3[Weighing Middlings M_M]
    H1 --> I1[Manual Assay Grade c_C]
    H2 --> I2[Manual Assay Grade c_T]
    H3 --> I3[Manual Assay Grade c_M]
    I1 --> J[Mass Balance Verification Node]
    I2 --> J
    I3 --> J
    J --> K1{Efficiency Calculator}
    K1 --> K2[Recovery R_v]
    K1 --> K3[Recovery R_g]
    K1 --> K4[Yield Y]
    K1 --> K5[Newton Efficiency eta_N]
    K1 --> K6[Grade Recovery Curve]
    J --> L[Report and Plot Generation]
    K2 --> L
    K3 --> L
    K4 --> L
    K5 --> L
    K6 --> L
    L --> M[Final KTU Lab Report Submission]
```

## 4.2 Cross-Sectional View of Spiral Trough — Functional Block Diagram

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the **physics of separation** as it occurs in the cross-section of the spiral trough, mapping force vectors and stream positions to their functional roles.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SP[Spiral Trough Cross Section]
        Outer[Outer Edge Low Density Grains] -->|Slow downward flow| Output1[Tailing Outlet]
        Middle[Mid Stream Mixed Phase] -->|Secondary helical flow| Output2[Middling Outlet]
        Inner[Inner Edge High Density Stones] -->|Fast settling| Output3[Concentrate Outlet]
    end
    FeedInput[Slurry Inlet from Top] --> Outer
    FeedInput --> Middle
    FeedInput --> Inner
    Force1[Gravity F_g] -.Acts on.-> SP
    Force2[Centripetal F_c] -.Acts on.-> SP
    Force3[Buoyancy F_b] -.Acts on.-> SP
    Force4[Hydraulic Lift F_h] -.Acts on.-> SP
```

## 4.3 Experimental Setup Layout (Textual Block Diagram)

```
+----------------------------------------------------------+
|              KTU LAB BENCH LAYOUT                        |
+----------------------------------------------------------+
|  [1] Slurry Tank   --> [2] Stirrer (manual/mechanical)   |
|         |                                                |
|         v                                                |
|  [3] Peristaltic Pump --> [4] Flow Control Valve         |
|         |                                                |
|         v                                                |
|  [5] FEED INLET (top of spiral column)                   |
|         |                                                |
|         v                                                |
|  [6] SPIRAL TROUGH (3-5 turns, perspex/FRP)              |
|         |                                                |
|         +----------------+----------------+               |
|         v                v                v               |
|  [7a] CONCENTRATE   [7b] MIDDLING     [7c] TAILING       |
|       (Inner)         (Middle)         (Outer)            |
|         |                |                |               |
|         v                v                v               |
|  [8a] Drying Tray   [8b] Drying Tray   [8c] Drying Tray   |
|         |                |                |               |
|         v                v                v               |
|  [9] Electronic Balance (0.01 g precision)                |
|         |                                                |
|         v                                                |
|  [10] Data Sheet --> [11] Efficiency Calculation         |
+----------------------------------------------------------+
```

> [!NOTE]
> **In KTU Viva Questions:** Students are often asked *"Why are the streams dried before weighing?"* — The correct answer is: *To remove the water content so that only the dry mass of solid particles is measured, since the feed grade, recovery, and efficiency are all calculated on a **dry-solids basis**. Wet mass would give erroneous results due to retained moisture.*

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: Define the term "separation efficiency" with respect to a spiral separator. Mention the formula used in food process engineering labs.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Separation efficiency of a spiral separator is a quantitative measure of the effectiveness of the device in concentrating the valuable (clean) food grains into one product stream while rejecting the undesired heavy impurities (stones, metallic fragments) into another stream. It is calculated using the **Newton's efficiency formula** which combines the recovery of valuable material and the recovery of gangue:

$$\eta_N = R_v - R_g = \left[ \frac{M_C \, c_C}{M_F \, c_F} - \frac{M_C \, (1 - c_C)}{M_F \, (1 - c_F)} \right] \times 100 \;\%$$

A higher efficiency indicates better separation performance. In food processing, a typical good separation efficiency for grain cleaning is **80–90 %**. (3 Marks)

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: State the principle of operation of a spiral separator. What is the role of secondary helical flow?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A spiral separator operates on the principle of **gravity concentration combined with centrifugal stratification**. When a dilute slurry flows down a helical trough, particles stratify based on density — heavier particles (stones, metallics) settle to the inner edge, while lighter particles (clean grains) are carried to the outer edge by the slower-moving upper layers of the slurry film.

The **secondary helical flow** refers to the swirling motion of slurry that develops perpendicular to the primary downward flow. This secondary flow creates a centrifugal-like effect that amplifies the density-based segregation. (3 Marks)

---

## 5.2 PART B — ESE Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

**Q: A spiral separator experiment was conducted on a pulse (green gram) sample contaminated with stone particles. The following data were obtained in a single-pass test:**

- Mass of feed (dry basis): $M_F = 5.0$ kg
- Mass of concentrate (stones): $M_C = 0.4$ kg, with grain grade $c_C = 0.10$
- Mass of tailing (clean pulses): $M_T = 4.6$ kg, with grain grade $c_T = 0.995$
- Feed grain grade: $c_F = 0.92$

**(a) [7 Marks] Verify the mass balance and calculate the recovery of valuable material ($R_v$) and the recovery of gangue in concentrate ($R_g$). Comment on the values obtained.**

**(b) [7 Marks] Calculate the yield (Y), Newton's separation efficiency ($\eta_N$), and the grade efficiency ($\eta_G$). Plot a brief description of how the efficiency changes with feed grade.**

### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Mass Balance and Recovery Calculations**

**Step 1: Overall Mass Balance**

$$M_F \stackrel{?}{=} M_C + M_T$$

$$5.0 \stackrel{?}{=} 0.4 + 4.6 = 5.0 \;\; \checkmark \text{ (Mass balance verified)}$$

**Step 2: Component Mass Balance**

Left-hand side (wheat in feed):

$$M_F \cdot c_F = 5.0 \times 0.92 = 4.60 \text{ kg}$$

Right-hand side (wheat in streams):

$$M_C \cdot c_C + M_T \cdot c_T = (0.4 \times 0.10) + (4.6 \times 0.995) = 0.04 + 4.577 = 4.617 \text{ kg}$$

Discrepancy: $4.617 - 4.60 = 0.017$ kg (0.37 % error — acceptable for lab data). ✓

**Step 3: Recovery of Valuable Material (R_v)**

$$R_v = \frac{M_C \cdot c_C}{M_F \cdot c_F} \times 100 = \frac{0.4 \times 0.10}{5.0 \times 0.92} \times 100 = \frac{0.04}{4.60} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{R_v = 0.870 \; \%}$$

**Step 4: Recovery of Gangue in Concentrate (R_g)**

$$R_g = \frac{M_C \cdot (1 - c_C)}{M_F \cdot (1 - c_F)} \times 100 = \frac{0.4 \times 0.90}{5.0 \times 0.08} \times 100 = \frac{0.36}{0.40} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{R_g = 90.0 \; \%}$$

**Comment:** $R_v = 0.87\%$ means only 0.87 % of clean pulses went to the concentrate (stones) — this is desirable since the concentrate should ideally contain only stones. $R_g = 90\%$ means 90 % of the stones ended up in the concentrate — this is also desirable since the goal is to remove stones. (7 Marks)

**Part (b) — Yield, Newton Efficiency, Grade Efficiency**

**Step 1: Yield (Y)**

$$Y = \frac{M_C}{M_F} \times 100 = \frac{0.4}{5.0} \times 100 = 8.0 \;\%$$

**Step 2: Newton's Separation Efficiency**

Using compact form:

$$\eta_N = Y \times \left( \frac{c_C - c_F}{c_F (1 - c_F)} \right) \times 100$$

$$\eta_N = 8.0 \times \left( \frac{0.10 - 0.92}{0.92 \times (1 - 0.92)} \right) \times 100$$

$$\eta_N = 8.0 \times \left( \frac{-0.82}{0.92 \times 0.08} \right) \times 100$$

$$\eta_N = 8.0 \times \left( \frac{-0.82}{0.0736} \right) \times 100$$

$$\eta_N = 8.0 \times (-11.141) = -89.13 \;\%$$

**Interpretation:** The negative value of $\eta_N$ is because $c_C < c_F$ — the concentrate is *poorer* in valuable material than the feed, which is precisely the goal of a *cleaning* operation (not a *concentration* operation). The grade-based efficiency is more appropriate here.

**Step 3: Grade Efficiency (η_G)**

$$\eta_G = \frac{c_F - c_C}{1 - c_C} \times 100 \;\% \text{ (formulation for cleaning)}$$

$$\eta_G = \frac{0.92 - 0.10}{1 - 0.10} \times 100 = \frac{0.82}{0.90} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\eta_G = 91.11 \;\%}$$

**Step 4: Discussion — Effect of Feed Grade on Efficiency**

The efficiency of a spiral separator is **highly sensitive to feed grade**. As the feed grade $c_F$ increases (i.e., feed is cleaner), the marginal improvement per pass diminishes, and the efficiency curve flattens. Conversely, at low $c_F$ (heavily contaminated feed), even a small change in concentrate grade produces a large change in efficiency. This is why **multi-pass cleaning** is preferred for low-grade feeds — the first pass removes the bulk of impurities, and subsequent passes fine-tune the grade. (7 Marks)

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

**Q: (a) [7 Marks] With a neat labelled diagram, explain the working of a spiral separator used in the food industry. What factors affect its separation efficiency?**

**(b) [7 Marks] During a spiral separator test on rice grains contaminated with mud balls, the following data were recorded. Calculate (i) Yield, (ii) Recovery of valuable material, (iii) Newton's efficiency, and (iv) Tailing grade. Given: $M_F = 8.0$ kg, $M_C = 0.8$ kg, $c_F = 0.97$, $c_C = 0.20$, $R_v = 2.06 \;\%$.**

### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Working and Diagram (Textual)**

**Working Principle:** A spiral separator is a flowing-film gravity concentration device consisting of a **helical trough** wound around a central vertical column. Feed slurry (food grains + water + impurities) is introduced at the top. As the slurry flows downward, denser impurities settle rapidly to the inner trough surface and slide down to a **concentrate outlet**. Lighter grains are carried by the slower-moving upper flow layers toward the **outer edge** and exit through the **tailing outlet**. A middle stream of **middlings** may also be collected.

**Labelled Diagram (Textual Schematic):**

```
            +-------------------+
            |   FEED INLET (F)  |
            +---------+---------+
                      |
                      v
        +-----------------------------+
        |   SPIRAL COLUMN (5 turns)   |
        |                             |
        |   (1) Helical trough wall   |
        |   (2) Inner column          |
        |   (3) Splitter at bottom    |
        |   (4) Slurry film           |
        +-------------+---------------+
                      |
        +-------------+-------------+
        |             |             |
        v             v             v
   +---------+  +-----------+  +----------+
   | INNER   |  | MIDDLE    |  | OUTER    |
   | Outlet  |  | Outlet    |  | Outlet   |
   | (Conc)  |  | (Midds)   |  | (Tail)   |
   +---------+  +-----------+  +----------+
        C             M             T
```

**Factors Affecting Separation Efficiency:**

1. **Density difference** between grains and impurities — higher contrast → better separation.
2. **Particle size** — too fine particles stay suspended; too coarse particles settle too fast and clog.
3. **Pulp density (solids-to-water ratio)** — too thick → hindered settling; too dilute → turbulence dominates.
4. **Feed flow rate** — higher flow reduces residence time and separation quality.
5. **Number of spiral turns** — more turns → better separation but lower throughput.
6. **Splitter position** — determines the cut-point between concentrate and tailing.
7. **Trough surface condition** — smooth surfaces promote laminar flow. (7 Marks)

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculations**

**Given Data:**
- $M_F = 8.0$ kg, $M_C = 0.8$ kg, $c_F = 0.97$, $c_C = 0.20$, $R_v = 2.06\%$

**Step 1: Calculate Mass of Tailing ($M_T$)**

$$M_T = M_F - M_C = 8.0 - 0.8 = 7.2 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Yield (Y)**

$$Y = \frac{M_C}{M_F} \times 100 = \frac{0.8}{8.0} \times 100 = 10.0 \;\%$$

**Step 3: Verify Recovery of Valuable Material ($R_v$)**

$$R_v = \frac{M_C \cdot c_C}{M_F \cdot c_F} \times 100 = \frac{0.8 \times 0.20}{8.0 \times 0.97} \times 100 = \frac{0.16}{7.76} \times 100 = 2.06 \;\% \;\; \checkmark$$

**Step 4: Calculate Tailing Grade ($c_T$) Using Component Mass Balance**

$$M_F c_F = M_C c_C + M_T c_T$$

$$8.0 \times 0.97 = (0.8 \times 0.20) + (7.2 \times c_T)$$

$$7.76 = 0.16 + 7.2 \, c_T$$

$$7.2 \, c_T = 7.60$$

$$c_T = \frac{7.60}{7.2} = 1.0556$$

> [!WARNING]
> **The tailing grade $c_T > 1$ is physically impossible.** This indicates an inconsistency in the given data (likely the recovery $R_v$ was rounded, or the feed grade was slightly over-estimated). In KTU answer scripts, you should **report the calculated value and explicitly note the experimental inconsistency.** (A common practice: use $c_T = 99.8\% = 0.998$ and re-compute the component balance.)

**Step 5: Calculate Newton's Separation Efficiency**

Using compact form:

$$\eta_N = Y \times \left( \frac{c_C - c_F}{c_F (1 - c_F)} \right) \times 100$$

$$\eta_N = 10.0 \times \left( \frac{0.20 - 0.97}{0.97 \times 0.03} \right) \times 100$$

$$\eta_N = 10.0 \times \left( \frac{-0.77}{0.0291} \right) \times 100$$

$$\eta_N = 10.0 \times (-26.46) = -264.6 \;\%$$

**Final Summary Table:**

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Yield (Y) | 10.0 % |
| Recovery of Valuable ($R_v$) | 2.06 % |
| Tailing Grade ($c_T$) | ~1.06 (data inconsistency) |
| Newton's Efficiency ($\eta_N$) | -264.6 % |

**Conclusion:** The negative efficiency reflects the cleaning (not concentrating) nature of the operation. The low $R_v = 2.06\%$ confirms that almost all clean rice (97.9 %) was correctly directed to the tailing stream, achieving a successful cleaning separation despite the negative Newton efficiency value. (7 Marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Units Confusion:** Always express grades as **fractions (0 to 1)** in the formula and **percentages** in the final answer. Mixing units is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
> 2. **Sign of Efficiency:** A *negative* Newton efficiency is **not a wrong answer** — it simply means the operation is a cleaning one, not a concentrating one. Students who try to "fix" the sign by adding absolute values lose 2 marks.
> 3. **Mass Balance Check:** Always perform the mass balance check **first** before computing efficiency. If mass balance fails by > 5 %, the data is invalid and you must state this explicitly.
> 4. **Wet vs Dry Basis:** All masses must be on a **dry basis**. Forgetting to dry the streams before weighing is a 1-mark deduction.
> 5. **Confusing R_v and R_g:** $R_v$ is for **valuable** material (the one you want to recover), $R_g$ is for **gangue** (the impurity). Swapping these is a 2-mark deduction.
> 6. **Forgetting to Convert % to Fraction:** When using $c_F$ in the formula $\eta_N = Y \times \left( \frac{c_C - c_F}{c_F(1-c_F)}\right) \times 100$, the $c$ values must be in **fraction form**, not percentage.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] A **spiral separator** uses gravity + secondary helical flow to separate particles by density.
- [x] In **food processing**, it is used for removing stones, metallics, and damaged grains from rice, wheat, pulses, millets, and spices.
- [x] The two products are **Concentrate (C)** — heavy impurities; **Tailing (T)** — clean grains; optional **Middlings (M)**.
- [x] **Feed grade ($c_F$)**, **Concentrate grade ($c_C$)**, **Tailing grade ($c_T$)** must be on a **dry mass basis**.
- [x] **Mass balance** is the foundation: $M_F = M_C + M_T$ and $M_F c_F = M_C c_C + M_T c_T$.
- [x] **Yield** $Y = (M_C / M_F) \times 100 \;\%$.
- [x] **Recovery of valuable** $R_v = (M_C c_C / M_F c_F) \times 100 \;\%$.
- [x] **Recovery of gangue** $R_g = [M_C(1-c_C) / M_F(1-c_F)] \times 100 \;\%$.
- [x] **Newton's efficiency** $\eta_N = R_v - R_g$, or compactly: $\eta_N = Y \cdot [(c_C - c_F)/(c_F(1-c_F))] \times 100$.
- [x] **Grade efficiency** $\eta_G = [(c_F - c_C)/(1 - c_C)] \times 100$ is preferred for *cleaning* operations.
- [x] A **negative efficiency** is *physically meaningful* and indicates a cleaning operation, NOT a calculation error.
- [x] **Pulp density** of 15–25 % solids is standard; lower = poor separation, higher = hindered settling.
- [x] **Particle size** range: 1–8 mm for food grains; 75 µm–3 mm for minerals.
- [x] **Multi-pass cleaning** improves overall recovery at the cost of throughput.
- [x] KTU **viva-favourite questions**: "Why is drying done before weighing?" / "What is the role of splitter position?" / "How does feed grade affect efficiency?"
- [x] **Pre-lab preparation**: Calibrate the balance, prepare slurry, set flow rate, check spiral for clean trough.
- [x] **Post-lab**: Plot **grade-recovery curve** to visualise the trade-off between purity and yield.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance of a spray dryer in food product development
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTL408 - FOOD PROCESS ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Performance of a spray dryer in food product development

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Spray Drying** is a continuous, single-stage dehydration process in which a liquid feed (solution, emulsion, suspension, or paste) is atomized into a hot drying gas stream, resulting in the near-instantaneous formation of dry, free-flowing powder particles. The performance of a spray dryer in food product development refers to the systematic evaluation of its **thermal efficiency**, **evaporative capacity**, **product recovery yield**, **residual moisture content**, **particle morphology**, and **energy consumption** under controlled operating parameters.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight (PCFTL408 - Module 2):**
> Performance evaluation quantifies the effectiveness of converting a liquid food feed into a stable, dry particulate product by measuring the spray dryer's **drying efficiency, throughput, and product quality attributes** such as moisture content, bulk density, and solubility.

The principal performance metrics tracked in a KTU lab record include:

- **Inlet Air Temperature (T_in):** typically 150 °C – 220 °C for food products
- **Outlet Air Temperature (T_out):** typically 80 °C – 110 °C
- **Feed Rate (F):** in kg/hr or mL/min
- **Feed Solid Concentration (C_s):** in % w/w
- **Product Moisture Content (M_p):** in % wet basis
- **Thermal Efficiency (η_th):** dimensionless ratio
- **Mass Yield / Recovery (Y):** % of dry powder recovered
- **Evaporation Rate (E):** kg of water removed per hour

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy: "The Instant Tea Factory"**
> Imagine boiling milk in a kitchen. To make milk powder, you would normally need hours of slow boiling and stirring. A **spray dryer does the same job in a single second**. Liquid milk is squirted through a tiny nozzle (like a perfume atomizer) into a chamber filled with hot air. Each tiny droplet becomes a dry particle the moment it meets the hot air — like a snowflake forming in a cloud. The **performance** of this "snow-making machine" is judged by:
> 1. How much water did it actually remove? (**drying efficiency**)
> 2. How much finished powder did we collect back? (**recovery yield**)
> 3. Did the powder keep its flavor, color, and nutrients? (**product quality**)
> 4. How much energy did we waste? (**thermal efficiency**)

The **inlet temperature** is like the "heat intensity" of the drying cloud, and the **outlet temperature** is what remains after the droplets have absorbed the heat to evaporate their water. The closer the outlet temperature is to the inlet, the less heat was used — but a balance must be struck, because low outlet temperature means incomplete drying.

## 1.3 Standard Performance Constants and Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Values for Spray Dryer Performance (Food Grade):**
> - **Latent heat of vaporization of water (λ):** **2257 kJ/kg** (at 100 °C)
> - **Specific heat of air (c_p):** **1.005 kJ/(kg·K)**
> - **Specific heat of dry food solids (c_ps):** **1.0 – 2.0 kJ/(kg·K)** (typical 1.5)
> - **Acceptable product moisture:** **2 – 4 % w.b.** for most food powders
> - **Typical thermal efficiency:** **40 – 70 %** in well-designed co-current dryers

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The performance of a spray dryer is governed by the simultaneous **heat transfer** (from hot air to droplet) and **mass transfer** (moisture migration from droplet interior to surface, then evaporation). The process occurs in **three functional zones**:

### Zone 1: Atomization (Feed Preparation Zone)
- The liquid feed is pumped through a **centrifugal atomizer** (rotary disk) or **pressure nozzle**.
- High rotational speed / pressure breaks the liquid into a **spray of fine droplets** (typical diameter **20 – 200 μm**).
- A smaller droplet diameter **↑ specific surface area ↑ evaporation rate**.

### Zone 2: Hot Air Contact (Drying Zone)
- A high-velocity stream of **filtered, pre-heated air** enters the chamber.
- The droplets encounter hot air at the **inlet temperature (T_in)**.
- Two simultaneous phenomena occur:
  - **Heat transfer:** from air to droplet (driving force = T_air − T_droplet)
  - **Mass transfer:** water vapor leaves the droplet surface into the air (driving force = P_w,sat − P_w,air)

### Zone 3: Powder Separation (Collection Zone)
- Dry powder is conveyed to a **cyclone separator** (primary) and **bag filter** (secondary).
- Exhaust air is discharged after fine-particle recovery.
- Powder is collected in sealed containers under low humidity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Performance Evaluation Matters in Food Product Development:**
> A new food formulation (e.g., a probiotic-fortified fruit juice powder) must retain heat-sensitive bioactives. Spray dryer performance determines whether the product survives processing. For instance, if T_out > 105 °C, **probiotic viability drops drastically**, and the product fails quality tests — even if mass yield is high.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Formulas for KTU 2024 PCFTL408 — Exam-Viva Ready Table**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Moisture Content (wet basis) | $M_{wb} = \dfrac{W_w}{W_w + W_d} \times 100$ | % w.b. | W_w = water mass, W_d = dry solid mass |
| 2 | Moisture Content (dry basis) | $M_{db} = \dfrac{W_w}{W_d} \times 100$ | % d.b. | Used in drying rate equations |
| 3 | wb ↔ db conversion | $M_{wb} = \dfrac{M_{db}}{1 + M_{db}/100}$ | % | Both should be consistent in calculation |
| 4 | Evaporation Rate | $E = F \left( \dfrac{1 - X_{in}}{1 - X_{out}} - 1 \right)$ | kg water/hr | F = feed rate, X = solid fraction |
| 5 | Simplified Evaporation | $E = F (M_{in} - M_{out})$ (per kg feed) | kg water/kg feed | Common lab formula |
| 6 | Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{T_{in} - T_{out}}{T_{in} - T_{amb}} \times 100$ | % | T_amb = ambient air temperature |
| 7 | Specific Energy Consumption | $SEC = \dfrac{Q_{air} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{in} - T_{amb})}{M_{water,\,evap}}$ | kJ/kg water | Energy per kg of water removed |
| 8 | Product Recovery / Yield | $Y = \dfrac{M_{powder,\,collected}}{M_{solids,\,in\,feed}} \times 100$ | % | Includes cyclone + bag filter |
| 9 | Air Mass Flow Rate (from energy balance) | $m_{air} = \dfrac{E \cdot \lambda}{c_p (T_{in} - T_{out})}$ | kg dry air/hr | λ = latent heat of vaporization |
| 10 | Drying Capacity | $C = E \times 3600$ | kg water/hr | Continuous duty rating |
| 11 | Volumetric Evaporation Rate | $E_v = \dfrac{E}{V_{chamber}}$ | kg/(m³·hr) | Used to size chambers |
| 12 | Mass Balance (overall) | $F_{feed} = F_{dry\,air\,in} = F_{dry\,product} + F_{exhaust\,air}$ | kg/hr | Steady-state assumption |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Exam Pitfall:** Do **NOT** mix wet-basis and dry-basis moisture. The choice depends on whether you are tracking **product shelf-life (wb)** or **drying kinetics (db)**. Examiners deduct **2 marks** for unit-inconsistency mistakes.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Food Engineering

- **Dairy:** Skim milk powder, whey protein concentrate, infant formula
- **Beverages:** Instant coffee, tea, fruit juice powders
- **Functional Foods:** Probiotic encapsulation, omega-3 microencapsulation
- **Bakery:** Egg powder, maltodextrin carriers
- **Flavors & Nutraceuticals:** Encapsulated essential oils, vitamin premixes

Performance data guides **scale-up** from lab-scale (e.g., 5 kg/hr) to industrial spray dryers (e.g., 5,000 kg/hr), where thermodynamic similarity is preserved through dimensionless numbers (Reynolds, Nusselt, Sherwood).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Thermal Efficiency

The **thermal efficiency (η_th)** of a spray dryer is the fraction of the total heat input that is effectively used for moisture evaporation. It is derived from a steady-state energy balance on the air stream.

### Step 1 — Heat Supplied to the Air (Q_in)

The heater raises ambient air (T_amb) to the inlet temperature (T_in). For an air mass flow rate *m_air*:

$$Q_{in} = m_{air} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{in} - T_{amb})$$

### Step 2 — Heat Used for Evaporation (Q_useful)

Only the temperature drop from T_in to T_out represents heat transferred to droplets. So:

$$Q_{useful} = m_{air} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{in} - T_{out})$$

### Step 3 — Define Thermal Efficiency

The ratio of useful heat to total heat input is:

$$\eta_{th} = \dfrac{Q_{useful}}{Q_{in}} = \dfrac{m_{air} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{in} - T_{out})}{m_{air} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{in} - T_{amb})}$$

The m_air · c_p terms cancel:

$$\boxed{\eta_{th} = \dfrac{T_{in} - T_{out}}{T_{in} - T_{amb}} \times 100 \;\; \%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions:** If T_out = T_amb, η_th = 0 % (no drying). If T_out = T_in, η_th = 100 % (impossible — air would exit saturated but no evaporation, contradiction). Realistic values lie between **40 – 70 %**.

## 3.2 Derivation of Evaporation Rate from Mass Balance

Consider a feed entering at rate *F* (kg/hr) with moisture content M_in (wet basis, decimal). Dry solid mass rate is constant:

$$\text{Dry solids in feed} = F (1 - M_{in})$$

Dry solids leave in the powder at moisture M_out. Let the powder output rate be *P*:

$$P (1 - M_{out}) = F (1 - M_{in})$$

Solving for *P*:

$$P = F \cdot \dfrac{1 - M_{in}}{1 - M_{out}}$$

The water evaporated is the feed water minus the residual water in the powder:

$$E = F \cdot M_{in} - P \cdot M_{out}$$

Substituting *P*:

$$E = F \cdot M_{in} - F \cdot \dfrac{1 - M_{in}}{1 - M_{out}} \cdot M_{out}$$

Factor *F* and simplify the bracket:

$$E = F \left[ M_{in} - \dfrac{M_{out} (1 - M_{in})}{1 - M_{out}} \right]$$

$$E = F \left[ \dfrac{M_{in} (1 - M_{out}) - M_{out} (1 - M_{in})}{1 - M_{out}} \right]$$

$$E = F \left[ \dfrac{M_{in} - M_{in} M_{out} - M_{out} + M_{in} M_{out}}{1 - M_{out}} \right]$$

$$E = F \left[ \dfrac{M_{in} - M_{out}}{1 - M_{out}} \right]$$

$$\boxed{E = F \cdot \dfrac{M_{in} - M_{out}}{1 - M_{out}} \;\; \text{kg water/hr}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Alternate compact form using solid fraction (X = 1 − M):**
> $$E = F \left( \dfrac{1 - X_{out}}{X_{in}} - 1 \right) \cdot X_{in} = F \left( \dfrac{X_{in} - X_{out}}{X_{out}} \right)$$
> where X_in, X_out are the mass fractions of dry solids in the feed and powder, respectively.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Lab Calculation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Sample Lab Data (typical maltodextrin solution spray drying run):**
> - Feed rate, F = 4.0 kg/hr
> - Feed moisture, M_in = 80 % w.b. (i.e., 20 % solids)
> - Product moisture, M_out = 3 % w.b. (i.e., 97 % solids)
> - Inlet air temperature, T_in = 180 °C
> - Outlet air temperature, T_out = 95 °C
> - Ambient temperature, T_amb = 30 °C
> - Latent heat of vaporization, λ = 2257 kJ/kg
> - Specific heat of air, c_p = 1.005 kJ/(kg·K)

### (a) Evaporation Rate

$$E = 4.0 \times \dfrac{0.80 - 0.03}{1 - 0.03} = 4.0 \times \dfrac{0.77}{0.97}$$

$$E = 4.0 \times 0.7938 = 3.175 \;\; \text{kg water/hr}$$

### (b) Powder Output Rate

$$P = 4.0 \times \dfrac{1 - 0.80}{1 - 0.03} = 4.0 \times \dfrac{0.20}{0.97} = 0.825 \;\; \text{kg powder/hr}$$

### (c) Thermal Efficiency

$$\eta_{th} = \dfrac{180 - 95}{180 - 30} \times 100 = \dfrac{85}{150} \times 100 = 56.67 \;\; \%$$

### (d) Air Mass Flow Rate (from energy balance)

$$m_{air} = \dfrac{E \cdot \lambda}{c_p (T_{in} - T_{out})} = \dfrac{3.175 \times 2257}{1.005 \times (180 - 95)}$$

$$m_{air} = \dfrac{7165.97}{85.425} = 83.88 \;\; \text{kg dry air/hr}$$

### (e) Specific Energy Consumption

$$SEC = \dfrac{m_{air} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{in} - T_{amb})}{E} = \dfrac{83.88 \times 1.005 \times 150}{3.175}$$

$$SEC = \dfrac{12644.46}{3.175} = 3982.7 \;\; \text{kJ/kg water evaporated}$$

### (f) Product Recovery (assuming 0.78 kg collected)

$$Y = \dfrac{0.78}{0.825} \times 100 = 94.5 \;\; \%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Performance Evaluation Summary Table for this run:**

| Performance Parameter | Computed Value | KTU Acceptable Range | Verdict |
|----------------------|----------------|----------------------|---------|
| Evaporation Rate | 3.175 kg/hr | 2 – 10 kg/hr (lab scale) | Satisfactory |
| Powder Output | 0.825 kg/hr | — | Satisfactory |
| Thermal Efficiency | 56.67 % | 40 – 70 % | **Good** |
| SEC | 3982.7 kJ/kg | 3500 – 5500 kJ/kg | **Good** |
| Product Recovery | 94.5 % | ≥ 90 % | **Excellent** |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Spray Dryer System for Food Product Development

The following Mermaid block diagram represents the **functional architecture of a lab-scale spray dryer** as it is interfaced in PCFTL408 lab sessions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[FEED TANK] --> A2[PERISTALTIC PUMP]
    A2 --> A3[ATOMIZER UNIT]
    A3 --> A4[DRYING CHAMBER]
    H1[HEATER] --> A4
    B1[AMBIENT AIR INLET] --> H1
    A4 --> A5[CYCLONE SEPARATOR]
    A5 --> A6[BAG FILTER]
    A5 --> A7[POWDER COLLECTION VESSEL]
    A6 --> A8[EXHAUST BLOWER]
    A8 --> A9[STACK DISCHARGE]
    T1[INLET TEMP SENSOR] -.-> A4
    T2[OUTLET TEMP SENSOR] -.-> A4
    T3[FEED FLOW SENSOR] -.-> A2
    D1[DATA LOGGER] --- T1
    D1 --- T2
    D1 --- T3
```

**Block-Level Description (for KTU record submission):**

| Block ID | Function | Key Instrument |
|----------|----------|---------------|
| FEED TANK | Stores liquid food feed (e.g., milk, juice) | Stainless steel vessel |
| PERISTALTIC PUMP | Controls feed rate to atomizer | Variable speed pump |
| ATOMIZER UNIT | Disperses feed into fine droplets | Centrifugal disk / nozzle |
| HEATER | Preheats inlet air to T_in | Electric heater / gas burner |
| DRYING CHAMBER | Zone of droplet-air contact | Insulated cylindrical tower |
| CYCLONE SEPARATOR | Primary powder recovery by centrifugal force | Stainless cyclone |
| BAG FILTER | Secondary fine-particle recovery | Fabric filter bags |
| POWDER COLLECTION VESSEL | Sealed container for finished product | Glass jar / drum |
| DATA LOGGER | Records T_in, T_out, feed flow | Digital acquisition system |

## 4.2 Performance Evaluation Flowchart (Sequential Logic for Lab Report)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[START LAB RUN] --> S2[SET INLET TEMP T_in]
    S2 --> S3[CALIBRATE FEED RATE F]
    S3 --> S4[BEGIN FEED AND AIR FLOW]
    S4 --> S5[REACH STEADY STATE]
    S5 --> S6[RECORD T_in, T_out, F, M_in, M_out]
    S6 --> P1[CALCULATE EVAPORATION RATE E]
    S6 --> P2[CALCULATE THERMAL EFFICIENCY]
    S6 --> P3[CALCULATE SEC]
    S6 --> P4[CALCULATE PRODUCT RECOVERY Y]
    S6 --> P5[CALCULATE AIR MASS FLOW]
    P1 --> Q1[VALIDATE PERFORMANCE TABLE]
    P2 --> Q1
    P3 --> Q1
    P4 --> Q1
    P5 --> Q1
    Q1 --> Q2{ALL PARAMETERS WITHIN RANGE?}
    Q2 -->|YES| R1[REPORT SATISFACTORY RUN]
    Q2 -->|NO| R2[REPEAT WITH ADJUSTED T_in OR F]
    R2 --> S2
```

## 4.3 Heat and Mass Transfer Interaction (Co-Current Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AIR1[HOT AIR IN AT 180C] --> CHAM[DRYING CHAMBER]
    FEED1[LIQUID FEED AT 25C] --> ATOM[ATOMIZER DROPLETS 20-200 UM] --> CHAM
    CHAM --> EVAP[WATER EVAPORATES]
    CHAM --> POWD[DRY POWDER FORMS]
    CHAM --> AIR2[COOLER MOIST AIR OUT AT 95C]
    POWD --> CYC[CYCLONE COLLECTS POWDER]
    AIR2 --> CYC
    CYC --> EXH[EXHAUST TO FILTER]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Co-Current vs Counter-Current for Food:**
> KTU lab spray dryers are typically **co-current** — the hottest air meets the wettest droplets. This protects heat-sensitive food components (vitamins, probiotics, color pigments) because the droplet surface temperature never exceeds the **wet-bulb temperature** (~50 – 60 °C) until the final drying stage. This is the **primary reason spray drying is preferred over drum drying for thermally sensitive foods**.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer / Viva)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define the term "thermal efficiency" of a spray dryer. List any two factors that affect it.**

**Model Answer:**
Thermal efficiency of a spray dryer is the ratio of heat effectively used for moisture evaporation to the total heat supplied to the drying air.

$$\eta_{th} = \dfrac{T_{in} - T_{out}}{T_{in} - T_{amb}} \times 100 \; \%$$

**Two factors affecting it:**
1. **Inlet air temperature (T_in)** — higher T_in raises the numerator, but excessive heat may degrade product.
2. **Feed solid concentration** — higher solids reduce the water load, increasing efficiency.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is the significance of "product recovery" in spray dryer performance evaluation?**

**Model Answer:**
Product recovery (or yield) is the mass percentage of dry powder collected (cyclone + bag filter) relative to the total dry solids fed. It indicates:
- **Powder loss** to exhaust air (typically 5 – 15 %)
- **Cyclone efficiency** in separating fine particles
- **Economic viability** of the process for commercial food powder production

A recovery > 90 % is considered industrially acceptable.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2]*

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **co-current spray dryer** used in food product development. List the major components and state the function of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** During a spray drying experiment on a tomato juice concentrate, the following data were recorded:
- Feed rate, F = 5.0 kg/hr
- Feed moisture, M_in = 75 % (w.b.)
- Product moisture, M_out = 4 % (w.b.)
- Inlet air temperature, T_in = 190 °C
- Outlet air temperature, T_out = 98 °C
- Ambient temperature, T_amb = 32 °C
- Latent heat of vaporization, λ = 2257 kJ/kg
- c_p (air) = 1.005 kJ/(kg·K)

Calculate the **(i) Evaporation rate**, **(ii) Thermal efficiency**, **(iii) Powder output rate**, and **(iv) Specific energy consumption (SEC)**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**Part (a) — Block Diagram (3 marks) + Explanation (3 marks) + Components list (1 mark):**

A co-current spray dryer operates with **hot air and feed droplets moving in the same direction** (top-to-bottom or vice versa). The major components and their functions are:

| Component | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| Feed tank | Stores homogenized liquid food feed |
| Feed pump (peristaltic) | Delivers controlled feed rate to atomizer |
| Atomizer (centrifugal/nozzle) | Disperses feed into fine droplets of 20 – 200 μm |
| Air heater | Preheats ambient air to 150 – 220 °C |
| Drying chamber | Provides residence time for droplet-air interaction |
| Cyclone separator | Recovers coarse dry powder by centrifugal action |
| Bag filter | Recovers fine particles escaping cyclone |
| Exhaust blower | Discharges moist exhaust air |
| Temperature sensors | Monitor T_in, T_out for energy balance |

**Working principle:** The liquid feed is atomized into the drying chamber where it meets hot air flowing in the same direction. Due to co-current flow, the **wettest droplets encounter the hottest air**, but the evaporative cooling keeps the droplet surface near the wet-bulb temperature, protecting heat-sensitive nutrients.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Evaporation Rate:**

$$E = F \cdot \dfrac{M_{in} - M_{out}}{1 - M_{out}} = 5.0 \times \dfrac{0.75 - 0.04}{1 - 0.04}$$

$$E = 5.0 \times \dfrac{0.71}{0.96} = 5.0 \times 0.7396 = 3.698 \;\; \text{kg water/hr}$$

**(ii) Thermal Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{th} = \dfrac{T_{in} - T_{out}}{T_{in} - T_{amb}} \times 100 = \dfrac{190 - 98}{190 - 32} \times 100$$

$$\eta_{th} = \dfrac{92}{158} \times 100 = 58.23 \;\; \%$$

**(iii) Powder Output Rate:**

$$P = F \cdot \dfrac{1 - M_{in}}{1 - M_{out}} = 5.0 \times \dfrac{1 - 0.75}{1 - 0.04} = 5.0 \times \dfrac{0.25}{0.96}$$

$$P = 5.0 \times 0.2604 = 1.302 \;\; \text{kg powder/hr}$$

**(iv) Air Mass Flow Rate:**

$$m_{air} = \dfrac{E \cdot \lambda}{c_p (T_{in} - T_{out})} = \dfrac{3.698 \times 2257}{1.005 \times (190 - 98)}$$

$$m_{air} = \dfrac{8346.99}{92.46} = 90.28 \;\; \text{kg dry air/hr}$$

**Specific Energy Consumption:**

$$SEC = \dfrac{m_{air} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{in} - T_{amb})}{E} = \dfrac{90.28 \times 1.005 \times (190 - 32)}{3.698}$$

$$SEC = \dfrac{14322.92}{3.698} = 3872.6 \;\; \text{kJ/kg water}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part (b):**
> Students commonly confuse the **feed moisture formula** by inverting the denominator as (1 − M_in) instead of (1 − M_out). The correct formula always uses the **outlet (product) moisture** in the denominator. A typical 2-mark deduction applies for this error. Also, **T_in − T_amb** (not T_in − T_out) goes in the SEC denominator, which is a frequently tested distinction.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]*

**(a)** Explain the role of **atomization** in spray dryer performance. Compare **pressure nozzle** and **centrifugal disk atomizers** with respect to droplet size, capacity, and food suitability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A milk powder manufacturing plant operates a spray dryer with the following steady-state data:
- Feed rate = 2000 kg/hr of whole milk
- Feed total solids = 12.5 %
- Product moisture content = 3.5 % (w.b.)
- Inlet air temperature = 185 °C
- Outlet air temperature = 92 °C
- Air mass flow rate = 32,000 kg/hr
- Ambient temperature = 28 °C
- Latent heat of vaporization = 2260 kJ/kg
- c_p of air = 1.005 kJ/(kg·K)

Calculate the **(i) Evaporation rate**, **(ii) Thermal efficiency**, **(iii) Mass of dry powder produced per hour**, and **(iv) Product yield if the actual collected powder is 240 kg/hr**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**Part (a) — Role of Atomization + Comparison (7 marks):**

**Role of atomization in spray dryer performance:**
Atomization is the **first and most critical step** that determines the entire downstream performance. It directly controls:
- **Droplet surface area:** Smaller droplets → larger surface-to-volume ratio → faster evaporation
- **Drying kinetics:** Atomization quality dictates residence time and final particle size
- **Product uniformity:** Improper atomization leads to wide size distribution, sticky powders, and wall deposits
- **Energy efficiency:** Over-sized droplets may not dry fully within chamber residence time

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Pressure Nozzle | Centrifugal Disk |
|---------|-----------------|------------------|
| Operating principle | Liquid forced through small orifice under high pressure | Liquid flung off high-speed rotating disk |
| Typical droplet size | 100 – 300 μm | 20 – 200 μm |
| Capacity | Low to medium (up to ~1000 kg/hr per nozzle) | High (up to 20,000 kg/hr) |
| Droplet size uniformity | Narrow (good) | Wide (broader distribution) |
| Feed viscosity tolerance | Limited (best for low-viscosity feeds) | High (handles pastes, emulsions) |
| Food suitability | Skim milk, coffee extract | Tomato paste, cheese whey, viscous foods |
| Wear and tear | High (nozzle erosion) | Low (disk is robust) |

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Evaporation Rate:**

Using solid fractions:
- X_in (feed solids) = 1 − M_in = 1 − 0.875 = 0.125 (since milk is 12.5 % solids, moisture = 87.5 %)
- X_out (powder solids) = 1 − M_out = 1 − 0.035 = 0.965

$$E = F \cdot \dfrac{M_{in} - M_{out}}{1 - M_{out}} = 2000 \times \dfrac{0.875 - 0.035}{0.965}$$

$$E = 2000 \times \dfrac{0.840}{0.965} = 2000 \times 0.8705 = 1740.9 \;\; \text{kg water/hr}$$

**(ii) Thermal Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{th} = \dfrac{185 - 92}{185 - 28} \times 100 = \dfrac{93}{157} \times 100 = 59.24 \;\; \%$$

**(iii) Mass of Dry Powder Produced (theoretical):**

$$P_{theoretical} = F \cdot \dfrac{1 - M_{in}}{1 - M_{out}} = 2000 \times \dfrac{0.125}{0.965}$$

$$P_{theoretical} = 2000 \times 0.1295 = 259.07 \;\; \text{kg powder/hr}$$

**(iv) Product Yield:**

$$Y = \dfrac{P_{actual}}{P_{theoretical}} \times 100 = \dfrac{240}{259.07} \times 100 = 92.64 \;\; \%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part (b):**
> - Students often forget that **feed moisture = 100% − feed solids %**. Here 12.5 % solids implies M_in = **87.5 % w.b.**, not 12.5 %. A **3-mark deduction** is typical for this critical oversight.
> - For yield calculation, the **theoretical powder** (not the evaporation rate) is the denominator. Do not confuse with thermal efficiency formulas.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Notes for KTU PCFTL408 — Module 2**

- ✅ **Spray drying = one-step** conversion of liquid food → dry powder using atomization + hot air.
- ✅ The three zones of a spray dryer are **atomization, drying, and separation**.
- ✅ **Co-current flow** is preferred for heat-sensitive foods (droplet temperature ≈ wet-bulb).
- ✅ Key inlet parameters: **T_in (150 – 220 °C)**, feed rate, atomizer speed, feed concentration.
- ✅ Key outlet parameters: **T_out (80 – 110 °C)**, product moisture, particle size, recovery.
- ✅ **Thermal efficiency formula:** $\eta_{th} = (T_{in} - T_{out})/(T_{in} - T_{amb}) \times 100$
- ✅ **Evaporation rate:** $E = F (M_{in} - M_{out})/(1 - M_{out})$
- ✅ **Product yield:** (Powder collected ÷ Dry solids in feed) × 100
- ✅ **SEC** is reported in **kJ per kg of water evaporated** — not per kg of feed.
- ✅ **Latent heat of vaporization** of water at 100 °C = **2257 kJ/kg** (memorize).
- ✅ **Specific heat of air** = **1.005 kJ/(kg·K)** (memorize).
- ✅ **Acceptable product moisture** for food powders = **2 – 4 % w.b.**
- ✅ Industrial **product recovery** benchmark = **≥ 90 %**.
- ✅ **Atomization type** determines feed viscosity tolerance: **centrifugal > pressure** for viscous foods.
- ✅ **Cyclone + bag filter** together achieve >95 % particulate recovery; bag filter handles fines (< 10 μm).
- ✅ Always check **units consistency** — never mix % w.b. and % d.b. in the same equation.
